export const meta = {
  name: 'pass5-verify',
  description: 'Pass-5: 2-agent blind verification of 24 findings',
  phases: [{ title: 'Verify' }, { title: 'Triage' }],
}
const ROOT = '/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay'
const FINDINGS = [{"id": "H-001", "severity": "high", "file": "src/assay_engine/methodology/confirm.py", "line": "101-143, 292-397", "title": "alpha and stability_threshold are post-hoc free parameters, not pre-registered \u2014 threshold HARKing survives the firewall", "description": "The pre-registration digest (canonical_hypothesis_digest, preregistration.py:114-118) binds statement/kind/origin/test_name/decision_rule/source_claim_id/predicted_direction/params. Verified: `alpha` and `stability_threshold` are NOT fields on Hypothesis (hypothesis.py:61-74) and are NOT in the digest payload. They are supplied as plain runtime arguments to verdict_from_pvalue/confirm_whole_corpus/confirm_unit_level at confirmation time, and verdict_from_pvalue even CONSTRUCTS the recorded decision_rule string from the runtime alpha (confirm.py:131 `rule = f\"...alpha={alpha}...\"`) instead of comparing it against the locked decision_rule. Nothing reads hypothesis.decision_rule/params to constrain the confirm-time threshold. An analyst who has seen the null/baseline can therefore choose alpha=0.05 vs 0.10 or stability_threshold=0.9 vs 0.6 to flip indeterminate\u2194supported \u2014 exactly the post-hoc-threshold HARKing the firewall claims to prevent. The most decision-bearing numbers are not committed before confirmation, defeating the core methodological guarantee.", "poc": "Lock a WHOLE_CORPUS hypothesis with predicted_direction='greater' (alpha not a field, so the threshold is unlocked). After seeing the null: confirm_whole_corpus(h, observed=X, null_distribution=null, alpha=0.05, resample_statistics=R, stability_threshold=0.9, authority=auth) -> indeterminate ('does not beat null at alpha'). Re-run the SAME locked hypothesis (require_preregistered still passes \u2014 content unchanged) with alpha=0.10, stability_threshold=0.6 -> supported. Verdict changed purely by post-hoc threshold choice. Same applies to confirm_unit_level's alpha.", "suggested_fix": "Add `alpha` (and optional `stability_threshold`) as typed fields on Hypothesis, include them in canonical_hypothesis_digest, and have the confirmers READ them from the locked hypothesis instead of accepting caller arguments \u2014 or, if kept as arguments, require they equal the locked values and raise PreRegistrationError on mismatch (mirroring _resolve_direction's cross-check). Build the recorded decision_rule from the locked value, not the runtime arg.", "dimension": "methodology-integrity / pre-registration firewall (HARKing)"}, {"id": "H-002", "severity": "medium", "file": "src/assay_engine/methodology/confirm.py", "line": "101-143", "title": "`powered` is a trusted, unbound caller flag that gates indeterminate\u2194decisive after the data is seen", "description": "verdict_from_pvalue takes `powered: bool` from the caller and routes `not powered -> indeterminate` (confirm.py:137-138). Verified: `powered` is neither a Hypothesis field nor part of the digest, and the engine performs no power calculation. A confirmer thus decides AFTER seeing the held-out result whether the test was 'powered enough' to be decisive. Combined with H-001 this is a second post-hoc lever: a significant-but-against-the-claim result can be neutralized to indeterminate with powered=False, or a favorable result promoted with powered=True. The docstring frames powered as a method-capability fact but nothing binds it to a pre-stated power/sample-size criterion.", "poc": "For one locked unit-level hypothesis with a fixed held-out statistic/p_value: confirm_unit_level(..., powered=True) -> supported/contradicted; call again with powered=False -> indeterminate. Both pass the identical pre-registration check; decisiveness chosen post-hoc.", "suggested_fix": "Require the power/decisiveness criterion (e.g. minimum n, minimum detectable effect) to be pre-registered in params (bound in the digest) and have the engine derive `powered` from the held-out sample it evaluated against that locked criterion, rather than accepting an opaque boolean. At minimum record the locked criterion in evidence and assert the supplied `powered` is consistent with it.", "dimension": "methodology-integrity / pre-registration firewall (HARKing)"}, {"id": "H-003", "severity": "medium", "file": "src/assay_engine/methodology/confirm.py", "line": "113-119, 146-197", "title": "Locked `test_name` is bound in the digest but never enforced against the test actually executed", "description": "Verified: test_name IS in canonical_hypothesis_digest (preregistration.py:114) but is read NOWHERE except when building the digest \u2014 no confirmer or runner checks that the executed test matches the pre-registered test_name. On the unit-level path this is most acute: confirm_unit_level receives only an opaque statistic/p_value/direction_supports_claim ('the engine cannot itself infer the tail', confirm.py:163-169) and verdict_from_pvalue records a hard-coded test_description='held-out unit-level test' (confirm.py:196) rather than the locked test_name. A study can pre-register test_name='mann_whitney_u', then after seeing held-out data run whichever test gives the desired p_value and feed it in; require_preregistered passes because content (including the now-meaningless test_name) is unchanged. The docstrings claim the locked content governs the verdict; for the test choice it does not.", "poc": "Lock a unit-level hypothesis with test_name='mann_whitney_u'. After seeing the held-out partition, compute p_value with a t-test that clears alpha and call confirm_unit_level(h, ..., p_value=that_p, direction_supports_claim=True, authority=auth) -> supported. require_preregistered raises nothing; the executed test silently differs from the locked one.", "suggested_fix": "Have the engine-side confirmer take the test identity it ran and assert it equals hypothesis.test_name (raise PreRegistrationError on mismatch), and propagate test_name into verdict_from_pvalue's test_description. Where the engine cannot see the test (study-owned confirmers), document explicitly that test-choice integrity is an un-enforced caller obligation so the docstrings stop over-claiming.", "dimension": "methodology-integrity / pre-registration firewall (HARKing)"}, {"id": "H-004", "severity": "medium", "file": "src/assay_engine/reasoning/seam.py", "line": 512, "title": "Malformed reasoning params leak a raw ValueError/TypeError through the typed public boundary (run/run_json)", "description": "Verified: _attempt() coerces caller params inline before any tier backend \u2014 `temperature = float(p.get('temperature', 0.0))` (seam.py:512) and `int(seed)` (seam.py:527). A non-numeric/None temperature or non-integer _seed raises a bare ValueError/TypeError inside _attempt, which runs under _run_with_retries whose except clauses catch ONLY PermanentReasoningError/RateLimitError/ReasoningError (seam.py:592). A raw ValueError/TypeError therefore propagates out of run()/run_json() unwrapped, violating the module's documented typed-error contract; a caller catching ReasoningError will crash. The error is semantically permanent (a malformed param is not retryable), so it should be a PermanentReasoningError.", "poc": "seam = TieredReasoningSeam(); for params in ({'temperature':'hot'}, {'temperature':None}, {'_seed':'notint'}): req = ReasoningRequest(prompt='x', tier=StakesTier.BULK, purpose='t', params=params); seam.run(req) -> raises raw ValueError/TypeError instead of ReasoningError.", "suggested_fix": "Coerce/validate temperature and _seed inside _attempt under a guard that re-raises as PermanentReasoningError, e.g. wrap the float()/int() in try/except (ValueError, TypeError) -> raise PermanentReasoningError(f'invalid reasoning param: {exc}') from exc. Keeps run()/run_json() to the documented typed-error contract.", "dimension": "error-handling / API contract integrity"}, {"id": "H-005", "severity": "medium", "file": "src/assay_engine/reasoning/seam.py", "line": "414-503", "title": "HIGH_STAKES tier lacks the output-length cap that BULK enforces (asymmetric runaway-generation guard)", "description": "Verified: BULK always sets num_predict=BULK_NUM_PREDICT (seam.py:99, 368) and its docstring calls this 'the real guard' because a wall-clock timeout cannot interrupt a call already blocked in generation. _high_stakes_complete (seam.py:414-503) builds ClaudeAgentOptions with NO max-output-tokens equivalent \u2014 grep for max_tokens in seam.py returns nothing. A runaway BULK generation is bounded by tokens AND wall-clock; a runaway HIGH_STAKES generation is bounded only by anyio.fail_after(HIGH_STAKES_TIMEOUT). The cheaper/safer bound was applied only to the local tier, and HIGH_STAKES is the one consuming the fixed-cost subscription budget. The asymmetry is the same the BULK docstring argues against.", "poc": "A BULK call cannot emit more than ASSAY_BULK_NUM_PREDICT (2048) tokens. The parallel HIGH_STAKES call has no analogous bound: a prompt eliciting a very long completion runs until HIGH_STAKES_TIMEOUT (300s default), consuming the full subscription turn and a bounded-pool worker slot.", "suggested_fix": "Add an ASSAY_HIGH_STAKES_MAX_TOKENS cap and pass it to ClaudeAgentOptions (or the SDK's max-output-tokens option), mirroring num_predict. If the Agent SDK cannot bound output, document the asymmetry explicitly in the HIGH_STAKES docstring the way BULK's is documented.", "dimension": "resource-bounding / cost-and-runtime guards"}, {"id": "H-006", "severity": "medium", "file": "src/assay_engine/methodology/discovery.py", "line": "69-165", "title": "discover_and_confirm lacks the on_step provenance hook that adjudicate_with_baseline has (asymmetric audit-trail wiring)", "description": "The two parallel firewall runners (discovery's own docstring calls itself 'the mirror of adjudication') were hardened asymmetrically for per-step provenance. adjudicate_with_baseline takes on_step: Callable[[Hypothesis, Verdict], None] | None (adjudication.py:193, 264-267) so a caller can record each pre-registered hypothesis+verdict to the hash-chained trail as each happens (#93), preserving GOVERNANCE \u00a73's 'recorded before the next step runs' ordering. discover_and_confirm (discovery.py:69-165) has no such hook: a standalone discovery study can only batch-record after the whole loop returns, losing the append-before-next-step ordering its twin enforces.", "poc": "adjudicate_with_baseline(..., on_step=lambda h,v: trail.record(...)) lands each verdict in confirmation order. discover_and_confirm(corpus, split, discover=..., confirm=..., authority=...) accepts no on_step, so a standalone study records verdicts only after the loop returns or by re-implementing the loop.", "suggested_fix": "Add an `on_step: Callable[[Hypothesis, Verdict], None] | None = None` parameter to discover_and_confirm and invoke it after each verdict is validated (right after the misattribution check at discovery.py:164), mirroring adjudication.py:264-267.", "dimension": "governance / provenance audit-trail completeness"}, {"id": "H-007", "severity": "medium", "file": "scripts/license_gate.py", "line": "29-43", "title": "License gate silently passes UNKNOWN / empty / missing-license dependencies (allow-by-default)", "description": "Verified: main() does `lic = (pkg.get('License') or '').upper()` (line 34) and only appends to `bad` when a deny token matches (line 37); a missing/empty/UNKNOWN field yields '' and is treated as ALLOWED. pip-licenses frequently reports UNKNOWN for packages whose license lives only in a classifier/LICENSE file. A genuinely GPL/AGPL/SSPL dependency whose metadata pip-licenses cannot parse therefore bypasses the Apache-2.0 compatibility gate entirely \u2014 the exact supply-chain blind spot the gate exists to close.", "poc": "Feed the gate [{'Name':'evil','Version':'1.0','License':'UNKNOWN'}, {'License':''}, {missing License}] -> main() returns 0 ('license gate OK').", "suggested_fix": "Treat unparsable licenses as deny-by-default: fail (or quarantine to a reviewed allowlist file) any package whose normalized license is in {'', 'UNKNOWN', 'UNKNOWN LICENSE', None}. Collect such packages into an 'unclassified' bucket that fails unless explicitly allowlisted (mirroring .pip-audit-ignore).", "dimension": "supply-chain / CI gate completeness"}, {"id": "H-008", "severity": "medium", "file": ".github/workflows/ci.yml", "line": 1, "title": "Workflow defines no top-level permissions: \u2014 GITHUB_TOKEN runs with the repo-default (often write) scope", "description": "Verified: grep for 'permissions' in ci.yml returns no permissions block on any job or at the workflow level. The token therefore inherits the repo/org default, which on many repos is the legacy read-write default granting contents:write/packages:write. The audit job runs third-party tooling (pip-audit, pip-licenses) and does 'pip install -e .' over the fully-resolved dependency tree; an injected import-time payload in that step would run with whatever the default token allows.", "poc": "grep -n 'permissions' .github/workflows/ci.yml -> no match. With the org/repo default set to read-write, $GITHUB_TOKEN in the audit job carries contents:write/packages:write.", "suggested_fix": "Add a top-level `permissions: { contents: read }` block and grant narrower per-job scopes only where needed, making the token least-privilege regardless of the repo/org default.", "dimension": "supply-chain / CI least-privilege"}, {"id": "H-009", "severity": "medium", "file": ".github/workflows/ci.yml", "line": "44-69", "title": "Integration gate reports green even when service containers never came up \u2014 live backend tests silently skip", "description": "The integration job declares postgres/qdrant service containers and the workflow comment claims the live checkpointer + vector-store tests run for real, but every live test is guarded by skipif(not postgres_up()/qdrant_up()), which probe localhost with a short TCP connect and SKIP (not fail) when unreachable. If an image fails to pull, the health check never passes, or a port mapping breaks, all live persistence/observability tests skip and pytest still exits 0 \u2014 so the required integration check goes green while exercising none of the real backends it claims to cover. The reproducibility/coverage guarantee is unenforced.", "poc": "conftest postgres_up()/qdrant_up() return False on OSError; live tests use skipif. No ASSAY_REQUIRE_*/xfail_strict/no-skip enforcement. Break the qdrant service port mapping -> test_qdrant_* SKIP, job still succeeds.", "suggested_fix": "In the CI integration lane only, set ASSAY_REQUIRE_LIVE_BACKENDS=1 and have conftest convert the skip into a hard failure when set (or assert postgres_up()/qdrant_up() in a session-scoped autouse fixture gated on that flag), so local/dev runs skip cleanly while CI guarantees the live paths ran.", "dimension": "CI / test-coverage enforcement"}, {"id": "H-010", "severity": "medium", "file": "src/assay_engine/baseline/determinism.py", "line": "10-17", "title": "stable_seed docstring claims \"distinct inputs get distinct seeds\" but truncates SHA-256 to 32 bits", "description": "Verified: the docstring states \"distinct inputs get distinct seeds\" (line 14) while the implementation returns int(digest,16) % (1 << bits) with bits defaulting to 32 (line 17). Modulo into a 32-bit space cannot guarantee distinct seeds for distinct inputs \u2014 by the birthday bound a collision becomes more-likely-than-not at ~77k inputs and is guaranteed past 2^32. build_baseline_artifact derives the reproducibility seed from this, so two distinct corpora/configs can collide onto the same seed, contradicting the stated property. This is a documentation over-claim, not a determinism break (the same input still maps to the same seed).", "poc": "stable_seed reduces a 256-bit digest to 32 bits; generate ~10^5 distinct part-strings and a duplicate seed appears (birthday bound). At small bits it is trivially demonstrable: stable_seed('a', bits=8) and stable_seed('b', bits=8) can collide in 256 buckets.", "suggested_fix": "Soften the docstring to the truthful property: \"the same inputs always yield the same seed; collisions between distinct inputs are possible because the digest is reduced to `bits` bits.\" Optionally widen the default seed space or document the collision probability.", "dimension": "documentation honesty / determinism over-claim"}, {"id": "H-011", "severity": "medium", "file": "src/assay_engine/orchestration/gatenode.py", "line": "90-161", "title": "Graph gate node warns-but-allows a missing trail recorder while run_study's gate path always records (asymmetric audit guarantee)", "description": "Verified: in run_study the gate handler always records to the trail before enforcing (pipeline.py run_gate). The LangGraph graph path make_gate_node (gatenode.py:90-161) makes the trail recorder OPTIONAL: with recorder=None it merely warns (lines 114-115) and records only into non-tamper-evident LangGraph state. The docstring itself (lines 108-112) calls this a 'GOVERNANCE \u00a73 audit gap' where a checkpoint edit flipping rejected->approved is undetectable by verify_records. The same governance concern is structurally guaranteed on one path and merely warned-about on the other.", "poc": "make_gate_node(gate, propose) with no recorder builds successfully (only a warning). Operator approvals then live solely in LangGraph checkpoint state; verify_records cannot detect a checkpoint tamper that flips the decision.", "suggested_fix": "Make the recorder mandatory on make_gate_node (raise when None), matching run_study's unconditional trail.record \u2014 or, if a recorder-less node must remain supported, gate it behind an explicit opt-out flag (allow_unrecorded=True) so shipping the audit-gap configuration is a conscious choice.", "dimension": "governance / provenance audit-trail completeness"}, {"id": "H-012", "severity": "low", "file": "src/assay_engine/orchestration/gatenode.py", "line": "21, 144", "title": "gatenode over-claims: \"atomically\" recorded trail+state, and \"terminal-decision idempotency\" that holds only for graph state", "description": "Two related docstring over-claims, verified. (1) The recorder block asserts the validated decision lands in the hash-chained trail and graph state 'atomically' (~line 144), but the code records to the trail first then returns the update for LangGraph to commit to a separate store; there is no shared transaction. Only the recorder-raises direction is handled \u2014 if the recorder succeeds and the subsequent checkpoint write (or process) fails, the trail holds the approval while graph state does not. (2) The module docstring (line 21) claims 'an approved decision is terminal: re-entering ... does not re-prompt or re-record', but already_decided() (line 79) reads only state['gate_decisions'] \u2014 never the trail. Since the two stores are not atomic and the trail has no de-dup, a re-entry where graph state lost the prior approval but the trail retained it passes already_decided()==False and the recorder appends a SECOND 'approved' entry to the append-only trail, contradicting 'does not re-record'.", "poc": "(B) Wire a recorder, resume a parked gate with a valid approval, kill the process after the node returns but before the checkpoint commits: trail holds 'approved', recovered checkpoint gate_decisions does not. (C) Restore graph state from a checkpoint taken before the gate_decisions update landed while keeping the same trail; re-enter -> already_decided() False -> re-prompt -> recorder appends a duplicate 'approved' entry.", "suggested_fix": "Replace 'atomically' with the accurate guarantee ('records to the trail BEFORE returning the update so a recorder failure aborts the decision; the two stores are NOT transactional \u2014 a crash between them can leave the trail ahead of graph state'). Scope the idempotency claim to graph state, or key already_decided() off the durable trail as well so the no-op also covers re-recording.", "dimension": "documentation honesty / consistency over-claim"}, {"id": "H-013", "severity": "low", "file": "src/assay_engine/pipeline.py", "line": "739-746", "title": "Provenance-trail artifact tempfile leaked on every tracked run (delete=False, never unlinked)", "description": "Verified (consolidates the two duplicate reports of this issue): run_study's best-effort _log_trail callback serializes the hash-chained trail to tempfile.NamedTemporaryFile('w', suffix='_provenance.json', delete=False) (pipeline.py:739-740) then calls t.log_artifact(rid, path) (line 744) and never unlinks the file. MLflow copies the file into the artifact store but does not remove the source, so every successful study run with a tracker leaves one orphaned *_provenance.json in TMPDIR; over a long-lived production node these accumulate unbounded. The file also holds provenance payloads at a default-mode temp path. Contrast versioning.py which uses mkstemp + try/finally unlink.", "poc": "Run run_study(plan, gate_handler=auto_approve, tracker=MlflowExperimentTracker()) N times; ls $TMPDIR/*_provenance.json shows N leaked files. There is no os.unlink in the _log_trail closure or surrounding finally.", "suggested_fix": "Wrap log_artifact in try/finally and os.unlink(path) afterward (MLflow has already copied it), or write into a tempfile.TemporaryDirectory() context that auto-cleans. Optionally chmod 0o600 since the file holds provenance payloads.", "dimension": "resource hygiene / data-at-rest"}, {"id": "H-014", "severity": "low", "file": "src/assay_engine/reasoning/seam.py", "line": "200-238", "title": "Module-global reasoning ThreadPoolExecutor cannot self-heal if a worker future vanishes \u2014 saturation guard can wedge permanently", "description": "The reasoning seam uses one process-global ThreadPoolExecutor; _submit_bounded increments _inflight before submit and decrements via add_done_callback(_release). Accounting is guarded for KeyboardInterrupt/SystemExit and pool-shutdown, but there is no recovery if a worker thread dies abnormally without the future ever completing (a C-level backend crash that takes the worker but not the process, or pool-state corruption): the done-callback never fires, _inflight is never decremented, and after _POOL_WORKERS such events every subsequent call raises RateLimitError('pool saturated') for the rest of the process lifetime. future.result() timeouts are handled (slot intentionally held), but a vanished future is not. Latent availability/leak risk on the exception path; the checkpoint pool by contrast is self-healing.", "poc": "Monkeypatch _pool.submit to return a Future that never resolves; call _with_timeout _POOL_WORKERS times -> each times out but the futures never complete, so _release never runs; the next _submit_bounded raises RateLimitError unconditionally and stays that way forever.", "suggested_fix": "Add a watchdog/reap step: track each in-flight future with its submission time and, in _submit_bounded's saturation branch, force _release on futures that are done-but-unreleased or have exceeded (per-call timeout + grace) before refusing. Document that wait=False atexit may abandon in-flight backend calls.", "dimension": "concurrency / availability resilience"}, {"id": "H-015", "severity": "low", "file": "src/assay_engine/methodology/confirm.py", "line": "180-197", "title": "Unit-level confirmation lacks the directionless-lock refusal its whole-corpus twin enforces (tail chosen post-hoc)", "description": "Verified: confirm_whole_corpus routes the locked direction through _resolve_direction (confirm.py:254-289), which REFUSES a directionless-but-locked hypothesis (None -> #G-001 HARKing guard) and rejects any tail not in {greater,less}. confirm_unit_level (confirm.py:180-197) only shape-validates a non-None predicted_direction and otherwise lets the caller-supplied direction_supports_claim flag decide; a locked hypothesis with predicted_direction=None passes unit-level confirmation with the polarity effectively chosen post-hoc by the caller \u2014 exactly the HARKing case the whole-corpus path refuses. The asymmetry is documented (confirm.py:163-169) but is a cross-mechanism guard-strength divergence.", "poc": "Lock a UNIT_LEVEL hypothesis with predicted_direction=None. confirm_unit_level(..., direction_supports_claim=True) -> supported; flip the flag -> the SAME locked hypothesis -> contradicted. The whole-corpus twin refuses this outright via _resolve_direction.", "suggested_fix": "If unit-level studies are expected to bind the tail at lock time, add a symmetric refusal: when a unit-level hypothesis is locked, require predicted_direction to be non-None. If the asymmetry must stand, emit a runtime warning when direction_supports_claim is supplied for a directionless-locked hypothesis, mirroring _gate_preregistration's authority=None warning.", "dimension": "methodology-integrity / cross-mechanism consistency"}, {"id": "H-016", "severity": "low", "file": "src/assay_engine/methodology/discovery.py", "line": "120-165", "title": "Confirm-step double-dip leak check (assert_confirm_only) enforced by confirm_unit_level but not by the discovery/pipeline runners", "description": "DiscoverConfirmSplit.assert_confirm_only (firewalls.py:126-145) is the runtime guard that the confirm step touched ONLY held-out ids. confirm_unit_level calls it on the caller-supplied evaluated_ids (confirm.py:188). The two composing runners that also confirm \u2014 discover_and_confirm (discovery.py) and the pipeline's DISCOVERY/CONFIRM block \u2014 never call assert_confirm_only; they only verify the held-out partition is non-empty and subset the corpus, then trust the study-supplied confirmer to evaluate within it without checking which ids it actually scored. A confirmer that internally pulls in discovery-partition units is caught by the standalone primitive but NOT by either runner. Firewall-B's leak check exists on one parallel mechanism and is absent on the other two.", "poc": "Build a confirm_held_out that ignores its held_out arg and evaluates discovery-partition units. confirm_unit_level(hyp, split=split, evaluated_ids=<discovery ids>, ...) raises FirewallViolation 'double-dipping'. The same leak through discover_and_confirm(... confirm=that_callable ...) completes cleanly because the runner never asks the confirmer which ids it evaluated.", "suggested_fix": "Have the discovery runners obtain (or require) the set of ids the confirmer evaluated and pass it through split.assert_confirm_only before accepting the verdict (e.g. have HeldOutConfirmer return evaluated ids alongside the verdict). At minimum document in both runners that the confirm-time double-dip check is delegated to the study, making the asymmetry with confirm_unit_level explicit.", "dimension": "methodology-integrity / firewall-B leak enforcement"}, {"id": "H-017", "severity": "low", "file": "src/assay_engine/provenance.py", "line": "211", "title": "Persisted provenance records carry no key/algorithm marker \u2014 a keyed (HMAC) trail is indistinguishable from an unkeyed one on re-verify", "description": "entries_to_records()/to_records() serialize seq/kind/summary/payload/timestamp/prev_hash/entry_hash but record nothing about whether the chain was sealed with SHA-256 (unkeyed) or HMAC-SHA256 (keyed). from_records()/verify_records() require the caller to supply the correct secret out-of-band; omitting it on a keyed trail fails with the generic 'content was altered, or the wrong key was used' error, conflating a missing-key config with genuine tampering. Conversely there is no recorded signal that a trail SHOULD have been keyed, so a downstream consumer cannot detect that an audit-grade trail was persisted unkeyed. Weakens the provenance-completeness claim for persisted trails.", "poc": "trail = ProvenanceTrail(secret=b'x'*16); trail.record('a','b'); recs = trail.to_records(); from_records(recs) -> raises ProvenanceError as if tampered, because no secret was passed and nothing in recs indicates one is required.", "suggested_fix": "Emit a non-authenticated-but-informative envelope alongside the records (e.g. {'algo':'hmac-sha256'|'sha256','keyed':bool}) so a verifier can fail with a precise 'this trail is keyed; supply the secret' message and a consumer can detect an unkeyed audit trail. Do not include the secret. Optionally fold an algorithm tag into the hashed preimage so it is itself integrity-protected.", "dimension": "governance / provenance completeness"}, {"id": "H-018", "severity": "low", "file": "scripts/capture_transcripts.py", "line": "67", "title": "Operator-username PII not redacted from transcripts when the home-dir name is short (<4 chars) or appears in a dash-delimited project slug", "description": "_scrub redacts the operator username two ways: the path rule '(/(?:Users|home)/)[^/\\s\"']+' (requires a literal '/Users/[REDACTED]') and _USERNAME_RE derived from Path.home().name. The Claude project-dir slug is dash-delimited (e.g. '-Users-[REDACTED]-hc-grc'); the path rule does NOT match it (no slashes), so the only mask inside slugs/git-author strings/prose is _USERNAME_RE \u2014 which returns None for any home-dir name shorter than 4 chars (to avoid over-redacting common words). For operators whose login is <4 chars, the username then leaks verbatim into committed transcripts via the slug and prose, defeating the stated #F-024/#G-007 PII-scrubbing goal. The docstring claims operator PII is redacted, so this is an undisclosed completeness gap.", "poc": "Set HOME so Path.home().name='bob'; create $SRC=.../-Users-[REDACTED]-hc-grc/<id>.jsonl containing the slug path and 'bob'. Run capture_transcripts \u2014 the committed transcript still contains 'bob' and '-Users-[REDACTED]-...' because _USERNAME_RE is None (len<4) and the '/Users/' rule never matches the dash slug.", "suggested_fix": "Always derive the slug form of the home path (replace os.sep with '-') and redact it explicitly regardless of username length; lower/remove the len<4 skip in favor of word-boundary-anchored token redaction. const V = { type:'object', required:['finding_id','confirmed','reasoning','severity_assessment'],
  properties:{ finding_id:{type:'string'}, confirmed:{type:'boolean'}, reasoning:{type:'string'},
    severity_assessment:{type:'string', enum:['critical','high','medium','low','false_positive']}}}
const vp=(f,n)=>`You are verifier #${n}, independent. Do NOT optimistically confirm. Read the ACTUAL code at ${ROOT} and decide if this is a TRUE production-readiness problem in CURRENT code (4 hardening passes applied). Confirmed=true ONLY if the problem exists as described, the PoC reproduces, and it is genuine (not speculative/already-handled/by-design). Cite file:line.\n\n${f.id}: ${f.title}\nFile: ${f.file}:${f.line}\nDescription: ${f.description}\nPoC: ${f.poc}\nSuggested fix: ${f.suggested_fix}`
phase('Verify')
const results = await pipeline(FINDINGS,
  f => parallel([
    () => agent(vp(f,1), { label:'vA:'+f.id, phase:'Verify', schema:V }),
    () => agent(vp(f,2), { label:'vB:'+f.id, phase:'Verify', schema:V }),
  ]),
  (vs,f)=>{ const [a,b]=vs.filter(Boolean); const both=a&&b&&a.confirmed&&b.confirmed;
    const neither=(!a||!a.confirmed)&&(!b||!b.confirmed);
    return { id:f.id, severity:f.severity, file:f.file, line:f.line, title:f.title,
      description:f.description, poc:f.poc, suggested_fix:f.suggested_fix, dimension:f.dimension,
      both_confirmed:both, neither_confirmed:neither, split:!both&&!neither,
      a:a?{c:a.confirmed,s:a.severity_assessment,why:a.reasoning}:null,
      b:b?{c:b.confirmed,s:b.severity_assessment,why:b.reasoning}:null } })
phase('Triage')
const confirmed=results.filter(Boolean).filter(r=>r.both_confirmed)
const rejected=results.filter(Boolean).filter(r=>r.neither_confirmed)
const split=results.filter(Boolean).filter(r=>r.split)
log('confirmed='+confirmed.length+' rejected='+rejected.length+' split='+split.length)
return { confirmed, rejected, split, all: results.filter(Boolean) }

confirmed.title: alpha and stability_threshold are post-hoc free parameters, not pre-registered — threshold HARKing survives the firewall
confirmed.description: The pre-registration digest (canonical_hypothesis_digest, preregistration.py:114-118) binds statement/kind/origin/test_name/decision_rule/source_claim_id/predicted_direction/params. Verified: `alpha` and `stability_threshold` are NOT fields on Hypothesis (hypothesis.py:61-74) and are NOT in the digest payload. They are supplied as plain runtime arguments to verdict_from_pvalue/confirm_whole_corpus/confirm_unit_level at confirmation time, and verdict_from_pvalue even CONSTRUCTS the recorded decision_rule string from the runtime alpha (confirm.py:131 `rule = f"...alpha={alpha}..."`) instead of comparing it against the locked decision_rule. Nothing reads hypothesis.decision_rule/params to constrain the confirm-time threshold. An analyst who has seen the null/baseline can therefore choose alpha=0.05 vs 0.10 or stability_threshold=0.9 vs 0.6 to flip indeterminate↔supported — exactly the post-hoc-threshold HARKing the firewall claims to prevent. The most decision-bearing numbers are not committed before confirmation, defeating the core methodological guarantee.
confirmed.poc: Lock a WHOLE_CORPUS hypothesis with predicted_direction='greater' (alpha not a field, so the threshold is unlocked). After seeing the null: confirm_whole_corpus(h, observed=X, null_distribution=null, alpha=0.05, resample_statistics=R, stability_threshold=0.9, authority=auth) -> indeterminate ('does not beat null at alpha'). Re-run the SAME locked hypothesis (require_preregistered still passes — content unchanged) with alpha=0.10, stability_threshold=0.6 -> supported. Verdict changed purely by post-hoc threshold choice. Same applies to confirm_unit_level's alpha.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Add `alpha` (and optional `stability_threshold`) as typed fields on Hypothesis, include them in canonical_hypothesis_digest, and have the confirmers READ them from the locked hypothesis instead of accepting caller arguments — or, if kept as arguments, require they equal the locked values and raise PreRegistrationError on mismatch (mirroring _resolve_direction's cross-check). Build the recorded decision_rule from the locked value, not the runtime arg.

a.why:
CONFIRMED in current code (4 passes applied). The finding is accurate on every checked point and the PoC reproduces empirically.

Code verification:
- `alpha` and `stability_threshold` are NOT fields on Hypothesis. The dataclass fields are hypothesis_id, statement, kind, origin, test_name, decision_rule, source_claim_id, locked_at, timestamp_proof, predicted_direction, params (hypothesis.py:61-74). No alpha, no stability_threshold.
- They are NOT in the pre-registration digest. canonical_hypothesis_digest payload binds only hypothesis_id/statement/kind/origin/test_name/decision_rule/source_claim_id/predicted_direction/params (preregistration.py:109-119). Neither threshold is covered by the cryptographic content binding.
- They are supplied as plain runtime args at confirm time: confirm_whole_corpus(..., alpha, stability_threshold=0.9, ...) (confirm.py:297,299), confirm_unit_level(..., alpha, ...) (confirm.py:153), verdict_from_pvalue(..., alpha, ...) (confirm.py:106).
- The recorded decision_rule is CONSTRUCTED from the runtime alpha/threshold rather than read from / checked against the locked hypothesis: confirm.py:131 (`rule = f"{test_description} at alpha={alpha}..."`) and confirm.py:356-359 (`f"...at alpha={alpha}...stability across resamples >= {stability_threshold}..."`). Nothing reads hypothesis.decision_rule or hypothesis.params to constrain the confirm-time thresholds. This contrasts with _resolve_direction (confirm.py:254-289) which DOES enforce the locked predicted_direction and raises on mismatch/None — proving the codebase knows how to bind a decision-bearing parameter but does not do so for the thresholds.

Empirical PoC (executed against the repo via .venv/bin/python): a WHOLE_CORPUS hypothesis locked with predicted_direction='greater' and verified with a real LocalHmacAuthority. With observed=3.0, a 500-sample null, and 600 resamples: confirm_whole_corpus(..., alpha=0.05, stability_threshold=0.9) -> INDETERMINATE ('unstable across resamples', stability=0.603); the SAME locked+verified hypothesis with stability_threshold=0.6 -> SUPPORTED. p_support unchanged at 0.0020. require_preregistered still passes in both runs because content is identical. Verdict flipped indeterminate->supported purely by a post-hoc threshold choice — exactly the threshold-HARKing the firewall claims to prevent.

Genuineness: not speculative, not by-design, not already-handled. The module's own docstrings (e.g. #G-001 on _resolve_direction) state that decision-bearing parameters must be committed before confirmation to prevent HARKing; alpha and stability_threshold are the most decision-bearing numbers and are left unbound, so this is a real gap in the stated methodological guarantee, present in current code.

Severity high (not critical): exploitation requires analyst access to the confirmer call site and the verdict only flips in the borderline zone; it does not corrupt the cryptographic lock itself or enable remote/unauthenticated compromise. But it defeats the core anti-HARKing guarantee the firewall advertises, so it is a true production-readiness defect. The suggested fix (add typed alpha/stability_threshold fields to Hypothesis, include them in canonical_hypothesis_digest, and either read them from the locked hypothesis or require equality and raise PreRegistrationError on mismatch, mirroring _resolve_direction) is correct and appropriate.

b.why:
Confirmed in current code and the PoC reproduces against the actual engine.

Evidence:
1. Hypothesis (src/assay_engine/methodology/hypothesis.py:61-74) has NO alpha or stability_threshold field.
2. canonical_hypothesis_digest (preregistration.py:109-119) hashes statement/kind/origin/test_name/decision_rule/source_claim_id/predicted_direction/params/hypothesis_id only — neither threshold is bound by the pre-registration proof.
3. alpha and stability_threshold are accepted as plain runtime arguments at confirm time: confirm_whole_corpus (confirm.py:297, 299), confirm_unit_level (confirm.py:153), verdict_from_pvalue (confirm.py:106). Nothing reads hypothesis.decision_rule or hypothesis.params to constrain them.
4. The recorded decision_rule is CONSTRUCTED from the runtime alpha rather than compared to the locked rule: confirm.py:131 (verdict_from_pvalue) and confirm.py:356-359 (confirm_whole_corpus). So the audit record reflects the post-hoc threshold, not a committed one.
5. This is in stark, deliberate contrast to _resolve_direction (confirm.py:254-289), which reads the LOCKED predicted_direction and rejects any contradicting confirm-time argument (#G-001, pass 4) — proving the authors hardened exactly this HARKing class for direction but left the two most decision-bearing thresholds unguarded.

PoC executed against the real engine (.venv/bin/python, Python 3.14): a single WHOLE_CORPUS hypothesis locked via lock_hypothesis with predicted_direction='greater', null=range(100), observed=92.0, resamples=[95]*7+[50]*3.

Same locked hypothesis, identical timestamp_proof, both pass require_preregistered. Verdict flipped from indeterminate to supported purely by post-hoc threshold selection after the null/baseline is visible — precisely the threshold-HARKing the pre-registration firewall claims to prevent.

Not speculative, not by-design (the module docstrings and #G-001 fix establish that committing decision-bearing numbers before confirmation IS the intended guarantee), and not already-handled. High rather than critical because exploitation requires an analyst with confirm-time control (an internal/methodological integrity threat, not a remote attacker) and the lock itself is otherwise sound; but it defeats the core methodological guarantee of the system, so it is a true production-readiness problem.

confirmed.title: `powered` is a trusted, unbound caller flag that gates indeterminate↔decisive after the data is seen
confirmed.description: verdict_from_pvalue takes `powered: bool` from the caller and routes `not powered -> indeterminate` (confirm.py:137-138). Verified: `powered` is neither a Hypothesis field nor part of the digest, and the engine performs no power calculation. A confirmer thus decides AFTER seeing the held-out result whether the test was 'powered enough' to be decisive. Combined with H-001 this is a second post-hoc lever: a significant-but-against-the-claim result can be neutralized to indeterminate with powered=False, or a favorable result promoted with powered=True. The docstring frames powered as a method-capability fact but nothing binds it to a pre-stated power/sample-size criterion.
confirmed.poc: For one locked unit-level hypothesis with a fixed held-out statistic/p_value: confirm_unit_level(..., powered=True) -> supported/contradicted; call again with powered=False -> indeterminate. Both pass the identical pre-registration check; decisiveness chosen post-hoc.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Require the power/decisiveness criterion (e.g. minimum n, minimum detectable effect) to be pre-registered in params (bound in the digest) and have the engine derive `powered` from the held-out sample it evaluated against that locked criterion, rather than accepting an opaque boolean. At minimum record the locked criterion in evidence and assert the supplied `powered` is consistent with it.

a.why: Verified in current code. `verdict_from_pvalue` (src/assay_engine/methodology/confirm.py:101-143) takes `powered: bool` and routes `not powered -> Verdict.indeterminate(... notes="underpowered")` at confirm.py:137-138. `confirm_unit_level` (confirm.py:146-197) accepts `powered` as a parameter (confirm.py:154) and passes it through untouched (confirm.py:194). The flag is genuinely unbound and trusted: (1) `powered` is NOT a Hypothesis field (hypothesis.py:61-74 lists all fields; no power/sample-size criterion among them); (2) `powered` is NOT part of the cryptographic content digest (canonical_hypothesis_digest payload, preregistration.py:109-119, covers id/statement/kind/origin/test_name/decision_rule/source_claim_id/predicted_direction/params but never `powered`); (3) the engine performs no power calculation — `grep -rn powered src/` returns only the literal pass-through sites in confirm.py. The pre-registration gate `_gate_preregistration` (confirm.py:40-63) only verifies lock fields/proof, so the identical locked hypothesis with the identical held-out statistic/p_value passes the same gate regardless of `powered`. PoC reproduces exactly: confirm_unit_level(..., powered=True) with p_value<=alpha returns supported/contradicted (confirm.py:139-142); the same call with powered=False returns indeterminate (confirm.py:137-138). Decisiveness is therefore selected post-hoc by an opaque caller boolean after the held-out result is seen — a second post-hoc lever alongside direction_supports_claim, and structurally the same HARKing class the whole-corpus path was explicitly hardened against in _resolve_direction (#G-001, confirm.py:254-289). Not by-design in any documented sense: the docstring (confirm.py:113) frames `powered` as a method-capability fact ("the method cannot decide") but nothing binds it to a pre-stated criterion. Severity medium rather than high: exploitation requires a trusted in-process confirmer (same trust boundary as the already-accepted direction_supports_claim flag), it is not a remote/unauthenticated vector, and the supplied value is at least echoed into the recorded rule string (confirm.py:131) for partial auditability. The suggested fix (pre-register a power/decisiveness criterion in params so it is bound in the digest, and derive `powered` from the evaluated held-out sample against that locked criterion) is appropriate.

b.why:
Verified against current code (4 hardening passes applied). The finding is TRUE and the PoC reproduces exactly.

Evidence:
- `verdict_from_pvalue` takes `powered: bool` from the caller (src/assay_engine/methodology/confirm.py:107) and routes `not powered -> indeterminate` (confirm.py:137-138, notes="underpowered"). `confirm_unit_level` forwards the caller's `powered` unchanged (confirm.py:154,194).
- `powered` is NOT a Hypothesis field — confirmed via `Hypothesis.__dataclass_fields__` (src/assay_engine/methodology/hypothesis.py:61-74 lists every field; no `powered`).
- It is therefore NOT bound in the canonical digest: `canonical_hypothesis_digest` (src/assay_engine/methodology/preregistration.py:109-119) hashes only id/statement/kind/origin/test_name/decision_rule/source_claim_id/predicted_direction/params; `powered` is an out-of-band function argument.
- No power/sample-size calculation exists anywhere in the engine (grep for power/min_n/mde/sample_size finds only this boolean and the verdict docstring); the engine simply trusts the flag.

PoC reproduced live (.venv/bin/python): one locked UNIT_LEVEL hypothesis, fixed statistic=1.0, p_value=0.01, alpha=0.05, direction_supports_claim=True, evaluated on the held-out confirm partition. confirm_unit_level(..., powered=True) -> SUPPORTED; the identical call with powered=False -> INDETERMINATE (notes="underpowered"). Both pass the same pre-registration gate. Decisiveness is chosen post-hoc by an opaque caller boolean with no locked criterion behind it.

This is a genuine integrity weakness, not speculative or by-design-acceptable: the methodology's purpose is to prevent post-hoc manipulation of verdicts, and `powered` is a second post-hoc lever (parallel to the direction flag) on the decisive/indeterminate axis. The suggested fix (pre-register a power/decisiveness criterion in params so it enters the digest, and have the engine derive `powered` from the evaluated held-out sample against that locked criterion — or at minimum record the locked criterion in evidence and assert consistency) is appropriate.

confirmed.title: Locked `test_name` is bound in the digest but never enforced against the test actually executed
confirmed.description: Verified: test_name IS in canonical_hypothesis_digest (preregistration.py:114) but is read NOWHERE except when building the digest — no confirmer or runner checks that the executed test matches the pre-registered test_name. On the unit-level path this is most acute: confirm_unit_level receives only an opaque statistic/p_value/direction_supports_claim ('the engine cannot itself infer the tail', confirm.py:163-169) and verdict_from_pvalue records a hard-coded test_description='held-out unit-level test' (confirm.py:196) rather than the locked test_name. A study can pre-register test_name='mann_whitney_u', then after seeing held-out data run whichever test gives the desired p_value and feed it in; require_preregistered passes because content (including the now-meaningless test_name) is unchanged. The docstrings claim the locked content governs the verdict; for the test choice it does not.
confirmed.poc: Lock a unit-level hypothesis with test_name='mann_whitney_u'. After seeing the held-out partition, compute p_value with a t-test that clears alpha and call confirm_unit_level(h, ..., p_value=that_p, direction_supports_claim=True, authority=auth) -> supported. require_preregistered raises nothing; the executed test silently differs from the locked one.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Have the engine-side confirmer take the test identity it ran and assert it equals hypothesis.test_name (raise PreRegistrationError on mismatch), and propagate test_name into verdict_from_pvalue's test_description. Where the engine cannot see the test (study-owned confirmers), document explicitly that test-choice integrity is an un-enforced caller obligation so the docstrings stop over-claiming.

2. test_name is read NOWHERE else. grep across src/ returns exactly two references: the field definition (hypothesis.py:65) and the digest payload (preregistration.py:114). No confirmer or runner compares the executed test against the locked test_name.

(3) confirm_unit_level receives only opaque statistic/p_value/direction_supports_claim and explicitly cannot infer the tail/test (confirm.py:163-169, 120-125). The PoC reproduces: lock test_name='mann_whitney_u', then after seeing the held-out partition compute p_value with any other test (e.g. a t-test) that clears alpha and call confirm_unit_level(h, ..., p_value=that_p, direction_supports_claim=True, authority=auth). require_preregistered raises nothing because hypothesis content (including the test_name field) is unchanged — nothing ties the supplied p_value to the test named in test_name. Verdict returns 'supported'. This is post-hoc test selection (p-hacking/HARKing) that the methodology claims to prevent.

Severity = high (not critical): the unit-level path is deliberately a trusted-caller boundary (the engine delegates statistic computation to study-owned code, like direction_supports_claim), so for study-owned confirmers some delegation is by-design and the docstrings could be amended to honestly disclaim it. BUT it is a genuine production-readiness defect because (a) it is directly exploitable to manufacture a 'supported' verdict, (b) the module docstring (preregistration.py:17-18) over-claims that changing 'the test' after locking causes verification to fail — true only for the test_name string field, not the executed test, which is materially misleading, and (c) the exact-same HARKing class was already fixed for whole-corpus predicted_direction (#G-001 in _resolve_direction) but the analogous protection/disclosure for test choice was never applied. The suggested fix is sound: propagate test_name into test_description, offer an engine-side assertion where the test identity is visible, and explicitly document the un-enforced caller obligation where it is not. Not a false positive, not speculative, not already-handled.

confirmed.title: Malformed reasoning params leak a raw ValueError/TypeError through the typed public boundary (run/run_json)
confirmed.description: Verified: _attempt() coerces caller params inline before any tier backend — `temperature = float(p.get('temperature', 0.0))` (seam.py:512) and `int(seed)` (seam.py:527). A non-numeric/None temperature or non-integer _seed raises a bare ValueError/TypeError inside _attempt, which runs under _run_with_retries whose except clauses catch ONLY PermanentReasoningError/RateLimitError/ReasoningError (seam.py:592). A raw ValueError/TypeError therefore propagates out of run()/run_json() unwrapped, violating the module's documented typed-error contract; a caller catching ReasoningError will crash. The error is semantically permanent (a malformed param is not retryable), so it should be a PermanentReasoningError.
confirmed.poc: seam = TieredReasoningSeam(); for params in ({'temperature':'hot'}, {'temperature':None}, {'_seed':'notint'}): req = ReasoningRequest(prompt='x', tier=StakesTier.BULK, purpose='t', params=params); seam.run(req) -> raises raw ValueError/TypeError instead of ReasoningError.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Coerce/validate temperature and _seed inside _attempt under a guard that re-raises as PermanentReasoningError, e.g. wrap the float()/int() in try/except (ValueError, TypeError) -> raise PermanentReasoningError(f'invalid reasoning param: {exc}') from exc. Keeps run()/run_json() to the documented typed-error contract.

a.why:
Confirmed in current code and PoC reproduces exactly. _attempt() coerces caller params inline before any backend: `temperature = float(p.get("temperature", 0.0))` at src/assay_engine/reasoning/seam.py:512 and `int(seed)` at seam.py:527. _attempt runs under _run_with_retries (seam.py:591), whose except clauses (seam.py:592-599) catch ONLY PermanentReasoningError, RateLimitError, and ReasoningError. A raw ValueError/TypeError from float()/int() therefore propagates unwrapped through run() (seam.py:649-652). run_json() is also affected at seam.py:665 (`float(request.params.get("temperature", 0.0))` outside any handler).

Ran the PoC with the project venv (.venv/bin/python, 3.14): {'temperature':'hot'} -> raw ValueError; {'temperature':None} -> raw TypeError; {'_seed':'notint'} -> raw ValueError. None are wrapped as ReasoningError, violating the module's documented typed-error contract (module docstring/error hierarchy at seam.py:66-74 establish ReasoningError as the single typed boundary; every other failure mode is funneled through it). A caller catching ReasoningError, as the contract invites, will crash.

No upstream validation/coercion of params['temperature'] or ['_seed'] exists — params is an arbitrary caller-supplied Mapping[str, Any], frozen only (seam.py:57-60). The path is genuinely reachable; not by-design or already-handled. The error is semantically permanent (a malformed param is never retryable), so the suggested fix (wrap float()/int() in try/except (ValueError, TypeError) -> raise PermanentReasoningError(...) from exc) is correct and consistent with seam.py:554's handling of bad input.

Severity: low. It is a real API-contract/robustness gap, but it is triggered only by a caller passing programming-error-grade invalid params (non-numeric temperature, non-int seed), not by runtime/environmental conditions, and carries no security, data-integrity, or availability impact beyond an unwrapped exception type.

confirmed.title: License gate silently passes UNKNOWN / empty / missing-license dependencies (allow-by-default)
confirmed.description: Verified: main() does `lic = (pkg.get('License') or '').upper()` (line 34) and only appends to `bad` when a deny token matches (line 37); a missing/empty/UNKNOWN field yields '' and is treated as ALLOWED. pip-licenses frequently reports UNKNOWN for packages whose license lives only in a classifier/LICENSE file. A genuinely GPL/AGPL/SSPL dependency whose metadata pip-licenses cannot parse therefore bypasses the Apache-2.0 compatibility gate entirely — the exact supply-chain blind spot the gate exists to close.

confirmed.suggested_fix: Treat unparsable licenses as deny-by-default: fail (or quarantine to a reviewed allowlist file) any package whose normalized license is in {'', 'UNKNOWN', 'UNKNOWN LICENSE', None}. Collect such packages into an 'unclassified' bucket that fails unless explicitly allowlisted (mirroring .pip-audit-ignore).

confirmed.title: Workflow defines no top-level permissions: — GITHUB_TOKEN runs with the repo-default (often write) scope
confirmed.description: Verified: grep for 'permissions' in ci.yml returns no permissions block on any job or at the workflow level. The token therefore inherits the repo/org default, which on many repos is the legacy read-write default granting contents:write/packages:write. The audit job runs third-party tooling (pip-audit, pip-licenses) and does 'pip install -e .' over the fully-resolved dependency tree; an injected import-time payload in that step would run with whatever the default token allows.
confirmed.poc: grep -n 'permissions' .github/workflows/ci.yml -> no match. With the org/repo default set to read-write, $GITHUB_TOKEN in the audit job carries contents:write/packages:write.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Add a top-level `permissions: { contents: read }` block and grant narrower per-job scopes only where needed, making the token least-privilege regardless of the repo/org default.

a.why: Verified against current code. .github/workflows/ci.yml has no `permissions:` key at the workflow level or on any of its four jobs (core, integration, audit, all-checks). `grep -rn 'permissions' .github/` returns no match (exit 1); ci.yml is the only workflow file. Therefore GITHUB_TOKEN runs with the repo/org default scope, which on permissively-configured repos/orgs is the legacy read-write default (contents:write, packages:write, etc.). The audit and license-gate steps run `pip install -e .` and execute third-party tooling (pip-audit==2.10.1, pip-licenses==5.5.5, uv==0.11.21) — installs are hash-pinned, but dependency import/build-time code still executes during tests/license gating and would inherit the token's scope. None of the jobs push commits, publish packages, or create releases, so they need no write scope; adding a top-level `permissions: { contents: read }` is safe, non-breaking, and the standard least-privilege hardening control (matches OpenSSF Scorecard Token-Permissions and GitHub's hardening guidance). The finding's factual claims and PoC reproduce exactly. Severity is medium rather than high because the actual presence of write scope is conditional on the repo/org default-permissions setting (not visible in the code); where that default is read-only the live exploitability is low, making this a defense-in-depth/least-privilege gap rather than a guaranteed privilege escalation.

The PoC reproduces exactly (no grep match). This is a genuine, well-recognized least-privilege hardening gap (set explicit permissions: {contents: read} at workflow level). confirmed.title: Integration gate reports green even when service containers never came up — live backend tests silently skip

confirmed.title: stable_seed docstring claims "distinct inputs get distinct seeds" but truncates SHA-256 to 32 bits
confirmed.description: Verified: the docstring states "distinct inputs get distinct seeds" (line 14) while the implementation returns int(digest,16) % (1 << bits) with bits defaulting to 32 (line 17). Modulo into a 32-bit space cannot guarantee distinct seeds for distinct inputs — by the birthday bound a collision becomes more-likely-than-not at ~77k inputs and is guaranteed past 2^32. build_baseline_artifact derives the reproducibility seed from this, so two distinct corpora/configs can collide onto the same seed, contradicting the stated property. This is a documentation over-claim, not a determinism break (the same input still maps to the same seed).
confirmed.poc: stable_seed reduces a 256-bit digest to 32 bits; generate ~10^5 distinct part-strings and a duplicate seed appears (birthday bound). At small bits it is trivially demonstrable: stable_seed('a', bits=8) and stable_seed('b', bits=8) can collide in 256 buckets.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Soften the docstring to the truthful property: "the same inputs always yield the same seed; collisions between distinct inputs are possible because the digest is reduced to `bits` bits." Optionally widen the default seed space or document the collision probability.

a.why: Confirmed as a documentation over-claim in current code. In src/assay_engine/baseline/determinism.py, stable_seed's docstring (line 73) states "distinct inputs get distinct seeds," but the implementation (line 76) returns int(digest,16) % (1 << bits) with bits defaulting to 32 (line 69). Reducing a 256-bit SHA-256 digest into a 32-bit space cannot be injective: collisions are guaranteed past 2^32 distinct inputs (pigeonhole) and more-likely-than-not at ~77k inputs (birthday bound). PoC reproduces: at bits=8, distinct inputs "15" and "24" both map to seed 219. build_baseline_artifact derives the reproducibility seed from stable_seed (line 153), so two distinct corpora/configs can share a seed, contradicting the stated property. This is genuine (the false property is literally asserted), not speculative or by-design. However, it is purely a doc over-claim with no functional/security impact: same input still yields same seed (determinism holds), and each artifact still records its own distinct corpus_fingerprint and input_hashes (lines 138-144, 155), so a coincident seed integer does not break reproducibility. Severity: low — fix is to soften the docstring (and optionally widen default bits / document collision probability).

b.why: Verified against current code. determinism.py:73 docstring states "distinct inputs get distinct seeds" while determinism.py:76 implements `return int(digest, 16) % (1 << bits)` with `bits: int = 32` default (line 69). Reducing a 256-bit SHA-256 digest into a 32-bit space via modulo provably cannot guarantee distinct seeds for distinct inputs. PoC reproduces: stable_seed('15') and stable_seed('24') collide at bits=8 (seed 219), and the 32-bit default collides after ~107k distinct part-strings (consistent with the cited birthday bound where collision becomes more-likely-than-not near ~77k). build_baseline_artifact derives the reproducibility seed from this at line 153 (`seed = stable_seed(*(input_hashes[k] for k in sorted(input_hashes)))`), so two distinct corpora/configs can collide onto the same seed, contradicting the stated property. This is a genuine documentation over-claim in current code (after 4 hardening passes) — not speculative and not already-handled. Severity is low: it is a docstring accuracy issue, not a determinism/correctness break (the same input still deterministically maps to the same seed, and seed collisions across distinct studies do not affect each study's own reproducibility). Suggested fix to soften the docstring is appropriate.

confirmed.title: Graph gate node warns-but-allows a missing trail recorder while run_study's gate path always records (asymmetric audit guarantee)
confirmed.description: Verified: in run_study the gate handler always records to the trail before enforcing (pipeline.py run_gate). The LangGraph graph path make_gate_node (gatenode.py:90-161) makes the trail recorder OPTIONAL: with recorder=None it merely warns (lines 114-115) and records only into non-tamper-evident LangGraph state. The docstring itself (lines 108-112) calls this a 'GOVERNANCE §3 audit gap' where a checkpoint edit flipping rejected->approved is undetectable by verify_records. The same governance concern is structurally guaranteed on one path and merely warned-about on the other.
confirmed.poc: make_gate_node(gate, propose) with no recorder builds successfully (only a warning). Operator approvals then live solely in LangGraph checkpoint state; verify_records cannot detect a checkpoint tamper that flips the decision.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Make the recorder mandatory on make_gate_node (raise when None), matching run_study's unconditional trail.record — or, if a recorder-less node must remain supported, gate it behind an explicit opt-out flag (allow_unrecorded=True) so shipping the audit-gap configuration is a conscious choice.

a.why: Verified against current code. The asymmetry is real and exists as described. run_study's gate path (src/assay_engine/pipeline.py:326-342, run_gate) unconditionally calls trail.record(...) before enforcing the decision — the tamper-evident hash-chained trail is structurally guaranteed. The LangGraph graph path make_gate_node (src/assay_engine/orchestration/gatenode.py:90-161) declares recorder as optional (default None, line 94); when None it merely emits warnings.warn (lines 114-120) and the node body skips trail recording entirely (the recorder-is-not-None guard at line 143). The docstring itself (lines 108-112) labels this a 'GOVERNANCE §3 audit gap' where a checkpoint edit flipping rejected->approved is undetectable by verify_records. PoC reproduces exactly: make_gate_node(gate, propose) with no recorder builds successfully (warning only) and records operator decisions solely into non-tamper-evident LangGraph checkpoint state. The same governance invariant is thus structurally enforced on one path and merely warned-about on the other. Not speculative, not by-design (the docstring itself flags it as a gap), and not fully handled — pass 3 (#F-007) added only a warning, which is suppressible and easily unseen in production, leaving the insecure configuration shippable by accident. Severity is medium rather than high: it sits behind the optional 'orchestration' extra (not the default run_study path), requires the operator to both ship a recorder-less node and have checkpoint-tamper access, and a loud warning is emitted. The suggested fix (mandatory recorder, or explicit allow_unrecorded opt-out) is appropriate to make shipping the audit gap a conscious choice rather than an accidental default.

b.why:
Confirmed in current code. The asymmetry is real: run_study's run_gate (src/assay_engine/pipeline.py:326-342) records every gate decision to the hash-chained ProvenanceTrail unconditionally (trail.record at lines 331-338) before enforcing. The LangGraph graph path's make_gate_node (src/assay_engine/orchestration/gatenode.py:90-161) makes the recorder OPTIONAL: with recorder=None it only warns (lines 114-120) and the node still builds and runs, persisting decisions solely into LangGraph checkpoint state (the apply_gate_decision update at line 71), which is not tamper-evident. The recorder is invoked only inside `if recorder is not None` (line 143), so a recorder-less node never touches the chained trail.

PoC reproduces exactly: using the project's .venv/bin/python, make_gate_node(gate, propose) with no recorder returns a callable node and emits only a UserWarning ("built with no recorder: gate decisions will NOT be written to the hash-chained provenance trail (GOVERNANCE §3 audit gap)"); no exception is raised. So the audit-gap configuration ships with merely a warning.

The finding's claim is faithful to the code, and the docstring itself (gatenode.py:108-112) labels this a GOVERNANCE §3 audit gap where a checkpoint edit flipping rejected->approved is undetectable by verify_records. The graph path is a first-class, documented, supported execution path (compile_graph in graph.py supports gate-bearing graphs and even fails loud when a checkpointer is missing, lines 67-71), which makes the warns-but-allows treatment of the recorder genuinely inconsistent with the codebase's own fail-loud convention for the analogous checkpointer requirement.

Severity medium rather than high: this is an audit-integrity / fail-safe-default hardening gap, not an exploitable-by-default vulnerability. It is mitigated by a loud runtime warning and prominent docstrings in both make_gate_node and compile_graph, and exploitation requires an operator to both omit the recorder AND tamper with the durable checkpoint store. The suggested fix (mandatory recorder, or an explicit allow_unrecorded=True opt-out) is sound and matches the codebase's existing fail-loud pattern (graph.py:67-71). Not a false positive, not by-design, not already-handled.

confirmed.title: gatenode over-claims: "atomically" recorded trail+state, and "terminal-decision idempotency" that holds only for graph state
confirmed.description: Two related docstring over-claims, verified. (1) The recorder block asserts the validated decision lands in the hash-chained trail and graph state 'atomically' (~line 144), but the code records to the trail first then returns the update for LangGraph to commit to a separate store; there is no shared transaction. Only the recorder-raises direction is handled — if the recorder succeeds and the subsequent checkpoint write (or process) fails, the trail holds the approval while graph state does not. (2) The module docstring (line 21) claims 'an approved decision is terminal: re-entering ... does not re-prompt or re-record', but already_decided() (line 79) reads only state['gate_decisions'] — never the trail. Since the two stores are not atomic and the trail has no de-dup, a re-entry where graph state lost the prior approval but the trail retained it passes already_decided()==False and the recorder appends a SECOND 'approved' entry to the append-only trail, contradicting 'does not re-record'.
confirmed.poc: (B) Wire a recorder, resume a parked gate with a valid approval, kill the process after the node returns but before the checkpoint commits: trail holds 'approved', recovered checkpoint gate_decisions does not. (C) Restore graph state from a checkpoint taken before the gate_decisions update landed while keeping the same trail; re-enter -> already_decided() False -> re-prompt -> recorder appends a duplicate 'approved' entry.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Replace 'atomically' with the accurate guarantee ('records to the trail BEFORE returning the update so a recorder failure aborts the decision; the two stores are NOT transactional — a crash between them can leave the trail ahead of graph state'). Scope the idempotency claim to graph state, or key already_decided() off the durable trail as well so the no-op also covers re-recording.

a.why: Both over-claims are present in current code at the cited lines. (1) gatenode.py:144-146 comment asserts the decision lands in the hash-chained trail and graph state "atomically," but the implementation records to the trail (recorder(rec), line 152) then returns the partial update (line 159) for LangGraph to commit to a separate checkpoint store. There is no shared transaction; only the recorder-raises direction is guarded (lines 153-158). If the recorder succeeds and the subsequent checkpoint commit/process fails, the trail holds "approved" while graph state does not — exactly PoC (B). The word "atomically" is therefore an over-claim. (2) The module docstring (lines 21-24) states an approved decision is terminal and re-entry "does not re-prompt or re-record," but already_decided() (lines 79-84) reads only state["gate_decisions"], never the durable trail. Because the two stores are not transactional and the trail is append-only with no de-dup (recorder simply appends per line 152), a re-entry from a checkpoint taken before the gate_decisions update landed — while the same trail is reused — yields already_decided()==False, re-fires the interrupt, and appends a SECOND "approved" trail entry, contradicting "does not re-record" (PoC C). Both PoC scenarios are logically reproducible given the architecture. This is a genuine documentation/contract over-claim, not speculative or already-handled. It is not a runtime crash or security flaw — the underlying split between LangGraph-owned checkpoint commit and append-only trail is by design — so the defect is that the docstrings/comments promise stronger (atomic, idempotent-across-stores) guarantees than the code delivers. Severity low: fix is to correct the wording and/or scope the idempotency claim to graph state (or key already_decided() off the trail too).

confirmed.title: Provenance-trail artifact tempfile leaked on every tracked run (delete=False, never unlinked)

MLflow copies the file into the artifact store but does not remove the source, so every successful study run with a tracker leaves one orphaned *_provenance.json in TMPDIR; over a long-lived production node these accumulate unbounded. The file also holds provenance payloads at a default-mode temp path. Contrast versioning.py which uses mkstemp + try/finally unlink.
confirmed.poc: Run run_study(plan, gate_handler=auto_approve, tracker=MlflowExperimentTracker()) N times; ls $TMPDIR/*_provenance.json shows N leaked files. There is no os.unlink in the _log_trail closure or surrounding finally.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Wrap log_artifact in try/finally and os.unlink(path) afterward (MLflow has already copied it), or write into a tempfile.TemporaryDirectory() context that auto-cleans. Optionally chmod 0o600 since the file holds provenance payloads.

confirmed.title: Unit-level confirmation lacks the directionless-lock refusal its whole-corpus twin enforces (tail chosen post-hoc)
confirmed.description: Verified: confirm_whole_corpus routes the locked direction through _resolve_direction (confirm.py:254-289), which REFUSES a directionless-but-locked hypothesis (None -> #G-001 HARKing guard) and rejects any tail not in {greater,less}. confirm_unit_level (confirm.py:180-197) only shape-validates a non-None predicted_direction and otherwise lets the caller-supplied direction_supports_claim flag decide; a locked hypothesis with predicted_direction=None passes unit-level confirmation with the polarity effectively chosen post-hoc by the caller — exactly the HARKing case the whole-corpus path refuses. The asymmetry is documented (confirm.py:163-169) but is a cross-mechanism guard-strength divergence.
confirmed.poc: Lock a UNIT_LEVEL hypothesis with predicted_direction=None. confirm_unit_level(..., direction_supports_claim=True) -> supported; flip the flag -> the SAME locked hypothesis -> contradicted. The whole-corpus twin refuses this outright via _resolve_direction.
confirmed.suggested_fix: If unit-level studies are expected to bind the tail at lock time, add a symmetric refusal: when a unit-level hypothesis is locked, require predicted_direction to be non-None. If the asymmetry must stand, emit a runtime warning when direction_supports_claim is supplied for a directionless-locked hypothesis, mirroring _gate_preregistration's authority=None warning.

Hypothesis.predicted_direction defaults to None and __post_init__ (hypothesis.py:80-94) permits None, so a UNIT_LEVEL hypothesis can be locked directionless. _gate_preregistration (confirm.py:40-63) checks lock/pre-registration binding but does NOT require a non-None predicted_direction. Therefore the PoC reproduces exactly: a directionless-but-locked UNIT_LEVEL hypothesis passes unit-level confirmation with the polarity chosen post-hoc by the caller, while the whole-corpus twin refuses the identical situation via _resolve_direction's #G-001 HARKing guard (lines 270-277). This is a real cross-mechanism guard-strength divergence: the HARKing refusal that the whole-corpus path enforces has no equivalent (neither a symmetric refusal nor even a runtime warning like the authority=None warning) at unit level. Mitigating: the asymmetry is documented as by-design (confirm.py:163-169, #G-017) because the engine cannot itself infer the unit-level tail from an opaque held-out statistic, so direction_supports_claim is an intentional trusted caller obligation; the open HARKing path requires a caller to deliberately drive a directionless-locked hypothesis. It is genuine but a narrowly-scoped integrity-hardening gap rather than a critical/high defect, hence medium.

b.why:
Verified against current code at src/assay_engine/methodology/confirm.py.

The described asymmetry is real and the PoC reproduces:
- confirm_whole_corpus routes the locked tail through _resolve_direction (confirm.py:254-289), which at lines 271-277 REFUSES a directionless-but-locked hypothesis (predicted_direction is None) as HARKing (#G-001) and rejects any tail outside {greater,less}.
- confirm_unit_level (confirm.py:146-197) only shape-validates predicted_direction WHEN it is non-None (line 180: `if hypothesis.predicted_direction is not None and ...`). A None locked direction skips that guard entirely. It then calls _gate_preregistration (lock check only) and verdict_from_pvalue, where the polarity is decided SOLELY by the caller-supplied direction_supports_claim flag (confirm.py:140-142). So a directionless-locked unit-level hypothesis confirms as `supported` with direction_supports_claim=True and `contradicted` with the flag flipped — the exact post-hoc tail-selection (HARKing) the whole-corpus path refuses. PoC reproduces.

Severity is medium rather than high because: (1) the asymmetry is explicitly documented as by-design at confirm.py:163-169 — the unit-level path receives an opaque held-out statistic/p_value and structurally cannot derive the tail itself, so the engine delegates the deciding flag to the caller with a documented obligation to derive it from the locked tail; (2) it is not a silent computational error but a delegated contract.
