confirmed.title: Confirm-step double-dip leak check (assert_confirm_only) enforced by confirm_unit_level but not by the discovery/pipeline runners
confirmed.description: DiscoverConfirmSplit.assert_confirm_only (firewalls.py:126-145) is the runtime guard that the confirm step touched ONLY held-out ids. confirm_unit_level calls it on the caller-supplied evaluated_ids (confirm.py:188). The two composing runners that also confirm — discover_and_confirm (discovery.py) and the pipeline's DISCOVERY/CONFIRM block — never call assert_confirm_only; they only verify the held-out partition is non-empty and subset the corpus, then trust the study-supplied confirmer to evaluate within it without checking which ids it actually scored. A confirmer that internally pulls in discovery-partition units is caught by the standalone primitive but NOT by either runner. Firewall-B's leak check exists on one parallel mechanism and is absent on the other two.
confirmed.poc: Build a confirm_held_out that ignores its held_out arg and evaluates discovery-partition units. confirm_unit_level(hyp, split=split, evaluated_ids=<discovery ids>, ...) raises FirewallViolation 'double-dipping'. The same leak through discover_and_confirm(... confirm=that_callable ...) completes cleanly because the runner never asks the confirmer which ids it evaluated.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Have the discovery runners obtain (or require) the set of ids the confirmer evaluated and pass it through split.assert_confirm_only before accepting the verdict (e.g. have HeldOutConfirmer return evaluated ids alongside the verdict). At minimum document in both runners that the confirm-time double-dip check is delegated to the study, making the asymmetry with confirm_unit_level explicit.

a.why:
The factual claims are accurate against current code. `DiscoverConfirmSplit.assert_confirm_only` (firewalls.py:126-145) is the per-id double-dip leak check, and a src-wide grep shows it has exactly ONE caller: confirm_unit_level at confirm.py:188, where evaluated_ids is an explicit parameter. The two composing runners do NOT call it: discover_and_confirm (discovery.py:69-165) subsets the corpus (subset_corpus), guards that both partitions are non-empty (lines 100-126), validates DISCOVERY origin, pre-registration, and verdict<->hypothesis attribution (lines 133-163), but never obtains or checks which ids the confirmer scored — the HeldOutConfirmer protocol (discovery.py:42-45) returns only a Verdict, no evaluated-ids. The pipeline CONFIRM block (pipeline.py:558-584) is identical: it calls plan.confirm_held_out(h, held_out), validates Verdict type (566-570) and hypothesis-id attribution (571-575), but never calls assert_confirm_only. So Firewall-B's per-id leak check exists on confirm_unit_level and is absent on both runners — the asymmetry is real and as described. The PoC reproduces: a confirmer that returns a verdict without honestly scoring only held-out ids is caught by confirm_unit_level (which is handed evaluated_ids) but not by either runner (which never asks the confirmer which ids it scored).

I downgrade severity, however. The runners enforce the separation STRUCTURALLY by handing the confirmer only the held-out sub-corpus (discovery.py:119, pipeline.py:562) — the confirmer is never given the discovery partition or the full corpus, so it cannot ACCIDENTALLY double-dip. The finding's specific exploit ("a confirmer that internally pulls in discovery-partition units") requires the confirmer to willfully reach for data outside its handed corpus, which the discovery.py module docstring (lines 22-24) already explicitly disclaims as out of scope: "this is a *signature-level* guarantee — it prevents accidental double-dipping, not a step that willfully reflects into the caller's frame (only process isolation could)." Thus the genuine, novel issue is the inconsistency between confirm_unit_level offering a stronger evaluated-ids check that the runners don't replicate (and don't even document the omission of) — a defense-in-depth and documentation gap, not an exploitable firewall bypass. The suggested fix's own minimum bar is "document the asymmetry," consistent with a low-severity hardening item.

b.why:
The factual core of the finding is verified true in current code. `DiscoverConfirmSplit.assert_confirm_only` (firewalls.py:126-145) is the runtime double-dip leak check. Grep across src/ shows it has exactly ONE call site: `confirm_unit_level` (confirm.py:188). Neither composing runner calls it: `discover_and_confirm` confirms via `confirm(hypothesis, held_out)` at discovery.py:150 and the pipeline's DISCOVERY/CONFIRM block confirms via `plan.confirm_held_out(h, held_out)` at pipeline.py:562 — both only guard non-empty partitions (discovery.py:100-126; pipeline.py:504-527) and never check which ids the confirmer scored. The `HeldOutConfirmer` Protocol returns only a `Verdict` (discovery.py:42-45), so the runners structurally cannot learn the evaluated id set. Tests confirm the asymmetry: assert_confirm_only/evaluated_ids are exercised only for the standalone primitive (test_firewalls.py:40-64, test_confirm.py:535-616), with no runner-level leak-check coverage. The PoC reproduces as described: confirm_unit_level raises on a lying confirmer's discovery ids, while the same callable through discover_and_confirm/pipeline completes cleanly.

Severity is LOW, not high, for two concrete reasons: (1) Both runners hand the confirmer a structurally-subset corpus — `held_out = subset_corpus(corpus, split.confirm_ids)` (discovery.py:119, pipeline.py:500) — which contains only held-out units (and relations with both endpoints held-out, discovery.py:63-66). So ACCIDENTAL double-dipping by a normally-written confirmer operating on its argument is already prevented structurally; the PoC requires a confirmer that willfully ignores its handed corpus and reaches discovery data from a captured closure. (2) Even the "protected" primitive's check is weak in the same way: confirm_unit_level's `evaluated_ids` is caller-DECLARED, not derived from the actual computation, so a willful confirmer can declare held-out ids while internally scoring discovery units and assert_confirm_only still passes. The module docstring explicitly scopes this (discovery.py:23-24): "signature-level guarantee — it prevents accidental double-dipping, not a step that willfully reflects into the caller's frame (only process isolation could)." So the finding identifies a genuine consistency/defense-in-depth asymmetry worth closing (the suggested fix of having HeldOutConfirmer return evaluated ids, or at minimum documenting the delegation, is legitimate), but it is not a critical/high production breach — it neither enables accidental leakage nor is the comparison primitive a real barrier against the same willful actor.

confirmed.title: Persisted provenance records carry no key/algorithm marker — a keyed (HMAC) trail is indistinguishable from an unkeyed one on re-verify
confirmed.description: entries_to_records()/to_records() serialize seq/kind/summary/payload/timestamp/prev_hash/entry_hash but record nothing about whether the chain was sealed with SHA-256 (unkeyed) or HMAC-SHA256 (keyed). from_records()/verify_records() require the caller to supply the correct secret out-of-band; omitting it on a keyed trail fails with the generic 'content was altered, or the wrong key was used' error, conflating a missing-key config with genuine tampering. Conversely there is no recorded signal that a trail SHOULD have been keyed, so a downstream consumer cannot detect that an audit-grade trail was persisted unkeyed. Weakens the provenance-completeness claim for persisted trails.
confirmed.poc: trail = ProvenanceTrail(secret=b'x'*16); trail.record('a','b'); recs = trail.to_records(); from_records(recs) -> raises ProvenanceError as if tampered, because no secret was passed and nothing in recs indicates one is required.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Emit a non-authenticated-but-informative envelope alongside the records (e.g. {'algo':'hmac-sha256'|'sha256','keyed':bool}) so a verifier can fail with a precise 'this trail is keyed; supply the secret' message and a consumer can detect an unkeyed audit trail. Do not include the secret. Optionally fold an algorithm tag into the hashed preimage so it is itself integrity-protected.

a.why:
Verified against current code at src/assay_engine/provenance.py. entries_to_records() (lines 219-233) serializes exactly seq/kind/summary/payload/timestamp/prev_hash/entry_hash and records NO key/algorithm marker. _digest() (lines 69-91) branches on secret being None (SHA-256) vs set (HMAC-SHA256) but the choice leaves no trace in the serialized record. verify_records() (lines 236-257) and from_records() (lines 260-288) require the caller to supply the secret out-of-band; on a keyed trail verified without the secret, the recomputed digest mismatches and raises the generic message at line 255 "content was altered, or the wrong key was used (hash mismatch)", conflating a missing-key config with genuine tampering. The PoC reproduces verbatim: ProvenanceTrail(secret=b'x'*16); record('a','b'); to_records() -> record keys are ['entry_hash','kind','payload','prev_hash','seq','summary','timestamp'] (no algo/keyed field); from_records(recs) raises ProvenanceError "provenance entry 0 content was altered, or the wrong key was used (hash mismatch)". So both claims hold: (1) no recorded signal that a trail is/should-be keyed, and (2) the diagnostic conflates two distinct failure modes.

However, this is a diagnostics/completeness gap, not an integrity or security defect. The module docstring (lines 16-28) explicitly documents that keyed verification requires supplying the same secret out-of-band as by-design (it is the source of tamper-resistance — an unauthenticated keyed marker would be attacker-flippable, which the finding itself acknowledges by suggesting it be folded into the hashed preimage). The integrity guarantees are intact; the genuine weaknesses are a conflated error message and a downstream consumer's inability to detect an unkeyed audit trail. That mildly weakens the provenance-completeness UX but is not high severity.

b.why:
Verified against current code at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/provenance.py. All factual claims hold and the PoC reproduces exactly.

1. entries_to_records() (lines 211-233) and to_records() (lines 206-208) serialize ONLY seq/kind/summary/payload/timestamp/prev_hash/entry_hash. Confirmed at runtime: record keys are exactly those seven; no algo/keyed/algorithm field is present.

2. verify_records() (line 252) and from_records() (lines 260-288) require the caller to supply the correct secret out-of-band; default secret=None.

3. PoC reproduced verbatim: ProvenanceTrail(secret=b'x'*16); trail.record('a','b'); from_records(trail.to_records()) raises ProvenanceError: "provenance entry 0 content was altered, or the wrong key was used (hash mismatch)" — line 255. This conflates a missing-key config error with genuine tampering.

4. Confirmed: nothing in the persisted records signals that a trail SHOULD have been keyed, so a downstream consumer cannot detect that an audit-grade trail was persisted unkeyed.

Genuineness: This is a real, reproducible diagnosability/completeness gap, not speculative. However it is NOT an integrity vulnerability — within each chosen tier the integrity guarantees hold (a keyed trail still verifies correctly when the secret is supplied; unkeyed tampering of the documented kind is still caught). The module docstring (lines 16-28) already documents the two tiers and that verify_records must be given the same secret, so the tier boundary is partly by-design. The genuine defect is (a) the error message conflating config-vs-tamper and (b) the inability of a consumer to detect an unkeyed audit trail — both diagnostic/completeness weaknesses against the 'audit-grade provenance' claim, with the suggested remedy being an informational (non-authenticating) envelope. confirmed.title: Operator-username PII not redacted from transcripts when the home-dir name is short (<4 chars) or appears in a dash-delimited project slug
confirmed.description: _scrub redacts the operator username two ways: the path rule '(/(?:Users|home)/)[^/\s"']+' (requires a literal '/Users/[REDACTED]') and _USERNAME_RE derived from Path.home().name. The Claude project-dir slug is dash-delimited (e.g. '-Users-[REDACTED]-hc-grc'); the path rule does NOT match it (no slashes), so the only mask inside slugs/git-author strings/prose is _USERNAME_RE — which returns None for any home-dir name shorter than 4 chars (to avoid over-redacting common words). For operators whose login is <4 chars, the username then leaks verbatim into committed transcripts via the slug and prose, defeating the stated #F-024/#G-007 PII-scrubbing goal. The docstring claims operator PII is redacted, so this is an undisclosed completeness gap.
confirmed.poc: Set HOME so Path.home().name='bob'; create $SRC=.../-Users-[REDACTED]-hc-grc/<id>.jsonl containing the slug path and 'bob'. Run capture_transcripts — the committed transcript still contains 'bob' and '-Users-[REDACTED]-...' because _USERNAME_RE is None (len<4) and the '/Users/' rule never matches the dash slug.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Always derive the slug form of the home path (replace os.sep with '-') and redact it explicitly regardless of username length; lower/remove the len<4 skip in favor of word-boundary-anchored token redaction. At minimum document that usernames <4 chars are NOT auto-redacted so reviewers scrub before publishing.

The only other username mask is the path rule at line 51, `re.compile(r"(/(?:Users|home)/)[^/\s\"']+")`, which requires slash-delimited `/Users/[REDACTED] and never matches the dash-delimited Claude project slug `-Users-[REDACTED]-...`, git-author strings, or prose. PoC: HOME set so Path.home().name='bob' (3 chars); a transcript containing `/Users/[REDACTED]/...`, slug `-Users-[REDACTED]-hc-grc`, author `bob`, and prose `the operator bob did this` was run through capture_transcripts.py. This contradicts the explicit claims in the docstring/comments (lines 11, 34-35 "operator PII... username embedded in filesystem paths"; lines 56-61 and 83-84 claiming the bare username is "redact[ed]... everywhere" via #G-007 "wherever it appears... project-dir slug"). Severity is medium rather than high: it requires a specific precondition (operator login name <4 chars — the campaign operator is '[REDACTED]', 11 chars, so not currently exposed), transcripts are not shipped in the wheel, and the README already carries a general public-release review caveat. But the gap is real, the in-code claim of universal username redaction is false for short handles, and the skip is silent/undocumented at the failure point.

Confirmed mechanics: (1) line 51 path rule `(/(?:Users|home)/)[^/\s"']+` requires literal slashes and cannot match the dash-delimited Claude project slug `-Users-[REDACTED]-hc-grc`; (2) line 67 `if not name or len(name) < 4: return None` disables `_USERNAME_RE` for any home-dir name shorter than 4 chars; (3) lines 85-86 only mask the bare username when `_USERNAME_RE is not None`. PoC run with HOME making `Path.home().name='bob'` (len 3) and a transcript containing the slug, a git-author field, and prose: output was `{"cwd":"/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-grc","slug":"-Users-[REDACTED]-hc-grc","author":"bob","prose":"committed by bob today"}` — the `/Users/[REDACTED]/` path was masked but the slug, author, and prose leaked 'bob' verbatim (3 hits). confirmed.title: QdrantVectorStore.query has no k validation and assumes every returned point carries a 'ref' payload (raw KeyError)
confirmed.description: QdrantVectorStore.query (line 110) is the public VectorStore.query implementation. (1) It does not validate k — a k<=0 is forwarded straight to the backend, unlike upsert() which validates len-equality and batch_size>=1. (2) It reads p.payload['ref'] for every returned point; any point NOT written by this store's upsert() (inserted by another process/tool, or a future schema change) has no 'ref' key and query() raises a bare KeyError rather than a typed store error. The class docstring promises ids round-trip via the 'ref' payload but never defends the read path against foreign points. Both are edge-case gaps at an otherwise-hardened public boundary.
confirmed.poc: A point upserted by an external writer lacks 'ref'; query() does [(p.payload['ref'], float(p.score)) for p in res.points] -> KeyError 'ref' escapes untyped. Separately store.query(vector, k=0) forwards k=0 with no guard.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Validate k at the top of query ('if k < 1: raise ValueError("k must be >= 1")') to mirror upsert, and read defensively: ref = (p.payload or {}).get('ref'); if ref is None: continue (or raise a typed store error) so a foreign point cannot crash query with a raw KeyError.

a.why: Verified against current code at src/assay_engine/persistence/vectorstore.py. Both sub-claims are factually accurate. (1) query (line 110-114) does NOT validate k: it forwards k directly as limit=k to query_points (line 112) with no guard, in clear asymmetry with upsert which validates len-equality (line 96-97) and batch_size>=1 (line 98-99). (2) query reads p.payload["ref"] via bare subscript on line 114 ([(p.payload["ref"], float(p.score)) for p in res.points]); any returned point lacking the "ref" key raises a bare, untyped KeyError. The class docstring (lines 53-59) promises ids round-trip via the payload but the read path has no defense for foreign points. No typed store-error class exists in the persistence package and there are no internal callers, so query is a genuine public boundary. The PoC reproduces: an externally-written point without "ref" -> KeyError escapes; k=0 forwards unguarded. Severity is low rather than higher because: the k=0 case does not actually crash (Qdrant limit=0 returns empty results — it is an inconsistency, not a fault), and the KeyError requires a foreign writer into a loopback-only, study-owned collection (an edge case, partly defensible-by-design since the store documents it owns the payload). It is a real but minor boundary-hardening gap at an otherwise-hardened seam, not a speculative or already-handled issue.

b.why:
Both claims verified against actual code at src/assay_engine/persistence/vectorstore.py.

(1) k validation gap: query (line 110-113) forwards k directly to limit=k with no guard. This is a genuine asymmetry: the sibling upsert (lines 96-99) validates len-equality and batch_size>=1, but query does not validate k. PoC store.query(vector, k=0) forwards k=0 to the backend unchecked — reproduces as described.

(2) raw KeyError on foreign payload: line 114 reads p.payload["ref"] via raw subscript for every returned point. The class docstring (lines 53-59) states ids round-trip via the 'ref' payload, but the read path is undefended. Any point lacking 'ref' (inserted by an external writer, a different tool, or after a schema change) makes query() raise a bare KeyError instead of a typed store error. PoC reproduces exactly: [(p.payload["ref"], ...) for p in res.points] -> KeyError 'ref'.

Both are factually present, reproduce mechanically, and are not already-handled or by-design — query simply lacks the defensive handling its sibling upsert has. This is a real production-hardening gap at a public VectorStore boundary.

Severity is low rather than high: the collection is loopback-only and study-owned, so a foreign writer with a payload missing 'ref' is an edge case rather than a routine path; and k<=0 is input-hygiene asymmetry with no security/correctness/data-loss consequence (limit<=0 is rejected/empty at the backend). The fix is the suggested one: guard k<1 at the top of query, and read defensively via (p.payload or {}).get("ref") with continue-or-typed-error.

confirmed.title: Import-time int() of ASSAY_VECTOR_HTTP_PORT (and tracing port) raises an opaque ValueError masquerading as an import failure
confirmed.description: VECTOR_HTTP_PORT = int(os.environ.get('ASSAY_VECTOR_HTTP_PORT', '6333')) executes at module import (vectorstore.py:18); observability/tracing.py does the same for ASSAY_TRACING_PORT (line 30). A non-numeric value raises ValueError during `import`, surfacing as an import-time crash with no context tying it to the offending env var — and for any module importing these transitively it can present as a confusing ImportError-adjacent failure. The codebase elsewhere re-reads and bounds numeric env config at point-of-use with clamping (seam.py, checkpoint.py), so this unbounded import-time parse is a config-error-contract inconsistency. Low because it only triggers on misconfiguration, but the failure mode is opaque.
confirmed.poc: ASSAY_VECTOR_HTTP_PORT=notaport python -c 'import assay_engine.persistence.vectorstore' -> ValueError: invalid literal for int() with base 10: 'notaport' at import, no env-var context.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Parse these ports lazily inside vector_store_url()/get_qdrant_client() and tracing_endpoint(), or wrap int() in a helper raising a clear configuration error naming the env var. Keeps import side-effect-free and gives an actionable message.

a.why: Confirmed in current code. vectorstore.py:18 executes VECTOR_HTTP_PORT = int(os.environ.get("ASSAY_VECTOR_HTTP_PORT", "6333")) at module import; observability/tracing.py:30 does the same for ASSAY_TRACING_PORT. Both PoCs reproduce verbatim with the venv interpreter: `ASSAY_VECTOR_HTTP_PORT=notaport .venv/bin/python -c 'import assay_engine.persistence.vectorstore'` raises `ValueError: invalid literal for int() with base 10: 'notaport'` at vectorstore.py:18, and the tracing variant raises the identical error at tracing.py:30. The error message names only the offending literal value, not the env var, so it is opaque for an operator. The config-error-contract inconsistency claim is also accurate: checkpoint.py:61, 73, 263-265 parse numeric env config at point-of-use with max()/min() clamping (e.g. max(0, int(os.environ.get("ASSAY_MIGRATION_LOCK_TIMEOUT_MS","30000")))), whereas these two ports are parsed unbounded at import time. Caveats that keep this low: it triggers only under operator misconfiguration (non-numeric value), the traceback does cite the exact file/line, and the "masquerading as ImportError" framing is slightly overstated — it is a plain ValueError raised during import, not an actual ImportError. Net: a genuine, low-severity operability/config-contract inconsistency, not a correctness or security defect.

b.why:
Verified in current code. vectorstore.py:18 `VECTOR_HTTP_PORT = int(os.environ.get("ASSAY_VECTOR_HTTP_PORT", "6333"))` and tracing.py:30 `TRACING_PORT = int(os.environ.get("ASSAY_TRACING_PORT", "6006"))` both parse ports unconditionally at module-import scope. PoC reproduces exactly with the project's venv interpreter (.venv/bin/python3.14): `ASSAY_VECTOR_HTTP_PORT=notaport` -> traceback terminating at vectorstore.py:18 with `ValueError: invalid literal for int() with base 10: 'notaport'`, and the tracing equivalent fails identically at tracing.py:30. The error fires during `import` and the message contains no reference to the offending env var.

The "contract inconsistency" claim is substantiated: every other numeric env parse in the codebase bounds the value at parse — seam.py:99-110 (`max(1, int(_env(...)))`, etc.) and checkpoint.py:61,263-265 (`max(0, int(os.environ.get(...)))`) — while these two are unbounded. More tellingly, determinism.py:140-146 (audit #G-021) documents and implements the exact convention this finding recommends: catching an opaque parse error and re-raising with the offending key named. The two port parses violate that established config-error contract. tracing.py's own module docstring (lines 4-11) promises the module "loads ... with neither [extra] present" and degrades to a no-op, which an import-time crash on a malformed port contradicts — so this is not by-design.

Not speculative, not already-handled, reproduces as described, present after 4 hardening passes. Severity low is correct: only triggers on operator misconfiguration, no security or data-integrity impact, trivially recoverable by fixing the env var; the only harm is an opaque/mislocated failure message.

confirmed.title: redact_creds docstring claims the primary connection string is "always scrubbed precisely"
confirmed.description: Verified: redact_creds documents 'the primary connection string is always scrubbed precisely' (line 140) via layer 1, an exact substring match text.replace(conn_str, sanitized) (line 143). psycopg/libpq error messages do not always echo the DSN verbatim: a password can be URL-decoded, re-quoted, partially rendered, or the host/port reformatted, so the exact-match replace can miss the credential and only the generic best-effort regex backstops catch it. 'always' promises a precision the exact-match layer cannot deliver for every backend rendering.
confirmed.poc: Construct a conn_str with a percent-encoded password (e.g. 'a%2Fb') so libpq decodes it before embedding the decoded form in an error string. text.replace(conn_str, ...) does not match the decoded substring; layer 1 misses it and only the regex backstops (best-effort) apply.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Change 'the primary connection string is always scrubbed precisely' to '...is scrubbed precisely when the backend echoes it verbatim; the URI-userinfo and DSN-password regex layers backstop other renderings (best-effort).'

a.why: Verified against current code. checkpoint.py:128-145: redact_creds layer 1 is text.replace(conn_str, _sanitize_conn_str(conn_str)) (line 143) — an exact substring match — and the docstring at line 140 claims "the primary connection string is always scrubbed precisely." This is an accurate description of an overstated guarantee. PoC reproduces: with conn_str 'postgresql://[REDACTED]@localhost:5432/db', if libpq URL-decodes %2F to '/' before echoing it ('...postgresql://[REDACTED]@localhost:5432/db'), the exact-match layer 1 does NOT match the decoded substring (confirmed by execution: "after layer1" shows the password still present). Only the best-effort regex backstop _CREDS_RE (layer 2, line 99/144) then strips it. So the "precise" layer 1 demonstrably misses a legitimate backend rendering, contradicting the word "always". The claim that layers 2-3 are merely for "secondary endpoints" is also inaccurate — they are doing the actual redaction work for the primary conn_str in this case. This is genuine: it is a real documentation-accuracy defect, not speculative (the decode behavior is standard libpq/URI handling) and not by-design (the docstring asserts a precision the implementation cannot deliver). However, it is low severity: it is a docstring overstatement, not a credential leak in realistic conditions — the regex backstops still scrub the common decoded-URI and keyword/value DSN renderings (verified layer 2 catches the URI case). The suggested fix (soften "always...precisely" to "...when the backend echoes it verbatim; ...regex layers backstop other renderings (best-effort)") correctly aligns the docstring with behavior. A residual prose-rendering case (e.g. password "a/b" without '=') would escape all layers, but libpq does not emit plaintext passwords in such prose, so this is not a practical leak.

b.why: Confirmed as a genuine docstring-overclaim issue in current code. At /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/persistence/checkpoint.py:140 the docstring asserts "the primary connection string is always scrubbed precisely," but the layer-1 implementation at line 143 is a literal exact-substring replacement: text.replace(conn_str, _sanitize_conn_str(conn_str)). Exact substring matching only succeeds when the backend echoes conn_str byte-for-byte. The PoC is sound: a DSN with a percent-encoded password (e.g. a%2Fb) is URL-decoded by libpq to a/b before use; if the error text embeds the decoded form (a/b) rather than the original encoded substring (a%2Fb), text.replace finds no match and layer 1 silently misses the credential. The same gap applies to any backend reformatting (re-quoting, host/port normalization, partial rendering). In those cases only the regex layers (_CREDS_RE at line 144, _DSN_PW_RE at line 145) catch it — and the docstring itself (lines 135-139) explicitly labels those as "best-effort." So "always ... precisely" promises a guarantee the exact-match layer cannot deliver for every backend rendering; the suggested rewording is accurate. Severity is low: this is a documentation accuracy/overclaim defect, not a runtime vulnerability — the multi-layer redaction still runs unconditionally (lines 144-145 always execute), so credentials are still attacked by the regex backstops even when layer 1 misses. The concrete harm is a misleading invariant that could lead a maintainer to over-trust layer 1; no credential is guaranteed to leak because the backstops remain in force.

confirmed.title: G-004 in-process unfreeze test is a probabilistic guard and its two assertions use the same set value (redundant coverage)
confirmed.description: test_unfreeze_frozenset_of_unorderable_types_is_deterministic (lines 43-48) asserts at the AMBIENT (unpinned) PYTHONHASHSEED. As a regression guard against a revert to raw set iteration it is probabilistic — raw frozenset iteration of {1,'a',2,'b'} happens to equal the stable order ~30% of seeds, so the buggy code would still pass ~1/3 of runs (the test's own comment at lines 52-53 admits this). Verified secondary defect: line 47 {1,'a',2,'b'} and line 48 {'b',2,'a',1} are the SAME set value with identical per-process iteration order, so line 48 adds zero coverage and gives a false impression of testing two independent orderings. NOTE: the real discriminating guard already exists — test_unfreeze_frozenset_order_is_seed_independent_subprocess (lines 51-69) pins PYTHONHASHSEED=2 in a subprocess and fails the buggy code every run — so this is a test-quality/redundancy issue, not a true coverage gap (downgraded from the reported high).
confirmed.poc: Across 200 PYTHONHASHSEEDs, list(frozenset({1,'a',2,'b'})) equals the stable [1,2,'a','b'] for ~60/200 seeds; on raw-iteration code the in-process assertion passes whenever the ambient seed lands there. And frozenset({1,'a',2,'b'}) == frozenset({'b',2,'a',1}), so both assertions exercise one ordering.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Treat the in-process assertion as documentation only (the subprocess test is the guard), and replace the duplicate {'b',2,'a',1} on line 48 with a genuinely different input (e.g. frozenset({1,'a',2.5,'b'})) so the two assertions cover distinct cases. Optionally parametrize the in-process check over several pinned-and-differing PYTHONHASHSEEDs run in subprocesses.

a.why: Verified both sub-claims in current code at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/tests/test_immutability.py. (1) Lines 47-48: frozenset({1,'a',2,'b'}) == frozenset({'b',2,'a',1}) returns True and list() of each gives the identical iteration order (confirmed empirically: both yield [1,'a',2,'b']). Line 48 therefore adds zero coverage over line 47 — it is the same set value, not a second independent ordering, contradicting the appearance of testing two orderings. (2) The in-process assertion runs at the ambient PYTHONHASHSEED and is probabilistic: across 200 seeds, raw frozenset iteration of {1,'a',2,'b'} equals the stable [1,2,'a','b'] for 60/200 (~30%) of seeds, so a revert to raw set iteration would still pass the in-process assertion ~1/3 of runs. The test's own comment (lines 52-56) admits this. (3) The finding's downgrade is also accurate: the real discriminating guard exists at lines 51-69 (test_unfreeze_frozenset_order_is_seed_independent_subprocess) which pins PYTHONHASHSEED=2 in a subprocess and asserts the stable output, failing buggy code every run. So this is a genuine but low-severity test-quality/redundancy defect, not a coverage gap, exactly as described.

b.why:
Both factual claims reproduce against current code at tests/test_immutability.py:47-48.

Secondary defect (the load-bearing claim): frozenset({1,'a',2,'b'}) (line 47) and frozenset({'b',2,'a',1}) (line 48) are the SAME set value. Verified: a==b is True, and list(a)==list(b) in-process (both yield [1,2,'b','a']). Within one interpreter process the hash seed is fixed, so both assertions exercise an identical iteration order — line 48 contributes zero additional coverage and falsely implies two independent orderings are tested.

Probabilistic claim: Across 200 PYTHONHASHSEEDs, raw list(frozenset({1,'a',2,'b'})) equals the stable [1,2,'a','b'] for 60/200 seeds (~30%), matching the PoC. So the in-process assertions (lines 47-48), as a regression guard against a revert to raw set iteration, would pass ~1/3 of runs even on buggy code.

Correctly downgraded: the genuine deterministic guard exists — test_unfreeze_frozenset_order_is_seed_independent_subprocess (lines 51-69) pins PYTHONHASHSEED=2 in a subprocess; I confirmed raw iteration at seed 2 yields [1,2,'b','a'] (differs from stable), so a revert to raw iteration fails that subprocess test every run. The unfreeze implementation (src/assay_engine/_frozen.py:153-161) is correct, falling back to a stable (type name, repr) sort key. Therefore this is a real test-quality/redundancy issue, not a coverage gap or production-readiness defect.

Severity: low — cosmetic/redundant test coverage; the actual invariant is correctly implemented and is guarded deterministically by the subprocess test.

rejected.title: Module-global reasoning ThreadPoolExecutor cannot self-heal if a worker future vanishes — saturation guard can wedge permanently
rejected.description: The reasoning seam uses one process-global ThreadPoolExecutor; _submit_bounded increments _inflight before submit and decrements via add_done_callback(_release). Accounting is guarded for KeyboardInterrupt/SystemExit and pool-shutdown, but there is no recovery if a worker thread dies abnormally without the future ever completing (a C-level backend crash that takes the worker but not the process, or pool-state corruption): the done-callback never fires, _inflight is never decremented, and after _POOL_WORKERS such events every subsequent call raises RateLimitError('pool saturated') for the rest of the process lifetime. future.result() timeouts are handled (slot intentionally held), but a vanished future is not. Latent availability/leak risk on the exception path; the checkpoint pool by contrast is self-healing.
rejected.poc: Monkeypatch _pool.submit to return a Future that never resolves; call _with_timeout _POOL_WORKERS times -> each times out but the futures never complete, so _release never runs; the next _submit_bounded raises RateLimitError unconditionally and stays that way forever.
rejected.suggested_fix: Add a watchdog/reap step: track each in-flight future with its submission time and, in _submit_bounded's saturation branch, force _release on futures that are done-but-unreleased or have exceeded (per-call timeout + grace) before refusing. Document that wait=False atexit may abandon in-flight backend calls.

a.why:
The mechanical observation is accurate: src/assay_engine/reasoning/seam.py:219-238 has no watchdog/reap step. _submit_bounded's saturation branch (lines 224-231) unconditionally raises RateLimitError without tracking in-flight futures or reaping any that are done-but-unreleased. _inflight is only ever decremented by _release, which is only invoked via add_done_callback (line 251), so a future that NEVER completes leaks its slot forever. The supplied PoC reproduces exactly: I replaced rc._pool.submit with a fake returning a bare concurrent.futures.Future(), called _with_timeout 8 (=_POOL_WORKERS) times, and every subsequent _submit_bounded raised RateLimitError("pool saturated") permanently with _inflight stuck at 8.

However, this is NOT a genuine production-readiness defect in current code, because the trigger condition is not achievable in the CPython runtime:

1. The PoC only produces a never-completing future by monkeypatching _pool.submit to return a bare Future. A real ThreadPoolExecutor cannot do this: CPython's _WorkItem.run() wraps the callable in try/except BaseException and ALWAYS sets the future's result or exception before the worker returns. Any Python-level return or raise (including KeyboardInterrupt/SystemExit) completes the future and fires the done-callback.

2. The described real triggers — "a C-level backend crash that takes the worker but not the process" — are not real failure modes for Python threads. Threads share one process/address space; a genuine C-extension segfault crashes the WHOLE process, not a single thread, so you never end up with a live interpreter serving requests while a counter leaks. "Pool-state corruption" is asserted with no concrete path.

3. The one realistic permanent-leak vector — a hung backend call — is already handled. The outer timeout intentionally holds the slot (lines 263-267, documented), and the inner bounds (anyio.fail_after at line 458 for HIGH_STAKES; the httpx.Timeout at lines 359-361 for BULK) guarantee the worker eventually returns, at which point the future completes and _release fires. I verified with the REAL pool: a callable that outlives the outer timeout but eventually returns leaves _inflight at 1 immediately after the timeout, then self-heals to 0 once the worker completes. So the genuinely reachable leak path self-recovers by design.

The finding itself concedes this is "Latent availability/leak risk" — i.e. a missing defensive belt-and-suspenders against a failure mode that cannot occur in CPython, not an exploitable/reachable availability bug. The cited "self-healing checkpoint pool" comparison (persistence/checkpoint.py) is a psycopg ConnectionPool with check_connection liveness on checkout — a different mechanism for a different (genuinely reachable) failure (DB disconnect), so it does not establish that the reasoning pool needs equivalent recovery for an unreachable failure. A reap step would be reasonable defensive hardening but does not address any reproducible problem in current code.

b.why:
The finding posits that a worker future can "vanish" (worker dies abnormally without the future ever completing), permanently leaking _inflight slots until the pool wedges with RateLimitError for the process lifetime. This cannot occur in CPython's threading model via the actual code path.

Verified against actual code at src/assay_engine/reasoning/seam.py:201-267:

1. The PoC only reproduces by monkeypatching _pool.submit to return a bare Future() that the executor never owns or runs (I confirmed it does wedge: inflight=8/8, next call raises RateLimitError). But this is artificial — the real _submit_bounded (line 234) ALWAYS calls _pool.submit, which returns an executor-managed future. CPython's concurrent.futures.thread._WorkItem.run wraps the task in `try: ... except BaseException: self.future.set_exception(exc)` (I inspected the source) — so the future is ALWAYS completed, for every Python-level outcome including SystemExit/KeyboardInterrupt raised inside the worker. I empirically confirmed: a worker that raises RuntimeError, and one that raises SystemExit, both leave future.done()==True and fire add_done_callback. Therefore _release always runs and the slot is always freed.

2. The only theoretical path to a non-completing real future is a C-level crash (segfault/abort) of the worker thread. The finding's premise — "takes the worker but not the process" — does not hold in CPython: all threads share one process; a segfault kills the ENTIRE process, so there is no surviving process left to wedge. There is no public CPython API to kill a single thread. The high-stakes backend runs as a SUBPROCESS (claude CLI via anyio.run) and the bulk backend is an httpx HTTP call — a crash in either returns an exception to the Python worker, which then completes the future normally; neither crashes the worker thread itself.

3. The genuinely realistic case — timeout while a worker is still running in a real backend call — is already designed and self-heals. I empirically verified: after a FuturesTimeout, inflight stays at 1 (slot intentionally held, documented at lines 264-266), and once the real worker returns the done-callback fires and inflight returns to 0. Releasing earlier would over-subscribe the pool, so holding the slot is correct, not a bug.

4. The finding's supporting comparison is also wrong: it claims "the checkpoint pool by contrast is self-healing," but src/assay_engine/persistence/checkpoint.py contains no inflight-counted ThreadPoolExecutor (grep shows only a comment mention of saturation), so there is no analogous self-healing pool to contrast against.

The accounting is already correct for every reachable failure mode (normal/BaseException in worker, timeout, pool-shutdown RuntimeError at line 235, and the interrupt-window fallback at line 252-253 with per-future idempotent _release at lines 201-216). The proposed watchdog would address a scenario that cannot arise in CPython. Speculative, not a true production-readiness defect in current code.

split.title: HIGH_STAKES tier lacks the output-length cap that BULK enforces (asymmetric runaway-generation guard)
split.description: Verified: BULK always sets num_predict=BULK_NUM_PREDICT (seam.py:99, 368) and its docstring calls this 'the real guard' because a wall-clock timeout cannot interrupt a call already blocked in generation. _high_stakes_complete (seam.py:414-503) builds ClaudeAgentOptions with NO max-output-tokens equivalent — grep for max_tokens in seam.py returns nothing. A runaway BULK generation is bounded by tokens AND wall-clock; a runaway HIGH_STAKES generation is bounded only by anyio.fail_after(HIGH_STAKES_TIMEOUT). The cheaper/safer bound was applied only to the local tier, and HIGH_STAKES is the one consuming the fixed-cost subscription budget. The asymmetry is the same the BULK docstring argues against.
split.poc: A BULK call cannot emit more than ASSAY_BULK_NUM_PREDICT (2048) tokens. The parallel HIGH_STAKES call has no analogous bound: a prompt eliciting a very long completion runs until HIGH_STAKES_TIMEOUT (300s default), consuming the full subscription turn and a bounded-pool worker slot.
split.suggested_fix: Add an ASSAY_HIGH_STAKES_MAX_TOKENS cap and pass it to ClaudeAgentOptions (or the SDK's max-output-tokens option), mirroring num_predict. If the Agent SDK cannot bound output, document the asymmetry explicitly in the HIGH_STAKES docstring the way BULK's is documented.

a.why:
The literal code observation is accurate: BULK always sets num_predict=BULK_NUM_PREDICT (seam.py:368, default 2048, documented "the real guard" at seam.py:96-98), while _high_stakes_complete (seam.py:414-503) builds ClaudeAgentOptions (seam.py:435-442) with no output-token cap — grep confirms max_tokens/max_output/num_predict appear nowhere in the high-stakes path, and the pinned SDK (claude-agent-sdk>=0.2.101,<0.3) has no max_tokens/max_output_tokens field. So a runaway HIGH_STAKES completion is bounded only by anyio.fail_after(HIGH_STAKES_TIMEOUT) (seam.py:458) and the outer _with_timeout (+30, seam.py:550).

But this is NOT a true production-readiness problem and the finding's premises are partly wrong:

1. The slot-leak harm the PoC invokes is already prevented. The BULK token cap is "the real guard" SPECIFICALLY because a ThreadPoolExecutor worker cannot be interrupted (seam.py:96-97, 264-266) — a hung sync call leaks its pool slot. HIGH_STAKES runs under anyio.fail_after which ACTUALLY CANCELS the subprocess and frees the bounded-pool worker (seam.py:454-459, explicitly documented). So HIGH_STAKES's slot protection is stronger, not weaker. The "same asymmetry the BULK docstring argues against" does not hold: the architectural reason BULK needs the token cap (uninterruptible worker) does not apply to HIGH_STAKES.

2. The economic harm is misstated. HIGH_STAKES is a fixed-cost OAuth subscription with every metered credential scrubbed (ADR-0003, seam.py:9-11). A long completion consumes a subscription turn, not a metered dollar budget; "consuming the full subscription turn" is the by-design cost of a single bounded call, not runaway spend.

3. The suggested fix's hedge ("if the SDK cannot bound output") is contradicted by the actual SDK: ClaudeAgentOptions DOES expose max_budget_usd (stops the query with error_max_budget_usd) and max_turns — directly addressing the cost concern. So an actionable bound exists today, meaning the finding's claim that the tier is "bounded only by wall-clock" is inaccurate (a cost cap is achievable) while simultaneously the absence of one is not a defect.

Net: a legitimate low-severity defense-in-depth opportunity (optionally wire ASSAY_HIGH_STAKES_MAX_BUDGET_USD or max_turns), but the described HIGH problem — unguarded runaway / leaked slot / budget burn parallel to BULK — does not exist in current code. Not confirmed.

b.why:
All grep-able factual claims are accurate in current code (src/assay_engine/reasoning/seam.py). BULK always sets num_predict=BULK_NUM_PREDICT (line 368) and its docstring (lines 95-98) calls bounding output tokens "the real guard." _high_stakes_complete (lines 414-503) builds ClaudeAgentOptions (lines 435-442) with no max-output-tokens field; there is no max_tokens anywhere in the file. HIGH_STAKES is bounded only by anyio.fail_after(HIGH_STAKES_TIMEOUT) (line 458) plus the outer _with_timeout (HIGH_STAKES_TIMEOUT + 30, line 550). The output-length cap asymmetry is therefore real.

However, the asserted SEVERITY (a genuine runaway-generation hazard mirroring what the BULK docstring argues against) does not reproduce, for two reasons grounded in the current code:

1. The motivation for the BULK cap does not transfer to HIGH_STAKES. BULK runs in a ThreadPoolExecutor worker that "cannot interrupt a thread already blocked in a backend call" (lines 264-266, 349-355), so its wall-clock timeout leaks the pool slot — that uninterruptibility is exactly why num_predict is needed as "the real guard." By contrast, HIGH_STAKES's anyio.fail_after is explicitly documented (lines 451-456, #127) to genuinely CANCEL: "the cancellation propagates into query() and tears down the child," freeing the bounded-pool worker before the outer wall-clock. So the slot-leak harm that motivates num_predict is already mitigated for HIGH_STAKES by a mechanism BULK lacks; the wall-clock IS an effective interruption here.

2. The cost-runaway angle is moot under the fixed-cost model. The module (lines 9-11) and docstrings stress HIGH_STAKES is a fixed-cost subscription with every metered credential scrubbed. A very long completion does not cost more money; it costs latency, hard-bounded at HIGH_STAKES_TIMEOUT (300s default). "Consuming the full subscription turn" is a latency/availability concern already bounded by the existing timeout, not unbounded cost.

So neither motivating harm (leaked worker slot, runaway metered cost) genuinely materializes. What remains is a real but minor consistency/documentation gap: the BULK docstring documents its inner bound while the HIGH_STAKES docstring does not document why it relies solely on the (cancelling) timeout. The finding is not a false positive — the code asymmetry literally exists and the suggested-fix's documentation alternative is reasonable — but it is a low-severity hardening/doc nit, not a HIGH production-readiness defect.

split.title: discover_and_confirm lacks the on_step provenance hook that adjudicate_with_baseline has (asymmetric audit-trail wiring)
split.description: The two parallel firewall runners (discovery's own docstring calls itself 'the mirror of adjudication') were hardened asymmetrically for per-step provenance. adjudicate_with_baseline takes on_step: Callable[[Hypothesis, Verdict], None] | None (adjudication.py:193, 264-267) so a caller can record each pre-registered hypothesis+verdict to the hash-chained trail as each happens (#93), preserving GOVERNANCE §3's 'recorded before the next step runs' ordering. discover_and_confirm (discovery.py:69-165) has no such hook: a standalone discovery study can only batch-record after the whole loop returns, losing the append-before-next-step ordering its twin enforces.
split.poc: adjudicate_with_baseline(..., on_step=lambda h,v: trail.record(...)) lands each verdict in confirmation order. discover_and_confirm(corpus, split, discover=..., confirm=..., authority=...) accepts no on_step, so a standalone study records verdicts only after the loop returns or by re-implementing the loop.
split.suggested_fix: Add an `on_step: Callable[[Hypothesis, Verdict], None] | None = None` parameter to discover_and_confirm and invoke it after each verdict is validated (right after the misattribution check at discovery.py:164), mirroring adjudication.py:264-267.

a.why:
Factual claims verified: adjudicate_with_baseline has on_step (adjudication.py:193, 264-267); discover_and_confirm does not (discovery.py:69-76). However this is NOT a genuine production-readiness problem.

Key disqualifier: the production composition layer (run_study in pipeline.py) does NOT call discover_and_confirm at all. It reimplements the discovery/preregister/confirm loop inline (pipeline.py:502-584) and records per-step provenance there via trail.record(...) inside the CONFIRM loop (pipeline.py:577-583), preserving GOVERNANCE §3 'recorded before the next step' ordering. The on_step hook on adjudicate_with_baseline exists solely because the pipeline DOES call that function (pipeline.py:660-668, hook #93); discovery never needed the hook because the pipeline owns that loop directly.

The hash-chained provenance trail is a pipeline-level concept. Neither standalone runner (adjudicate, discover_and_confirm) touches a trail — both just return results. The claimed 'asymmetry' between the two standalone entry points does not actually exist: standalone adjudicate() calls adjudicate_with_baseline WITHOUT on_step (adjudication.py:172-180), so a standalone adjudication caller has no per-step trail hook either. So discover_and_confirm is no worse off than its true twin (standalone adjudicate); both standalone paths lack a wired trail by design, because trail provenance is enforced in the pipeline.

The finding's PoC is technically accurate (discover_and_confirm accepts no on_step) but the consequence is mischaracterized: discover_and_confirm returns verdicts in confirmation order, so a standalone caller can record them in order from the returned list; nothing about append-before-next-step ordering is lost for any production path. No correctness, data-integrity, security, or reliability defect results. At most this is a low-value API-symmetry/ergonomics nicety, not a production-readiness defect — the suggested fix is a reasonable enhancement but its absence is by-design and already-handled for the production (pipeline) path.

b.why:
All factual claims verified against current code (4 passes applied).

1. adjudicate_with_baseline has on_step: Callable[[Hypothesis, Verdict], None] | None = None at adjudication.py:193, invoked per-step at 264-267 right after the misattribution check and verdicts.append, with a comment explicitly stating its purpose is "so a caller can record the pre-registered hypothesis AND its verdict to provenance as each happens, not in a post-hoc batch (#93)."

2. discover_and_confirm (discovery.py:69-165) has NO on_step parameter. Its signature is (corpus, split, *, discover, confirm, authority) -> list[Verdict]. The loop body (133-164) validates origin, pre-registration, verdict type, and hypothesis<->verdict attribution, then appends to verdicts — but offers no per-step callback. A standalone caller can therefore only batch-record after the function returns, losing the append-before-next-step ordering its twin enforces.

3. The asymmetry is real and consequential to the design's own stated intent: both modules' docstrings frame discovery as "the mirror of adjudication" (discovery.py:3). Critically, the pipeline (src/assay_engine/pipeline.py) does NOT call discover_and_confirm at all — it re-implements the discovery/preregister/confirm loop inline (pipeline.py:524-583) precisely so it can trail.record per step (538-544, 577-583), while it DOES reuse adjudicate_with_baseline with on_step=_record_adjudication_step (pipeline.py:641-668). This is direct evidence that per-step provenance is the intended pattern and that discover_and_confirm is the odd one out: the only public runner that cannot drive a hash-chained trail in confirmation order without re-implementation.

4. Not speculative, not by-design, not already-handled: there is no alternative per-step hook on discover_and_confirm, and the parallel "mirror" design philosophy argues against the omission being intentional. The suggested fix (add on_step and invoke it after discovery.py:164, mirroring adjudication.py:264-267) is correct and minimal.

Severity: this is an API symmetry / audit-trail ergonomics gap in a public helper, not a correctness, data-integrity, or firewall-bypass defect. The production pipeline already records discovery provenance per-step (via its inline loop), so GOVERNANCE §3 ordering is preserved where it actually runs; a standalone discover_and_confirm caller retains a workaround (batch-record post-return, or re-implement the loop as the pipeline does). Genuine and worth fixing for consistency, but low impact.

split.title: No concurrency group — superseded PR pushes keep running and audit/lockfile-compile can race
split.description: Verified: ci.yml has no concurrency block, so every push to an open PR spawns the full matrix without cancelling the prior in-flight run. Beyond wasted minutes, two overlapping audit runs on the same branch both run lockfile resolution / 'pip install -e .' and upload identically-named SBOM/license artifacts that can race. On a single dedicated compute node this compounds contention with the reasoning/checkpoint pools.
split.poc: grep -n concurrency .github/workflows/ci.yml -> no match. Push twice in quick succession to a PR; both runs execute to completion.
split.suggested_fix: Add a top-level concurrency: { group: ${{ github.workflow }}-${{ github.ref }}, cancel-in-progress: true } so a newer commit cancels the stale run and only one audit/lockfile-compile executes per ref.

a.why:
Verified against current code at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/.github/workflows/ci.yml (the only workflow file). The PoC reproduces exactly: `grep -ni concurrency .github/workflows/ci.yml` returns no match, and recursive grep over .github/ finds no concurrency block anywhere. The trigger is `on: pull_request` (line 6) with no type filter and `push` to main (lines 3-5), and there is no `cancel-in-progress`, so every new commit on an open PR spawns the full matrix (core x4 python versions, integration, audit, all-checks) while the prior in-flight run continues to completion. On the documented single dedicated compute node (per MEMORY context: one Mac-mini production node sharing reasoning/checkpoint pools), this is a genuine resource-contention / wasted-minutes problem. The suggested fix (top-level concurrency group keyed on github.workflow + github.ref with cancel-in-progress: true) is correct and standard.

Caveat lowering severity: the finding's secondary rationale about an artifact/lockfile RACE is technically inaccurate. The audit job uploads `sbom-cyclonedx` (ci.yml:154-160) and `license-report` (ci.yml:173-179); GitHub scopes uploaded artifacts per run ID, so concurrent runs on the same ref do not overwrite/corrupt each other's artifacts. Likewise `pip install -e .` (lines 29, 74, 169) runs in isolated ephemeral runner VMs, so there is no shared-state lockfile race between runs. The real, confirmed harm is duplicate concurrent execution wasting CI capacity on a single node — an efficiency/hardening concern, not a security or correctness bug.

b.why:
The literal grep claim is true: .github/workflows/ci.yml (the only workflow file, confirmed via ls and grep across .github/) has no `concurrency` block, so a newer push to an open PR does not cancel the prior in-flight run (ci.yml:1-7 trigger on push/pull_request with no concurrency key). So superseded runs do keep executing.

However, the finding's severity-justifying claims — the parts that elevate it above trivial CI hygiene — are false:

1. "two overlapping audit runs ... upload identically-named SBOM/license artifacts that can race": GitHub Actions artifacts are scoped per workflow RUN (distinct run IDs), not per ref. Two concurrent runs have separate artifact namespaces, so the fixed names (sbom-cyclonedx ci.yml:158, license-report ci.yml:177) cannot collide or corrupt each other across runs. No race.

2. "both run lockfile resolution / pip install -e . and can race": every job uses runs-on: ubuntu-latest (ci.yml:12,45,90,188) — ephemeral, fully isolated GitHub-hosted VMs per run. Concurrent runs share no filesystem or state, so the uv pip compile (ci.yml:108-116) and pip install -e . (ci.yml:29,74,169) cannot race.

3. "compounds contention with the reasoning/checkpoint pools on a single dedicated compute node": CI executes on GitHub-hosted runners, not the dedicated Mac mini production node. There is no resource contention with local pools.

Stripped of the false race/contention claims, the only genuine residual is wasted CI minutes on superseded pushes — a best-practice efficiency improvement, not a production-readiness defect or any kind of correctness/security problem. Adding cancel-in-progress is a reasonable nice-to-have, but the finding as DESCRIBED (race conditions, node contention) does not reproduce and is not a true hardening defect. Marking not confirmed / false_positive on the substantive (non-cosmetic) claims.

split.title: pip-audit / SBOM each re-fetch the advisory DB and re-resolve — a network blip fails the 'reproducible' audit gate non-reproducibly
split.description: The audit job invokes pip-audit multiple times (gate at line 131, SBOM generation at line 151) plus a license-gate install, each reaching the OSV vuln DB / PyPI at run time. The stated goal is a reproducible supply-chain gate, but the result still depends on live availability of those services: a transient OSV/PyPI outage fails a commit that passed yesterday — the same non-reproducibility class the version pins were meant to remove. There is no retry, no vuln-DB caching, and the SBOM step re-runs the whole audit instead of reusing the gate's result.
split.poc: The gate step (line 131) and SBOM step (line 151) both run pip-audit against requirements.lock, each performing its own vulnerability-service lookup; no --cache-dir reuse or retry wrapper. An OSV/PyPI 5xx during any fails the required audit job.
split.suggested_fix: Run pip-audit once with --cache-dir (or a pinned/cached vuln DB), emit both the pass/fail and the CycloneDX SBOM from that single invocation, and wrap network steps in a bounded retry — halving PyPI/OSV calls and removing the duplicate live resolution from the reproducible gate.

a.why: Verified against /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/.github/workflows/ci.yml. The cited mechanics are accurate: the audit job runs pip-audit twice against requirements.lock — the gate at line 131 (pip-audit --strict --require-hashes -r requirements.lock) and the SBOM step at line 151 (pip-audit -r requirements.lock --format cyclonedx-json -o sbom.cyclonedx.json) — each performing its own independent OSV/PyPI vulnerability lookup. The license gate at line 168 (pip install --require-hashes -r requirements.lock) adds a third live PyPI fetch. There is no --cache-dir reuse, no pinned/cached vuln DB, and no retry wrapper anywhere in the job. The job's own comments (lines 97-101, #G-022) state the explicit goal is reproducibility ("a green commit could fail tomorrow on a tooling bump"), yet runtime dependence on live OSV/PyPI availability reintroduces exactly that non-determinism for a different reason: a transient OSV/PyPI 5xx fails a commit that passed yesterday. The PoC is correct and reproduces by design — any network blip during these required steps fails the audit job. The duplicate audit invocation is also genuinely redundant (the SBOM step re-resolves the same set the gate already audited). Not speculative, not already-handled, not by-design.\n\nSeverity, however, is LOW. This is a CI-robustness/flakiness and minor-waste concern, not a security vulnerability or a production-runtime defect. It does not affect the shipped artifact or runtime behavior; worst case is occasional spurious red CI on transient upstream-service outages plus one redundant pip-audit call. The suggested fix (single invocation emitting both pass/fail and SBOM, --cache-dir reuse, bounded retry on network steps) is a reasonable hardening improvement but addresses reliability, not a critical/high production-readiness gap.

b.why:
The literal code facts are accurate: pip-audit runs twice against requirements.lock — the gate at .github/workflows/ci.yml:131 (`pip-audit --strict --require-hashes --progress-spinner off -r requirements.lock`) and the SBOM at :151 (`pip-audit ... --format cyclonedx-json -o sbom.cyclonedx.json`), each performing its own live OSV/PyPI lookup, with no `--cache-dir` and no retry wrapper (confirmed via grep — no cache/retry anywhere in the file). However, this is not a true production-readiness problem; it is a mischaracterized CI-quality/optimization suggestion.

Reasons it is a false positive:
1. Conflated reproducibility classes. The version pins (#F-031) make *dependency resolution* deterministic — the same lockfile installs the same set. That IS reproducible (and separately gated by the lockfile-sync step at :102-116). A vulnerability scan against the OSV advisory DB is, by design, time- and network-dependent: a newly disclosed CVE SHOULD fail a commit that passed yesterday. That is the entire purpose of vuln scanning, not a reproducibility regression. The achievable reproducibility (pinned tool version pip-audit==2.10.1 at :101, pinned audited set via requirements.lock) is already implemented. The finding's central premise — that this re-introduces 'the same non-reproducibility class the version pins were meant to remove' — is incorrect.
2. The transient-outage failure mode is generic CI flakiness common to every network-dependent step, remedied by a re-run; it is not broken, insecure, or incorrect behavior. No production behavior is affected — this job only runs in CI.
3. The duplicate invocation is intentional: the second run produces a different artifact (CycloneDX SBOM), and the comments at :135-153 document the deliberate coupling. Adding --cache-dir/retry or merging the two calls is a reasonable enhancement but its absence is not a defect — gate correctness and security are unaffected.

The suggested fix is a legitimate optimization, but the finding as framed (a reproducibility defect / production-readiness problem) does not hold.

all.title: alpha and stability_threshold are post-hoc free parameters, not pre-registered — threshold HARKing survives the firewall
all.description: The pre-registration digest (canonical_hypothesis_digest, preregistration.py:114-118) binds statement/kind/origin/test_name/decision_rule/source_claim_id/predicted_direction/params. Verified: `alpha` and `stability_threshold` are NOT fields on Hypothesis (hypothesis.py:61-74) and are NOT in the digest payload. They are supplied as plain runtime arguments to verdict_from_pvalue/confirm_whole_corpus/confirm_unit_level at confirmation time, and verdict_from_pvalue even CONSTRUCTS the recorded decision_rule string from the runtime alpha (confirm.py:131 `rule = f"...alpha={alpha}..."`) instead of comparing it against the locked decision_rule. Nothing reads hypothesis.decision_rule/params to constrain the confirm-time threshold. An analyst who has seen the null/baseline can therefore choose alpha=0.05 vs 0.10 or stability_threshold=0.9 vs 0.6 to flip indeterminate↔supported — exactly the post-hoc-threshold HARKing the firewall claims to prevent. The most decision-bearing numbers are not committed before confirmation, defeating the core methodological guarantee.
all.poc: Lock a WHOLE_CORPUS hypothesis with predicted_direction='greater' (alpha not a field, so the threshold is unlocked). After seeing the null: confirm_whole_corpus(h, observed=X, null_distribution=null, alpha=0.05, resample_statistics=R, stability_threshold=0.9, authority=auth) -> indeterminate ('does not beat null at alpha'). Re-run the SAME locked hypothesis (require_preregistered still passes — content unchanged) with alpha=0.10, stability_threshold=0.6 -> supported. Verdict changed purely by post-hoc threshold choice. Same applies to confirm_unit_level's alpha.
all.suggested_fix: Add `alpha` (and optional `stability_threshold`) as typed fields on Hypothesis, include them in canonical_hypothesis_digest, and have the confirmers READ them from the locked hypothesis instead of accepting caller arguments — or, if kept as arguments, require they equal the locked values and raise PreRegistrationError on mismatch (mirroring _resolve_direction's cross-check). Build the recorded decision_rule from the locked value, not the runtime arg.

a.why:
CONFIRMED in current code (4 passes applied). The finding is accurate on every checked point and the PoC reproduces empirically.

Code verification:
- `alpha` and `stability_threshold` are NOT fields on Hypothesis. The dataclass fields are hypothesis_id, statement, kind, origin, test_name, decision_rule, source_claim_id, locked_at, timestamp_proof, predicted_direction, params (hypothesis.py:61-74). No alpha, no stability_threshold.
- They are NOT in the pre-registration digest. canonical_hypothesis_digest payload binds only hypothesis_id/statement/kind/origin/test_name/decision_rule/source_claim_id/predicted_direction/params (preregistration.py:109-119). Neither threshold is covered by the cryptographic content binding.
- They are supplied as plain runtime args at confirm time: confirm_whole_corpus(..., alpha, stability_threshold=0.9, ...) (confirm.py:297,299), confirm_unit_level(..., alpha, ...) (confirm.py:153), verdict_from_pvalue(..., alpha, ...) (confirm.py:106).
- The recorded decision_rule is CONSTRUCTED from the runtime alpha/threshold rather than read from / checked against the locked hypothesis: confirm.py:131 (`rule = f"{test_description} at alpha={alpha}..."`) and confirm.py:356-359 (`f"...at alpha={alpha}...stability across resamples >= {stability_threshold}..."`). Nothing reads hypothesis.decision_rule or hypothesis.params to constrain the confirm-time thresholds. This contrasts with _resolve_direction (confirm.py:254-289) which DOES enforce the locked predicted_direction and raises on mismatch/None — proving the codebase knows how to bind a decision-bearing parameter but does not do so for the thresholds.

Empirical PoC (executed against the repo via .venv/bin/python): a WHOLE_CORPUS hypothesis locked with predicted_direction='greater' and verified with a real LocalHmacAuthority. With observed=3.0, a 500-sample null, and 600 resamples: confirm_whole_corpus(..., alpha=0.05, stability_threshold=0.9) -> INDETERMINATE ('unstable across resamples', stability=0.603); the SAME locked+verified hypothesis with stability_threshold=0.6 -> SUPPORTED. p_support unchanged at 0.0020. require_preregistered still passes in both runs because content is identical. Verdict flipped indeterminate->supported purely by a post-hoc threshold choice — exactly the threshold-HARKing the firewall claims to prevent.

Genuineness: not speculative, not by-design, not already-handled. The module's own docstrings (e.g. #G-001 on _resolve_direction) state that decision-bearing parameters must be committed before confirmation to prevent HARKing; alpha and stability_threshold are the most decision-bearing numbers and are left unbound, so this is a real gap in the stated methodological guarantee, present in current code.

Severity high (not critical): exploitation requires analyst access to the confirmer call site and the verdict only flips in the borderline zone; it does not corrupt the cryptographic lock itself or enable remote/unauthenticated compromise. But it defeats the core anti-HARKing guarantee the firewall advertises, so it is a true production-readiness defect. The suggested fix (add typed alpha/stability_threshold fields to Hypothesis, include them in canonical_hypothesis_digest, and either read them from the locked hypothesis or require equality and raise PreRegistrationError on mismatch, mirroring _resolve_direction) is correct and appropriate.

b.why:
Confirmed in current code and the PoC reproduces against the actual engine.

Evidence:
1. Hypothesis (src/assay_engine/methodology/hypothesis.py:61-74) has NO alpha or stability_threshold field.
2. canonical_hypothesis_digest (preregistration.py:109-119) hashes statement/kind/origin/test_name/decision_rule/source_claim_id/predicted_direction/params/hypothesis_id only — neither threshold is bound by the pre-registration proof.
3. alpha and stability_threshold are accepted as plain runtime arguments at confirm time: confirm_whole_corpus (confirm.py:297, 299), confirm_unit_level (confirm.py:153), verdict_from_pvalue (confirm.py:106). Nothing reads hypothesis.decision_rule or hypothesis.params to constrain them.
4. The recorded decision_rule is CONSTRUCTED from the runtime alpha rather than compared to the locked rule: confirm.py:131 (verdict_from_pvalue) and confirm.py:356-359 (confirm_whole_corpus). So the audit record reflects the post-hoc threshold, not a committed one.
5. This is in stark, deliberate contrast to _resolve_direction (confirm.py:254-289), which reads the LOCKED predicted_direction and rejects any contradicting confirm-time argument (#G-001, pass 4) — proving the authors hardened exactly this HARKing class for direction but left the two most decision-bearing thresholds unguarded.

PoC executed against the real engine (.venv/bin/python, Python 3.14): a single WHOLE_CORPUS hypothesis locked via lock_hypothesis with predicted_direction='greater', null=range(100), observed=92.0, resamples=[95]*7+[50]*3.
- confirm_whole_corpus(..., alpha=0.05, stability_threshold=0.9) -> INDETERMINATE ('unstable across resamples')
- confirm_whole_corpus(..., alpha=0.10, stability_threshold=0.6) -> SUPPORTED
Same locked hypothesis, identical timestamp_proof, both pass require_preregistered. Verdict flipped from indeterminate to supported purely by post-hoc threshold selection after the null/baseline is visible — precisely the threshold-HARKing the pre-registration firewall claims to prevent.

Not speculative, not by-design (the module docstrings and #G-001 fix establish that committing decision-bearing numbers before confirmation IS the intended guarantee), and not already-handled. High rather than critical because exploitation requires an analyst with confirm-time control (an internal/methodological integrity threat, not a remote attacker) and the lock itself is otherwise sound; but it defeats the core methodological guarantee of the system, so it is a true production-readiness problem.

all.title: `powered` is a trusted, unbound caller flag that gates indeterminate↔decisive after the data is seen
all.description: verdict_from_pvalue takes `powered: bool` from the caller and routes `not powered -> indeterminate` (confirm.py:137-138). Verified: `powered` is neither a Hypothesis field nor part of the digest, and the engine performs no power calculation. A confirmer thus decides AFTER seeing the held-out result whether the test was 'powered enough' to be decisive. Combined with H-001 this is a second post-hoc lever: a significant-but-against-the-claim result can be neutralized to indeterminate with powered=False, or a favorable result promoted with powered=True. The docstring frames powered as a method-capability fact but nothing binds it to a pre-stated power/sample-size criterion.
all.poc: For one locked unit-level hypothesis with a fixed held-out statistic/p_value: confirm_unit_level(..., powered=True) -> supported/contradicted; call again with powered=False -> indeterminate. Both pass the identical pre-registration check; decisiveness chosen post-hoc.
all.suggested_fix: Require the power/decisiveness criterion (e.g. minimum n, minimum detectable effect) to be pre-registered in params (bound in the digest) and have the engine derive `powered` from the held-out sample it evaluated against that locked criterion, rather than accepting an opaque boolean. At minimum record the locked criterion in evidence and assert the supplied `powered` is consistent with it.

a.why: Verified in current code. `verdict_from_pvalue` (src/assay_engine/methodology/confirm.py:101-143) takes `powered: bool` and routes `not powered -> Verdict.indeterminate(... notes="underpowered")` at confirm.py:137-138. `confirm_unit_level` (confirm.py:146-197) accepts `powered` as a parameter (confirm.py:154) and passes it through untouched (confirm.py:194). The flag is genuinely unbound and trusted: (1) `powered` is NOT a Hypothesis field (hypothesis.py:61-74 lists all fields; no power/sample-size criterion among them); (2) `powered` is NOT part of the cryptographic content digest (canonical_hypothesis_digest payload, preregistration.py:109-119, covers id/statement/kind/origin/test_name/decision_rule/source_claim_id/predicted_direction/params but never `powered`); (3) the engine performs no power calculation — `grep -rn powered src/` returns only the literal pass-through sites in confirm.py. The pre-registration gate `_gate_preregistration` (confirm.py:40-63) only verifies lock fields/proof, so the identical locked hypothesis with the identical held-out statistic/p_value passes the same gate regardless of `powered`. PoC reproduces exactly: confirm_unit_level(..., powered=True) with p_value<=alpha returns supported/contradicted (confirm.py:139-142); the same call with powered=False returns indeterminate (confirm.py:137-138). Decisiveness is therefore selected post-hoc by an opaque caller boolean after the held-out result is seen — a second post-hoc lever alongside direction_supports_claim, and structurally the same HARKing class the whole-corpus path was explicitly hardened against in _resolve_direction (#G-001, confirm.py:254-289). Not by-design in any documented sense: the docstring (confirm.py:113) frames `powered` as a method-capability fact ("the method cannot decide") but nothing binds it to a pre-stated criterion. Severity medium rather than high: exploitation requires a trusted in-process confirmer (same trust boundary as the already-accepted direction_supports_claim flag), it is not a remote/unauthenticated vector, and the supplied value is at least echoed into the recorded rule string (confirm.py:131) for partial auditability. The suggested fix (pre-register a power/decisiveness criterion in params so it is bound in the digest, and derive `powered` from the evaluated held-out sample against that locked criterion) is appropriate.

b.why:
Verified against current code (4 hardening passes applied). The finding is TRUE and the PoC reproduces exactly.

Evidence:
- `verdict_from_pvalue` takes `powered: bool` from the caller (src/assay_engine/methodology/confirm.py:107) and routes `not powered -> indeterminate` (confirm.py:137-138, notes="underpowered"). `confirm_unit_level` forwards the caller's `powered` unchanged (confirm.py:154,194).
- `powered` is NOT a Hypothesis field — confirmed via `Hypothesis.__dataclass_fields__` (src/assay_engine/methodology/hypothesis.py:61-74 lists every field; no `powered`).
- It is therefore NOT bound in the canonical digest: `canonical_hypothesis_digest` (src/assay_engine/methodology/preregistration.py:109-119) hashes only id/statement/kind/origin/test_name/decision_rule/source_claim_id/predicted_direction/params; `powered` is an out-of-band function argument.
- No power/sample-size calculation exists anywhere in the engine (grep for power/min_n/mde/sample_size finds only this boolean and the verdict docstring); the engine simply trusts the flag.

PoC reproduced live (.venv/bin/python): one locked UNIT_LEVEL hypothesis, fixed statistic=1.0, p_value=0.01, alpha=0.05, direction_supports_claim=True, evaluated on the held-out confirm partition. confirm_unit_level(..., powered=True) -> SUPPORTED; the identical call with powered=False -> INDETERMINATE (notes="underpowered"). Both pass the same pre-registration gate. Decisiveness is chosen post-hoc by an opaque caller boolean with no locked criterion behind it.

This is a genuine integrity weakness, not speculative or by-design-acceptable: the methodology's purpose is to prevent post-hoc manipulation of verdicts, and `powered` is a second post-hoc lever (parallel to the direction flag) on the decisive/indeterminate axis. Note `powered` is not even recorded in the `evidence` dict (confirm.py:135) — only in the free-text `rule` string (confirm.py:131) — so it is weakly auditable and not re-derivable from a locked criterion.

Severity high rather than critical: it requires a misbehaving/erroneous trusted confirmer (not an external attacker); `powered=False` can only suppress a result to indeterminate, never flip supported<->contradicted; and it does leave a trace in the rule string. The suggested fix (pre-register a power/decisiveness criterion in params so it enters the digest, and have the engine derive `powered` from the evaluated held-out sample against that locked criterion — or at minimum record the locked criterion in evidence and assert consistency) is appropriate.

all.title: Locked `test_name` is bound in the digest but never enforced against the test actually executed
all.description: Verified: test_name IS in canonical_hypothesis_digest (preregistration.py:114) but is read NOWHERE except when building the digest — no confirmer or runner checks that the executed test matches the pre-registered test_name. On the unit-level path this is most acute: confirm_unit_level receives only an opaque statistic/p_value/direction_supports_claim ('the engine cannot itself infer the tail', confirm.py:163-169) and verdict_from_pvalue records a hard-coded test_description='held-out unit-level test' (confirm.py:196) rather than the locked test_name. A study can pre-register test_name='mann_whitney_u', then after seeing held-out data run whichever test gives the desired p_value and feed it in; require_preregistered passes because content (including the now-meaningless test_name) is unchanged. The docstrings claim the locked content governs the verdict; for the test choice it does not.
all.poc: Lock a unit-level hypothesis with test_name='mann_whitney_u'. After seeing the held-out partition, compute p_value with a t-test that clears alpha and call confirm_unit_level(h, ..., p_value=that_p, direction_supports_claim=True, authority=auth) -> supported. require_preregistered raises nothing; the executed test silently differs from the locked one.
all.suggested_fix: Have the engine-side confirmer take the test identity it ran and assert it equals hypothesis.test_name (raise PreRegistrationError on mismatch), and propagate test_name into verdict_from_pvalue's test_description. Where the engine cannot see the test (study-owned confirmers), document explicitly that test-choice integrity is an un-enforced caller obligation so the docstrings stop over-claiming.

a.why:
Verified against current code (4 passes applied). All load-bearing claims hold:

1. test_name IS bound in the canonical digest: preregistration.py:114 includes "test_name": hypothesis.test_name in the SHA-256 payload, so it cannot change post-lock.
