2. test_name is read NOWHERE else. grep across src/ returns exactly two references: the field definition (hypothesis.py:65) and the digest payload (preregistration.py:114). No confirmer or runner compares the executed test against the locked test_name.

3. confirm_unit_level (confirm.py:146-197) accepts only opaque statistic/p_value/direction_supports_claim and never accepts a test-identity argument. It calls verdict_from_pvalue with a HARD-CODED test_description="held-out unit-level test" (confirm.py:196), discarding hypothesis.test_name. The docstring (confirm.py:118-125, 163-169) confirms statistic/p_value/direction are a "trusted caller obligation" and the engine "cannot itself infer the tail."

4. PoC reproduces: lock a UNIT_LEVEL hypothesis with test_name='mann_whitney_u', lock via LocalHmacAuthority, then on the held-out partition compute a clearing p_value with ANY test (e.g. a t-test) and call confirm_unit_level(..., p_value=that_p, direction_supports_claim=True, authority=auth). require_preregistered/verify_preregistration (preregistration.py:222-280) only verify the digest binds the UNCHANGED content (incl. the now-meaningless test_name) and that lock precedes confirmation — neither checks the executed test. Verdict.supported is returned; the executed test silently differs from the locked one.

Genuineness: This is a real preregistration-integrity gap, not speculative or already-handled. The digest binds test_name but nothing enforces it, so for the test-choice dimension the lock is decorative — matching the finding's "docstrings over-claim that locked content governs the verdict." Mitigating context (why high, not critical): (a) the unit-level direction/statistic trust model is explicitly documented as a caller obligation because study-owned confirmers run outside engine visibility; (b) exploitation requires a study to deviate from its own preregistration (insider/HARKing), not an external attack. The genuinely fixable parts — confirm_unit_level not taking/asserting the executed test identity, and verdict_from_pvalue dropping the locked test_name from provenance — are real and the suggested fix is sound.

b.why:
Verified against current code. (1) test_name IS bound in canonical_hypothesis_digest (preregistration.py:114). (2) A full-tree grep shows test_name is read NOWHERE except (a) its dataclass declaration (hypothesis.py:65) and (b) the digest payload (preregistration.py:114) — no confirmer or runner ever asserts the executed test matches the locked test_name. (3) confirm_unit_level receives only opaque statistic/p_value/direction_supports_claim and explicitly cannot infer the tail/test (confirm.py:163-169, 120-125). (4) verdict_from_pvalue is invoked with a hard-coded test_description='held-out unit-level test' (confirm.py:196), discarding the locked test_name. The PoC reproduces: lock test_name='mann_whitney_u', then after seeing the held-out partition compute p_value with any other test (e.g. a t-test) that clears alpha and call confirm_unit_level(h, ..., p_value=that_p, direction_supports_claim=True, authority=auth). require_preregistered raises nothing because hypothesis content (including the test_name field) is unchanged — nothing ties the supplied p_value to the test named in test_name. Verdict returns 'supported'. This is post-hoc test selection (p-hacking/HARKing) that the methodology claims to prevent.

Severity = high (not critical): the unit-level path is deliberately a trusted-caller boundary (the engine delegates statistic computation to study-owned code, like direction_supports_claim), so for study-owned confirmers some delegation is by-design and the docstrings could be amended to honestly disclaim it. BUT it is a genuine production-readiness defect because (a) it is directly exploitable to manufacture a 'supported' verdict, (b) the module docstring (preregistration.py:17-18) over-claims that changing 'the test' after locking causes verification to fail — true only for the test_name string field, not the executed test, which is materially misleading, and (c) the exact-same HARKing class was already fixed for whole-corpus predicted_direction (#G-001 in _resolve_direction) but the analogous protection/disclosure for test choice was never applied. The suggested fix is sound: propagate test_name into test_description, offer an engine-side assertion where the test identity is visible, and explicitly document the un-enforced caller obligation where it is not. Not a false positive, not speculative, not already-handled.

all.title: Malformed reasoning params leak a raw ValueError/TypeError through the typed public boundary (run/run_json)
all.description: Verified: _attempt() coerces caller params inline before any tier backend — `temperature = float(p.get('temperature', 0.0))` (seam.py:512) and `int(seed)` (seam.py:527). A non-numeric/None temperature or non-integer _seed raises a bare ValueError/TypeError inside _attempt, which runs under _run_with_retries whose except clauses catch ONLY PermanentReasoningError/RateLimitError/ReasoningError (seam.py:592). A raw ValueError/TypeError therefore propagates out of run()/run_json() unwrapped, violating the module's documented typed-error contract; a caller catching ReasoningError will crash. The error is semantically permanent (a malformed param is not retryable), so it should be a PermanentReasoningError.
all.poc: seam = TieredReasoningSeam(); for params in ({'temperature':'hot'}, {'temperature':None}, {'_seed':'notint'}): req = ReasoningRequest(prompt='x', tier=StakesTier.BULK, purpose='t', params=params); seam.run(req) -> raises raw ValueError/TypeError instead of ReasoningError.
all.suggested_fix: Coerce/validate temperature and _seed inside _attempt under a guard that re-raises as PermanentReasoningError, e.g. wrap the float()/int() in try/except (ValueError, TypeError) -> raise PermanentReasoningError(f'invalid reasoning param: {exc}') from exc. Keeps run()/run_json() to the documented typed-error contract.

a.why:
Confirmed in current code and PoC reproduces exactly. _attempt() coerces caller params inline before any backend: `temperature = float(p.get("temperature", 0.0))` at src/assay_engine/reasoning/seam.py:512 and `int(seed)` at seam.py:527. _attempt runs under _run_with_retries (seam.py:591), whose except clauses (seam.py:592-599) catch ONLY PermanentReasoningError, RateLimitError, and ReasoningError. A raw ValueError/TypeError from float()/int() therefore propagates unwrapped through run() (seam.py:649-652). run_json() is also affected at seam.py:665 (`float(request.params.get("temperature", 0.0))` outside any handler).

Ran the PoC with the project venv (.venv/bin/python, 3.14): {'temperature':'hot'} -> raw ValueError; {'temperature':None} -> raw TypeError; {'_seed':'notint'} -> raw ValueError. None are wrapped as ReasoningError, violating the module's documented typed-error contract (module docstring/error hierarchy at seam.py:66-74 establish ReasoningError as the single typed boundary; every other failure mode is funneled through it). A caller catching ReasoningError, as the contract invites, will crash.

No upstream validation/coercion of params['temperature'] or ['_seed'] exists — params is an arbitrary caller-supplied Mapping[str, Any], frozen only (seam.py:57-60). The path is genuinely reachable; not by-design or already-handled. The error is semantically permanent (a malformed param is never retryable), so the suggested fix (wrap float()/int() in try/except (ValueError, TypeError) -> raise PermanentReasoningError(...) from exc) is correct and consistent with seam.py:554's handling of bad input.

Severity: low. It is a real API-contract/robustness gap, but it is triggered only by a caller passing programming-error-grade invalid params (non-numeric temperature, non-int seed), not by runtime/environmental conditions, and carries no security, data-integrity, or availability impact beyond an unwrapped exception type.

b.why:
Confirmed in current code and reproduced with the exact PoC. In src/assay_engine/reasoning/seam.py:512 `_attempt` does `temperature = float(p.get("temperature", 0.0))` and at line 527 `int(seed)`, both inline before any tier backend. ReasoningRequest.__post_init__ (seam.py:59-60) only freezes the params mapping — it performs no coercion or validation — so malformed values reach _attempt unchecked. _attempt runs under _run_with_retries (seam.py:590-603) whose except clauses catch only PermanentReasoningError, RateLimitError, and ReasoningError; a bare ValueError/TypeError is not a subclass of any of these (ReasoningError extends RuntimeError, seam.py:66), so it propagates unwrapped out of run() (seam.py:649-652) and run_json() (run_json additionally calls float(temperature) itself at seam.py:665).

PoC run against the project venv (.venv/bin/python) produced exactly: {'temperature':'hot'} -> RAW LEAK ValueError; {'temperature':None} -> RAW LEAK TypeError; {'_seed':'notint'} -> RAW LEAK ValueError. None were caught as ReasoningError. This violates the module's documented typed-error contract (docstring lines 13-21, plus the catch-only-typed-errors retry design), and a caller catching ReasoningError will crash. The error is semantically permanent (a malformed param is not retryable), so the suggested fix to wrap the float()/int() coercion and re-raise as PermanentReasoningError is correct.

Severity assessed as low rather than higher: the trigger is a caller passing wrong Python types into params (a programming error in internal calling code), not external/attacker-controlled input — there is no privilege, data-integrity, or availability impact from outside the trust boundary. It is nonetheless a genuine, non-speculative, non-by-design contract violation in current code, hence confirmed.

all.title: HIGH_STAKES tier lacks the output-length cap that BULK enforces (asymmetric runaway-generation guard)
all.description: Verified: BULK always sets num_predict=BULK_NUM_PREDICT (seam.py:99, 368) and its docstring calls this 'the real guard' because a wall-clock timeout cannot interrupt a call already blocked in generation. _high_stakes_complete (seam.py:414-503) builds ClaudeAgentOptions with NO max-output-tokens equivalent — grep for max_tokens in seam.py returns nothing. A runaway BULK generation is bounded by tokens AND wall-clock; a runaway HIGH_STAKES generation is bounded only by anyio.fail_after(HIGH_STAKES_TIMEOUT). The cheaper/safer bound was applied only to the local tier, and HIGH_STAKES is the one consuming the fixed-cost subscription budget. The asymmetry is the same the BULK docstring argues against.
all.poc: A BULK call cannot emit more than ASSAY_BULK_NUM_PREDICT (2048) tokens. The parallel HIGH_STAKES call has no analogous bound: a prompt eliciting a very long completion runs until HIGH_STAKES_TIMEOUT (300s default), consuming the full subscription turn and a bounded-pool worker slot.
all.suggested_fix: Add an ASSAY_HIGH_STAKES_MAX_TOKENS cap and pass it to ClaudeAgentOptions (or the SDK's max-output-tokens option), mirroring num_predict. If the Agent SDK cannot bound output, document the asymmetry explicitly in the HIGH_STAKES docstring the way BULK's is documented.

a.why:
The literal code observation is accurate: BULK always sets num_predict=BULK_NUM_PREDICT (seam.py:368, default 2048, documented "the real guard" at seam.py:96-98), while _high_stakes_complete (seam.py:414-503) builds ClaudeAgentOptions (seam.py:435-442) with no output-token cap — grep confirms max_tokens/max_output/num_predict appear nowhere in the high-stakes path, and the pinned SDK (claude-agent-sdk>=0.2.101,<0.3) has no max_tokens/max_output_tokens field. So a runaway HIGH_STAKES completion is bounded only by anyio.fail_after(HIGH_STAKES_TIMEOUT) (seam.py:458) and the outer _with_timeout (+30, seam.py:550).

But this is NOT a true production-readiness problem and the finding's premises are partly wrong:

1. The slot-leak harm the PoC invokes is already prevented. The BULK token cap is "the real guard" SPECIFICALLY because a ThreadPoolExecutor worker cannot be interrupted (seam.py:96-97, 264-266) — a hung sync call leaks its pool slot. HIGH_STAKES runs under anyio.fail_after which ACTUALLY CANCELS the subprocess and frees the bounded-pool worker (seam.py:454-459, explicitly documented). So HIGH_STAKES's slot protection is stronger, not weaker. The "same asymmetry the BULK docstring argues against" does not hold: the architectural reason BULK needs the token cap (uninterruptible worker) does not apply to HIGH_STAKES.

2. The economic harm is misstated. HIGH_STAKES is a fixed-cost OAuth subscription with every metered credential scrubbed (ADR-0003, seam.py:9-11). A long completion consumes a subscription turn, not a metered dollar budget; "consuming the full subscription turn" is the by-design cost of a single bounded call, not runaway spend.

3. The suggested fix's hedge ("if the SDK cannot bound output") is contradicted by the actual SDK: ClaudeAgentOptions DOES expose max_budget_usd (stops the query with error_max_budget_usd) and max_turns — directly addressing the cost concern. So an actionable bound exists today, meaning the finding's claim that the tier is "bounded only by wall-clock" is inaccurate (a cost cap is achievable) while simultaneously the absence of one is not a defect.

Net: a legitimate low-severity defense-in-depth opportunity (optionally wire ASSAY_HIGH_STAKES_MAX_BUDGET_USD or max_turns), but the described HIGH problem — unguarded runaway / leaked slot / budget burn parallel to BULK — does not exist in current code. Not confirmed.

b.why:
All grep-able factual claims are accurate in current code (src/assay_engine/reasoning/seam.py). BULK always sets num_predict=BULK_NUM_PREDICT (line 368) and its docstring (lines 95-98) calls bounding output tokens "the real guard." _high_stakes_complete (lines 414-503) builds ClaudeAgentOptions (lines 435-442) with no max-output-tokens field; there is no max_tokens anywhere in the file. HIGH_STAKES is bounded only by anyio.fail_after(HIGH_STAKES_TIMEOUT) (line 458) plus the outer _with_timeout (HIGH_STAKES_TIMEOUT + 30, line 550). The output-length cap asymmetry is therefore real.

However, the asserted SEVERITY (a genuine runaway-generation hazard mirroring what the BULK docstring argues against) does not reproduce, for two reasons grounded in the current code:

1. The motivation for the BULK cap does not transfer to HIGH_STAKES. BULK runs in a ThreadPoolExecutor worker that "cannot interrupt a thread already blocked in a backend call" (lines 264-266, 349-355), so its wall-clock timeout leaks the pool slot — that uninterruptibility is exactly why num_predict is needed as "the real guard." By contrast, HIGH_STAKES's anyio.fail_after is explicitly documented (lines 451-456, #127) to genuinely CANCEL: "the cancellation propagates into query() and tears down the child," freeing the bounded-pool worker before the outer wall-clock. So the slot-leak harm that motivates num_predict is already mitigated for HIGH_STAKES by a mechanism BULK lacks; the wall-clock IS an effective interruption here.

2. The cost-runaway angle is moot under the fixed-cost model. The module (lines 9-11) and docstrings stress HIGH_STAKES is a fixed-cost subscription with every metered credential scrubbed. A very long completion does not cost more money; it costs latency, hard-bounded at HIGH_STAKES_TIMEOUT (300s default). "Consuming the full subscription turn" is a latency/availability concern already bounded by the existing timeout, not unbounded cost.

So neither motivating harm (leaked worker slot, runaway metered cost) genuinely materializes. What remains is a real but minor consistency/documentation gap: the BULK docstring documents its inner bound while the HIGH_STAKES docstring does not document why it relies solely on the (cancelling) timeout. The finding is not a false positive — the code asymmetry literally exists and the suggested-fix's documentation alternative is reasonable — but it is a low-severity hardening/doc nit, not a HIGH production-readiness defect.

all.title: discover_and_confirm lacks the on_step provenance hook that adjudicate_with_baseline has (asymmetric audit-trail wiring)
all.description: The two parallel firewall runners (discovery's own docstring calls itself 'the mirror of adjudication') were hardened asymmetrically for per-step provenance. adjudicate_with_baseline takes on_step: Callable[[Hypothesis, Verdict], None] | None (adjudication.py:193, 264-267) so a caller can record each pre-registered hypothesis+verdict to the hash-chained trail as each happens (#93), preserving GOVERNANCE §3's 'recorded before the next step runs' ordering. discover_and_confirm (discovery.py:69-165) has no such hook: a standalone discovery study can only batch-record after the whole loop returns, losing the append-before-next-step ordering its twin enforces.
all.poc: adjudicate_with_baseline(..., on_step=lambda h,v: trail.record(...)) lands each verdict in confirmation order. discover_and_confirm(corpus, split, discover=..., confirm=..., authority=...) accepts no on_step, so a standalone study records verdicts only after the loop returns or by re-implementing the loop.
all.suggested_fix: Add an `on_step: Callable[[Hypothesis, Verdict], None] | None = None` parameter to discover_and_confirm and invoke it after each verdict is validated (right after the misattribution check at discovery.py:164), mirroring adjudication.py:264-267.

a.why:
Factual claims verified: adjudicate_with_baseline has on_step (adjudication.py:193, 264-267); discover_and_confirm does not (discovery.py:69-76). However this is NOT a genuine production-readiness problem.

Key disqualifier: the production composition layer (run_study in pipeline.py) does NOT call discover_and_confirm at all. It reimplements the discovery/preregister/confirm loop inline (pipeline.py:502-584) and records per-step provenance there via trail.record(...) inside the CONFIRM loop (pipeline.py:577-583), preserving GOVERNANCE §3 'recorded before the next step' ordering. The on_step hook on adjudicate_with_baseline exists solely because the pipeline DOES call that function (pipeline.py:660-668, hook #93); discovery never needed the hook because the pipeline owns that loop directly.

The hash-chained provenance trail is a pipeline-level concept. Neither standalone runner (adjudicate, discover_and_confirm) touches a trail — both just return results. The claimed 'asymmetry' between the two standalone entry points does not actually exist: standalone adjudicate() calls adjudicate_with_baseline WITHOUT on_step (adjudication.py:172-180), so a standalone adjudication caller has no per-step trail hook either. So discover_and_confirm is no worse off than its true twin (standalone adjudicate); both standalone paths lack a wired trail by design, because trail provenance is enforced in the pipeline.

The finding's PoC is technically accurate (discover_and_confirm accepts no on_step) but the consequence is mischaracterized: discover_and_confirm returns verdicts in confirmation order, so a standalone caller can record them in order from the returned list; nothing about append-before-next-step ordering is lost for any production path. No correctness, data-integrity, security, or reliability defect results. At most this is a low-value API-symmetry/ergonomics nicety, not a production-readiness defect — the suggested fix is a reasonable enhancement but its absence is by-design and already-handled for the production (pipeline) path.

b.why:
All factual claims verified against current code (4 passes applied).

1. adjudicate_with_baseline has on_step: Callable[[Hypothesis, Verdict], None] | None = None at adjudication.py:193, invoked per-step at 264-267 right after the misattribution check and verdicts.append, with a comment explicitly stating its purpose is "so a caller can record the pre-registered hypothesis AND its verdict to provenance as each happens, not in a post-hoc batch (#93)."

2. discover_and_confirm (discovery.py:69-165) has NO on_step parameter. Its signature is (corpus, split, *, discover, confirm, authority) -> list[Verdict]. The loop body (133-164) validates origin, pre-registration, verdict type, and hypothesis<->verdict attribution, then appends to verdicts — but offers no per-step callback. A standalone caller can therefore only batch-record after the function returns, losing the append-before-next-step ordering its twin enforces.

3. The asymmetry is real and consequential to the design's own stated intent: both modules' docstrings frame discovery as "the mirror of adjudication" (discovery.py:3). Critically, the pipeline (src/assay_engine/pipeline.py) does NOT call discover_and_confirm at all — it re-implements the discovery/preregister/confirm loop inline (pipeline.py:524-583) precisely so it can trail.record per step (538-544, 577-583), while it DOES reuse adjudicate_with_baseline with on_step=_record_adjudication_step (pipeline.py:641-668). This is direct evidence that per-step provenance is the intended pattern and that discover_and_confirm is the odd one out: the only public runner that cannot drive a hash-chained trail in confirmation order without re-implementation.

4. Not speculative, not by-design, not already-handled: there is no alternative per-step hook on discover_and_confirm, and the parallel "mirror" design philosophy argues against the omission being intentional. The suggested fix (add on_step and invoke it after discovery.py:164, mirroring adjudication.py:264-267) is correct and minimal.

Severity: this is an API symmetry / audit-trail ergonomics gap in a public helper, not a correctness, data-integrity, or firewall-bypass defect. The production pipeline already records discovery provenance per-step (via its inline loop), so GOVERNANCE §3 ordering is preserved where it actually runs; a standalone discover_and_confirm caller retains a workaround (batch-record post-return, or re-implement the loop as the pipeline does). Genuine and worth fixing for consistency, but low impact.

all.title: License gate silently passes UNKNOWN / empty / missing-license dependencies (allow-by-default)
all.description: Verified: main() does `lic = (pkg.get('License') or '').upper()` (line 34) and only appends to `bad` when a deny token matches (line 37); a missing/empty/UNKNOWN field yields '' and is treated as ALLOWED. pip-licenses frequently reports UNKNOWN for packages whose license lives only in a classifier/LICENSE file. A genuinely GPL/AGPL/SSPL dependency whose metadata pip-licenses cannot parse therefore bypasses the Apache-2.0 compatibility gate entirely — the exact supply-chain blind spot the gate exists to close.
all.poc: Feed the gate [{'Name':'evil','Version':'1.0','License':'UNKNOWN'}, {'License':''}, {missing License}] -> main() returns 0 ('license gate OK').
all.suggested_fix: Treat unparsable licenses as deny-by-default: fail (or quarantine to a reviewed allowlist file) any package whose normalized license is in {'', 'UNKNOWN', 'UNKNOWN LICENSE', None}. Collect such packages into an 'unclassified' bucket that fails unless explicitly allowlisted (mirroring .pip-audit-ignore).

a.why:
Confirmed in current code. scripts/license_gate.py:34 computes `lic = (pkg.get("License") or "").upper()`, then only flags a package when an explicit deny token matches (line 37). A missing, empty, or "UNKNOWN" License field normalizes to a string containing none of the _DENY tokens, so the package is silently allowed and main() returns 0 (line 42-43). PoC reproduces exactly: feeding [{"License":"UNKNOWN"}, {"License":""}, {missing License}] prints "license gate OK (3 packages checked)" and exits 0.

This is allow-by-default for unclassifiable licenses, which is the core blind spot the gate exists to close. The CI wiring (.github/workflows/ci.yml:170-172) runs `pip-licenses --format=json --with-system` (default --from=meta, no classifier parsing) and pipes the output straight into the gate with NO upstream UNKNOWN filtering. pip-licenses legitimately reports "UNKNOWN" for packages whose license is declared only via Trove classifiers or a bundled LICENSE file rather than the metadata License field — a common case. Therefore a genuinely GPL/AGPL/SSPL dependency whose metadata License is unparseable bypasses the Apache-2.0 compatibility gate entirely. No compensating control exists (no separate UNKNOWN check, no allowlist). Genuine, not by-design (the module docstring states the gate's purpose is to fail strong-copyleft, and silently passing unclassified packages defeats that).

Severity assessment: high rather than critical — it is a gate bypass requiring a specific precondition (a copyleft dep with metadata that pip-licenses cannot parse making it into requirements.lock), not directly exploitable code execution; impact is legal/licensing/supply-chain compliance, the exact risk the control targets. Suggested fix is sound: deny-by-default for normalized license in {"", "UNKNOWN", "UNKNOWN LICENSE", None}, with an explicit reviewed allowlist bucket mirroring .pip-audit-ignore.

b.why: Confirmed against current code. scripts/license_gate.py:34 normalizes a missing/empty/None License field to "" via `(pkg.get("License") or "").upper()`. Line 37 only flags a package when a _DENY token is a substring of `lic`; an empty string or "UNKNOWN" matches none of the deny tokens, so the package is treated as allowed. Lines 42-43 return 0 ("license gate OK") whenever the bad list is empty. PoC reproduced exactly: feeding [{License:'UNKNOWN'},{License:''},{missing License}] returns rc=0 and prints "license gate OK (3 packages checked)". This is allow-by-default for unparseable licenses. pip-licenses legitimately reports UNKNOWN when license info lives only in a trove classifier or LICENSE file, so a genuinely GPL/AGPL/SSPL dependency with unparseable metadata bypasses the gate — the exact blind spot the gate exists to close. Not by-design and not handled elsewhere in this script. Suggested fix (deny-by-default for normalized license in {'', 'UNKNOWN', None} with an explicit allowlist) is appropriate.

all.title: Workflow defines no top-level permissions: — GITHUB_TOKEN runs with the repo-default (often write) scope
all.description: Verified: grep for 'permissions' in ci.yml returns no permissions block on any job or at the workflow level. The token therefore inherits the repo/org default, which on many repos is the legacy read-write default granting contents:write/packages:write. The audit job runs third-party tooling (pip-audit, pip-licenses) and does 'pip install -e .' over the fully-resolved dependency tree; an injected import-time payload in that step would run with whatever the default token allows.
all.poc: grep -n 'permissions' .github/workflows/ci.yml -> no match. With the org/repo default set to read-write, $GITHUB_TOKEN in the audit job carries contents:write/packages:write.
all.suggested_fix: Add a top-level `permissions: { contents: read }` block and grant narrower per-job scopes only where needed, making the token least-privilege regardless of the repo/org default.

a.why: Verified against current code. .github/workflows/ci.yml has no `permissions:` key at the workflow level or on any of its four jobs (core, integration, audit, all-checks). `grep -rn 'permissions' .github/` returns no match (exit 1); ci.yml is the only workflow file. Therefore GITHUB_TOKEN runs with the repo/org default scope, which on permissively-configured repos/orgs is the legacy read-write default (contents:write, packages:write, etc.). The audit and license-gate steps run `pip install -e .` and execute third-party tooling (pip-audit==2.10.1, pip-licenses==5.5.5, uv==0.11.21) — installs are hash-pinned, but dependency import/build-time code still executes during tests/license gating and would inherit the token's scope. None of the jobs push commits, publish packages, or create releases, so they need no write scope; adding a top-level `permissions: { contents: read }` is safe, non-breaking, and the standard least-privilege hardening control (matches OpenSSF Scorecard Token-Permissions and GitHub's hardening guidance). The finding's factual claims and PoC reproduce exactly. Severity is medium rather than high because the actual presence of write scope is conditional on the repo/org default-permissions setting (not visible in the code); where that default is read-only the live exploitability is low, making this a defense-in-depth/least-privilege gap rather than a guaranteed privilege escalation.

b.why: Verified against current code. /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/.github/workflows/ci.yml is the only workflow file, and a recursive grep for 'permissions' across .github/ returns no match (exit 1). There is no top-level permissions: block (ci.yml:1-7 jumps straight from name/on to jobs:) and no per-job permissions block on core, integration, audit, or all-checks. Consequently GITHUB_TOKEN inherits the repo/org default workflow-permission setting; where that default is the legacy read-write, the token carries contents:write/packages:write. The audit job (ci.yml:89-179) does run third-party tooling and executes editable install + license enumeration (pip install --no-deps -e . at line 169, pip-licenses), which imports the resolved tree, so an import-time payload would run with the inherited token scope. The PoC reproduces exactly (no grep match). This is a genuine, well-recognized least-privilege hardening gap (set explicit permissions: {contents: read} at workflow level). It is NOT speculative or by-design. Severity is tempered, not elevated, because: (1) impact is conditional on the org/repo default being read-write — modern GitHub defaults to read-only; (2) all installs use --require-hashes against committed hashed lockfiles (lines 28, 73, 168), which strongly constrains the supply-chain injection precondition; (3) no step actually uses the token for writes (no push/publish/release), so even an over-scoped token has no in-workflow consumer. Real fix is trivial and worthwhile (one-line top-level block), but it is defense-in-depth rather than an actively-exploitable write path in current code.

all.title: Integration gate reports green even when service containers never came up — live backend tests silently skip
all.description: The integration job declares postgres/qdrant service containers and the workflow comment claims the live checkpointer + vector-store tests run for real, but every live test is guarded by skipif(not postgres_up()/qdrant_up()), which probe localhost with a short TCP connect and SKIP (not fail) when unreachable. If an image fails to pull, the health check never passes, or a port mapping breaks, all live persistence/observability tests skip and pytest still exits 0 — so the required integration check goes green while exercising none of the real backends it claims to cover. The reproducibility/coverage guarantee is unenforced.
all.poc: conftest postgres_up()/qdrant_up() return False on OSError; live tests use skipif. No ASSAY_REQUIRE_*/xfail_strict/no-skip enforcement. Break the qdrant service port mapping -> test_qdrant_* SKIP, job still succeeds.
all.suggested_fix: In the CI integration lane only, set ASSAY_REQUIRE_LIVE_BACKENDS=1 and have conftest convert the skip into a hard failure when set (or assert postgres_up()/qdrant_up() in a session-scoped autouse fixture gated on that flag), so local/dev runs skip cleanly while CI guarantees the live paths ran.

a.why: Confirmed in current code. CI integration job (.github/workflows/ci.yml:44-82) declares postgres:16 and qdrant service containers and its comments (lines 77-79) claim the live Postgres checkpointer + Qdrant vector store tests now run via the containers. But the guards make those tests skip-not-fail when a backend is unreachable: conftest.py:42-47 qdrant_up()/postgres_up() return False on OSError from a 1s TCP connect; test_persistence_live.py:36,48,76,111,148,184 wrap every live test in @pytest.mark.skipif(not qdrant_up()/postgres_up()). There is NO enforcement: pyproject.toml:82-98 [tool.pytest.ini_options] has no xfail_strict, no addopts forbidding skips; the integration step (ci.yml:82) runs plain `pytest -q --cov` and sets no ASSAY_REQUIRE_LIVE_BACKENDS; grep for autouse/pytest_sessionstart/pytest_collection/REQUIRE_LIVE across tests/ returns nothing. Consequently if an image fails to pull, the health check never passes, or a port mapping breaks, all live persistence/vector-store tests SKIP, pytest exits 0, the integration job succeeds, and all-checks (ci.yml:185-200) goes green while exercising none of the real backends. The PoC reproduces exactly: break the qdrant port mapping -> test_qdrant_* SKIP -> job green. Genuine, not by-design (the comments explicitly promise the live paths execute) and not already-handled. Severity medium: it is a CI assurance/coverage-guarantee gap producing false confidence in the merge gate, not a runtime or security defect in shipped code.

b.why:
Confirmed in current code. ci.yml:44-82 declares postgres (line 48) and qdrant (line 57) service containers and the comment at lines 77-79 explicitly claims the live Postgres checkpointer + Qdrant vector store run via the service containers. However tests/integration/conftest.py:22-27 tcp_open() returns False on any OSError, and qdrant_up()/postgres_up() (lines 42-47) rely on it. Every live backend test in tests/integration/test_persistence_live.py is guarded by @pytest.mark.skipif (lines 36, 48, 76, 111, 148, 184), which SKIPS rather than fails when the probe is False. A repo-wide grep found no ASSAY_REQUIRE_LIVE_BACKENDS, no xfail_strict, and no addopts enforcement (pyproject.toml [tool.pytest.ini_options] has only filterwarnings); the CI run is plain pytest -q --cov (ci.yml:82). pytest exits 0 on skips, so the integration job succeeds and all-checks (ci.yml:185-200) only checks needs.integration.result == success, so the gate goes green. PoC reproduces: break the qdrant port mapping or image pull, tcp_open raises OSError, qdrant_up() returns False, test_qdrant_* SKIP, job green while exercising none of the real backends. The advertised integration coverage is genuinely unenforced.</reasoning>
</invoke>

all.title: stable_seed docstring claims "distinct inputs get distinct seeds" but truncates SHA-256 to 32 bits
all.description: Verified: the docstring states "distinct inputs get distinct seeds" (line 14) while the implementation returns int(digest,16) % (1 << bits) with bits defaulting to 32 (line 17). Modulo into a 32-bit space cannot guarantee distinct seeds for distinct inputs — by the birthday bound a collision becomes more-likely-than-not at ~77k inputs and is guaranteed past 2^32. build_baseline_artifact derives the reproducibility seed from this, so two distinct corpora/configs can collide onto the same seed, contradicting the stated property. This is a documentation over-claim, not a determinism break (the same input still maps to the same seed).
all.poc: stable_seed reduces a 256-bit digest to 32 bits; generate ~10^5 distinct part-strings and a duplicate seed appears (birthday bound). At small bits it is trivially demonstrable: stable_seed('a', bits=8) and stable_seed('b', bits=8) can collide in 256 buckets.
all.suggested_fix: Soften the docstring to the truthful property: "the same inputs always yield the same seed; collisions between distinct inputs are possible because the digest is reduced to `bits` bits." Optionally widen the default seed space or document the collision probability.

a.why: Confirmed as a documentation over-claim in current code. In src/assay_engine/baseline/determinism.py, stable_seed's docstring (line 73) states "distinct inputs get distinct seeds," but the implementation (line 76) returns int(digest,16) % (1 << bits) with bits defaulting to 32 (line 69). Reducing a 256-bit SHA-256 digest into a 32-bit space cannot be injective: collisions are guaranteed past 2^32 distinct inputs (pigeonhole) and more-likely-than-not at ~77k inputs (birthday bound). PoC reproduces: at bits=8, distinct inputs "15" and "24" both map to seed 219. build_baseline_artifact derives the reproducibility seed from stable_seed (line 153), so two distinct corpora/configs can share a seed, contradicting the stated property. This is genuine (the false property is literally asserted), not speculative or by-design. However, it is purely a doc over-claim with no functional/security impact: same input still yields same seed (determinism holds), and each artifact still records its own distinct corpus_fingerprint and input_hashes (lines 138-144, 155), so a coincident seed integer does not break reproducibility. Severity: low — fix is to soften the docstring (and optionally widen default bits / document collision probability).

b.why: Verified against current code. determinism.py:73 docstring states "distinct inputs get distinct seeds" while determinism.py:76 implements `return int(digest, 16) % (1 << bits)` with `bits: int = 32` default (line 69). Reducing a 256-bit SHA-256 digest into a 32-bit space via modulo provably cannot guarantee distinct seeds for distinct inputs. PoC reproduces: stable_seed('15') and stable_seed('24') collide at bits=8 (seed 219), and the 32-bit default collides after ~107k distinct part-strings (consistent with the cited birthday bound where collision becomes more-likely-than-not near ~77k). build_baseline_artifact derives the reproducibility seed from this at line 153 (`seed = stable_seed(*(input_hashes[k] for k in sorted(input_hashes)))`), so two distinct corpora/configs can collide onto the same seed, contradicting the stated property. This is a genuine documentation over-claim in current code (after 4 hardening passes) — not speculative and not already-handled. Severity is low: it is a docstring accuracy issue, not a determinism/correctness break (the same input still deterministically maps to the same seed, and seed collisions across distinct studies do not affect each study's own reproducibility). Suggested fix to soften the docstring is appropriate.

all.title: Graph gate node warns-but-allows a missing trail recorder while run_study's gate path always records (asymmetric audit guarantee)
all.description: Verified: in run_study the gate handler always records to the trail before enforcing (pipeline.py run_gate). The LangGraph graph path make_gate_node (gatenode.py:90-161) makes the trail recorder OPTIONAL: with recorder=None it merely warns (lines 114-115) and records only into non-tamper-evident LangGraph state. The docstring itself (lines 108-112) calls this a 'GOVERNANCE §3 audit gap' where a checkpoint edit flipping rejected->approved is undetectable by verify_records. The same governance concern is structurally guaranteed on one path and merely warned-about on the other.
all.poc: make_gate_node(gate, propose) with no recorder builds successfully (only a warning). Operator approvals then live solely in LangGraph checkpoint state; verify_records cannot detect a checkpoint tamper that flips the decision.
all.suggested_fix: Make the recorder mandatory on make_gate_node (raise when None), matching run_study's unconditional trail.record — or, if a recorder-less node must remain supported, gate it behind an explicit opt-out flag (allow_unrecorded=True) so shipping the audit-gap configuration is a conscious choice.

a.why: Verified against current code. The asymmetry is real and exists as described. run_study's gate path (src/assay_engine/pipeline.py:326-342, run_gate) unconditionally calls trail.record(...) before enforcing the decision — the tamper-evident hash-chained trail is structurally guaranteed. The LangGraph graph path make_gate_node (src/assay_engine/orchestration/gatenode.py:90-161) declares recorder as optional (default None, line 94); when None it merely emits warnings.warn (lines 114-120) and the node body skips trail recording entirely (the recorder-is-not-None guard at line 143). The docstring itself (lines 108-112) labels this a 'GOVERNANCE §3 audit gap' where a checkpoint edit flipping rejected->approved is undetectable by verify_records. PoC reproduces exactly: make_gate_node(gate, propose) with no recorder builds successfully (warning only) and records operator decisions solely into non-tamper-evident LangGraph checkpoint state. The same governance invariant is thus structurally enforced on one path and merely warned-about on the other. Not speculative, not by-design (the docstring itself flags it as a gap), and not fully handled — pass 3 (#F-007) added only a warning, which is suppressible and easily unseen in production, leaving the insecure configuration shippable by accident. Severity is medium rather than high: it sits behind the optional 'orchestration' extra (not the default run_study path), requires the operator to both ship a recorder-less node and have checkpoint-tamper access, and a loud warning is emitted. The suggested fix (mandatory recorder, or explicit allow_unrecorded opt-out) is appropriate to make shipping the audit gap a conscious choice rather than an accidental default.

b.why:
Confirmed in current code. The asymmetry is real: run_study's run_gate (src/assay_engine/pipeline.py:326-342) records every gate decision to the hash-chained ProvenanceTrail unconditionally (trail.record at lines 331-338) before enforcing. The LangGraph graph path's make_gate_node (src/assay_engine/orchestration/gatenode.py:90-161) makes the recorder OPTIONAL: with recorder=None it only warns (lines 114-120) and the node still builds and runs, persisting decisions solely into LangGraph checkpoint state (the apply_gate_decision update at line 71), which is not tamper-evident. The recorder is invoked only inside `if recorder is not None` (line 143), so a recorder-less node never touches the chained trail.

PoC reproduces exactly: using the project's .venv/bin/python, make_gate_node(gate, propose) with no recorder returns a callable node and emits only a UserWarning ("built with no recorder: gate decisions will NOT be written to the hash-chained provenance trail (GOVERNANCE §3 audit gap)"); no exception is raised. So the audit-gap configuration ships with merely a warning.

The finding's claim is faithful to the code, and the docstring itself (gatenode.py:108-112) labels this a GOVERNANCE §3 audit gap where a checkpoint edit flipping rejected->approved is undetectable by verify_records. The graph path is a first-class, documented, supported execution path (compile_graph in graph.py supports gate-bearing graphs and even fails loud when a checkpointer is missing, lines 67-71), which makes the warns-but-allows treatment of the recorder genuinely inconsistent with the codebase's own fail-loud convention for the analogous checkpointer requirement.

Severity medium rather than high: this is an audit-integrity / fail-safe-default hardening gap, not an exploitable-by-default vulnerability. It is mitigated by a loud runtime warning and prominent docstrings in both make_gate_node and compile_graph, and exploitation requires an operator to both omit the recorder AND tamper with the durable checkpoint store. The suggested fix (mandatory recorder, or an explicit allow_unrecorded=True opt-out) is sound and matches the codebase's existing fail-loud pattern (graph.py:67-71). Not a false positive, not by-design, not already-handled.

all.title: gatenode over-claims: "atomically" recorded trail+state, and "terminal-decision idempotency" that holds only for graph state
all.description: Two related docstring over-claims, verified. (1) The recorder block asserts the validated decision lands in the hash-chained trail and graph state 'atomically' (~line 144), but the code records to the trail first then returns the update for LangGraph to commit to a separate store; there is no shared transaction. Only the recorder-raises direction is handled — if the recorder succeeds and the subsequent checkpoint write (or process) fails, the trail holds the approval while graph state does not. (2) The module docstring (line 21) claims 'an approved decision is terminal: re-entering ... does not re-prompt or re-record', but already_decided() (line 79) reads only state['gate_decisions'] — never the trail. Since the two stores are not atomic and the trail has no de-dup, a re-entry where graph state lost the prior approval but the trail retained it passes already_decided()==False and the recorder appends a SECOND 'approved' entry to the append-only trail, contradicting 'does not re-record'.
all.poc: (B) Wire a recorder, resume a parked gate with a valid approval, kill the process after the node returns but before the checkpoint commits: trail holds 'approved', recovered checkpoint gate_decisions does not. (C) Restore graph state from a checkpoint taken before the gate_decisions update landed while keeping the same trail; re-enter -> already_decided() False -> re-prompt -> recorder appends a duplicate 'approved' entry.
all.suggested_fix: Replace 'atomically' with the accurate guarantee ('records to the trail BEFORE returning the update so a recorder failure aborts the decision; the two stores are NOT transactional — a crash between them can leave the trail ahead of graph state'). Scope the idempotency claim to graph state, or key already_decided() off the durable trail as well so the no-op also covers re-recording.

a.why: Both over-claims are present in current code at the cited lines. (1) gatenode.py:144-146 comment asserts the decision lands in the hash-chained trail and graph state "atomically," but the implementation records to the trail (recorder(rec), line 152) then returns the partial update (line 159) for LangGraph to commit to a separate checkpoint store. There is no shared transaction; only the recorder-raises direction is guarded (lines 153-158). If the recorder succeeds and the subsequent checkpoint commit/process fails, the trail holds "approved" while graph state does not — exactly PoC (B). The word "atomically" is therefore an over-claim. (2) The module docstring (lines 21-24) states an approved decision is terminal and re-entry "does not re-prompt or re-record," but already_decided() (lines 79-84) reads only state["gate_decisions"], never the durable trail. Because the two stores are not transactional and the trail is append-only with no de-dup (recorder simply appends per line 152), a re-entry from a checkpoint taken before the gate_decisions update landed — while the same trail is reused — yields already_decided()==False, re-fires the interrupt, and appends a SECOND "approved" trail entry, contradicting "does not re-record" (PoC C). Both PoC scenarios are logically reproducible given the architecture. This is a genuine documentation/contract over-claim, not speculative or already-handled. It is not a runtime crash or security flaw — the underlying split between LangGraph-owned checkpoint commit and append-only trail is by design — so the defect is that the docstrings/comments promise stronger (atomic, idempotent-across-stores) guarantees than the code delivers. Severity low: fix is to correct the wording and/or scope the idempotency claim to graph state (or key already_decided() off the trail too).

b.why: Both over-claims are present in current code. (1) gatenode.py:144-149 comment asserts the validated decision lands in the hash-chained trail and graph state "atomically", but lines 150-159 call recorder(rec) then `return update` (line 159); the update is committed to a SEPARATE LangGraph checkpoint store outside any shared transaction. Only the recorder-raises direction is handled (lines 153-158 raise GateError before returning). If recorder succeeds but the subsequent checkpoint write/process fails, the trail holds "approved" while graph state does not — not atomic. (2) Module docstring line 21-22 claims an approved decision is terminal and "does not re-prompt or re-record", but already_decided() (lines 79-84) reads ONLY state.get("gate_decisions") — it never consults the trail. Since the two stores are not atomic and the trail has no de-dup, a re-entry where graph state was restored from an earlier checkpoint (lost the approval) while the trail retained it yields already_decided()==False (line 123) -> interrupt re-fires (line 137) -> on a fresh valid approval recorder() appends a SECOND "approved" entry (line 152) to the append-only trail, contradicting "does not re-record". PoC (B) and (C) are reproducible given the code structure. The defect is genuine: the docstring/comment promise guarantees (atomicity; trail-level idempotency) the code does not provide. It is a correctness-of-claims/documentation-accuracy defect, not an exploitable runtime bug — the normal path is sound (record-before-return, fail-closed on recorder error), and the duplicate trail entry only arises after a checkpoint rollback. Hence low severity, but confirmed.

all.title: Provenance-trail artifact tempfile leaked on every tracked run (delete=False, never unlinked)
all.description: Verified (consolidates the two duplicate reports of this issue): run_study's best-effort _log_trail callback serializes the hash-chained trail to tempfile.NamedTemporaryFile('w', suffix='_provenance.json', delete=False) (pipeline.py:739-740) then calls t.log_artifact(rid, path) (line 744) and never unlinks the file. MLflow copies the file into the artifact store but does not remove the source, so every successful study run with a tracker leaves one orphaned *_provenance.json in TMPDIR; over a long-lived production node these accumulate unbounded. The file also holds provenance payloads at a default-mode temp path. Contrast versioning.py which uses mkstemp + try/finally unlink.
all.poc: Run run_study(plan, gate_handler=auto_approve, tracker=MlflowExperimentTracker()) N times; ls $TMPDIR/*_provenance.json shows N leaked files. There is no os.unlink in the _log_trail closure or surrounding finally.
all.suggested_fix: Wrap log_artifact in try/finally and os.unlink(path) afterward (MLflow has already copied it), or write into a tempfile.TemporaryDirectory() context that auto-cleans. Optionally chmod 0o600 since the file holds provenance payloads.

a.why: Confirmed against current code. pipeline.py:739-744 (_log_trail closure) creates tempfile.NamedTemporaryFile("w", suffix="_provenance.json", delete=False, encoding="utf-8"), dumps the provenance records, captures path=fh.name, then calls t.log_artifact(rid, path) and returns — with no os.unlink anywhere. The surrounding try/finally (lines 771-778) only does trace_ctx.close() and tracker.end_run(); it never unlinks the temp file. The closure is invoked via track(_log_trail) (line 746) on every successful tracked run (reached whenever tracker is not None and run_id is not None on the happy path). MLflow's log_artifact copies the source into the artifact store but does not delete the source, so each successful study run with a tracker leaves one orphaned *_provenance.json in TMPDIR, accumulating unbounded on a long-lived node — exactly as the PoC describes. The contrast is also accurate: persistence/versioning.py:74-81 uses tempfile.mkstemp + try/finally tmp.unlink(missing_ok=True), the codebase's own established cleanup pattern, which is absent here. One minor correction to the report: NamedTemporaryFile already creates the file with 0o600 perms by default (Python opens it O_CREAT|O_EXCL, mode 0o600), so the "default-mode temp path" exposure concern is largely already mitigated; the genuine issue is the unbounded file leak, not permissions. Genuine, not speculative or by-design, but bounded impact (small files, disk-hygiene/exhaustion over long uptime rather than a correctness or security break) — hence low severity. Fix as suggested: wrap log_artifact in try/finally with os.unlink(path), or use TemporaryDirectory().

b.why: Verified against current code. In src/assay_engine/pipeline.py the _log_trail closure (lines 733-744) creates tempfile.NamedTemporaryFile("w", suffix="_provenance.json", delete=False, encoding="utf-8") (lines 739-741), dumps the hash-chained trail into it, captures path = fh.name (743), then calls t.log_artifact(rid, path) (744) and never unlinks the file. The track() wrapper (lines 317-324) merely invokes fn under try/except and does no cleanup. The MLflow adapter log_artifact (observability/tracking.py:93) delegates to client.log_artifact, which copies the source into the artifact store but leaves the source in place. The surrounding finally block (pipeline.py:771+) closes the trace context and ends the run but never unlinks the temp file. Therefore every successful study run with a tracker leaves one orphaned *_provenance.json in TMPDIR, accumulating unbounded on a long-lived node — the PoC reproduces as described. The contrast with persistence/versioning.py (mkstemp + try/finally unlink at lines 74-80) is also accurate. Confirmed as a genuine, currently-present resource leak. Severity is low rather than high: the leaked files are small JSON, only on the optional tracked path, no correctness/security/availability impact in the near term, and the default-mode temp permissions are a minor hardening nit on a single-tenant node — but it is a real unbounded-growth bug worth fixing with try/finally os.unlink or a TemporaryDirectory context.

all.title: Module-global reasoning ThreadPoolExecutor cannot self-heal if a worker future vanishes — saturation guard can wedge permanently
all.description: The reasoning seam uses one process-global ThreadPoolExecutor; _submit_bounded increments _inflight before submit and decrements via add_done_callback(_release). Accounting is guarded for KeyboardInterrupt/SystemExit and pool-shutdown, but there is no recovery if a worker thread dies abnormally without the future ever completing (a C-level backend crash that takes the worker but not the process, or pool-state corruption): the done-callback never fires, _inflight is never decremented, and after _POOL_WORKERS such events every subsequent call raises RateLimitError('pool saturated') for the rest of the process lifetime. future.result() timeouts are handled (slot intentionally held), but a vanished future is not. Latent availability/leak risk on the exception path; the checkpoint pool by contrast is self-healing.
all.poc: Monkeypatch _pool.submit to return a Future that never resolves; call _with_timeout _POOL_WORKERS times -> each times out but the futures never complete, so _release never runs; the next _submit_bounded raises RateLimitError unconditionally and stays that way forever.
all.suggested_fix: Add a watchdog/reap step: track each in-flight future with its submission time and, in _submit_bounded's saturation branch, force _release on futures that are done-but-unreleased or have exceeded (per-call timeout + grace) before refusing. Document that wait=False atexit may abandon in-flight backend calls.

a.why:
The mechanical observation is accurate: src/assay_engine/reasoning/seam.py:219-238 has no watchdog/reap step. _submit_bounded's saturation branch (lines 224-231) unconditionally raises RateLimitError without tracking in-flight futures or reaping any that are done-but-unreleased. _inflight is only ever decremented by _release, which is only invoked via add_done_callback (line 251), so a future that NEVER completes leaks its slot forever. The supplied PoC reproduces exactly: I replaced rc._pool.submit with a fake returning a bare concurrent.futures.Future(), called _with_timeout 8 (=_POOL_WORKERS) times, and every subsequent _submit_bounded raised RateLimitError("pool saturated") permanently with _inflight stuck at 8.

However, this is NOT a genuine production-readiness defect in current code, because the trigger condition is not achievable in the CPython runtime:

1. The PoC only produces a never-completing future by monkeypatching _pool.submit to return a bare Future. A real ThreadPoolExecutor cannot do this: CPython's _WorkItem.run() wraps the callable in try/except BaseException and ALWAYS sets the future's result or exception before the worker returns. Any Python-level return or raise (including KeyboardInterrupt/SystemExit) completes the future and fires the done-callback.

2. The described real triggers — "a C-level backend crash that takes the worker but not the process" — are not real failure modes for Python threads. Threads share one process/address space; a genuine C-extension segfault crashes the WHOLE process, not a single thread, so you never end up with a live interpreter serving requests while a counter leaks. "Pool-state corruption" is asserted with no concrete path.

3. The one realistic permanent-leak vector — a hung backend call — is already handled. The outer timeout intentionally holds the slot (lines 263-267, documented), and the inner bounds (anyio.fail_after at line 458 for HIGH_STAKES; the httpx.Timeout at lines 359-361 for BULK) guarantee the worker eventually returns, at which point the future completes and _release fires. I verified with the REAL pool: a callable that outlives the outer timeout but eventually returns leaves _inflight at 1 immediately after the timeout, then self-heals to 0 once the worker completes. So the genuinely reachable leak path self-recovers by design.

The finding itself concedes this is "Latent availability/leak risk" — i.e. a missing defensive belt-and-suspenders against a failure mode that cannot occur in CPython, not an exploitable/reachable availability bug. The cited "self-healing checkpoint pool" comparison (persistence/checkpoint.py) is a psycopg ConnectionPool with check_connection liveness on checkout — a different mechanism for a different (genuinely reachable) failure (DB disconnect), so it does not establish that the reasoning pool needs equivalent recovery for an unreachable failure. A reap step would be reasonable defensive hardening but does not address any reproducible problem in current code.

b.why:
The finding posits that a worker future can "vanish" (worker dies abnormally without the future ever completing), permanently leaking _inflight slots until the pool wedges with RateLimitError for the process lifetime. This cannot occur in CPython's threading model via the actual code path.

Verified against actual code at src/assay_engine/reasoning/seam.py:201-267:

1. The PoC only reproduces by monkeypatching _pool.submit to return a bare Future() that the executor never owns or runs (I confirmed it does wedge: inflight=8/8, next call raises RateLimitError). But this is artificial — the real _submit_bounded (line 234) ALWAYS calls _pool.submit, which returns an executor-managed future. CPython's concurrent.futures.thread._WorkItem.run wraps the task in `try: ... except BaseException: self.future.set_exception(exc)` (I inspected the source) — so the future is ALWAYS completed, for every Python-level outcome including SystemExit/KeyboardInterrupt raised inside the worker. I empirically confirmed: a worker that raises RuntimeError, and one that raises SystemExit, both leave future.done()==True and fire add_done_callback. Therefore _release always runs and the slot is always freed.

2. The only theoretical path to a non-completing real future is a C-level crash (segfault/abort) of the worker thread. The finding's premise — "takes the worker but not the process" — does not hold in CPython: all threads share one process; a segfault kills the ENTIRE process, so there is no surviving process left to wedge. There is no public CPython API to kill a single thread. The high-stakes backend runs as a SUBPROCESS (claude CLI via anyio.run) and the bulk backend is an httpx HTTP call — a crash in either returns an exception to the Python worker, which then completes the future normally; neither crashes the worker thread itself.

3. The genuinely realistic case — timeout while a worker is still running in a real backend call — is already designed and self-heals. I empirically verified: after a FuturesTimeout, inflight stays at 1 (slot intentionally held, documented at lines 264-266), and once the real worker returns the done-callback fires and inflight returns to 0. Releasing earlier would over-subscribe the pool, so holding the slot is correct, not a bug.

4. The finding's supporting comparison is also wrong: it claims "the checkpoint pool by contrast is self-healing," but src/assay_engine/persistence/checkpoint.py contains no inflight-counted ThreadPoolExecutor (grep shows only a comment mention of saturation), so there is no analogous self-healing pool to contrast against.

The accounting is already correct for every reachable failure mode (normal/BaseException in worker, timeout, pool-shutdown RuntimeError at line 235, and the interrupt-window fallback at line 252-253 with per-future idempotent _release at lines 201-216). The proposed watchdog would address a scenario that cannot arise in CPython. Speculative, not a true production-readiness defect in current code.

all.title: Unit-level confirmation lacks the directionless-lock refusal its whole-corpus twin enforces (tail chosen post-hoc)
all.description: Verified: confirm_whole_corpus routes the locked direction through _resolve_direction (confirm.py:254-289), which REFUSES a directionless-but-locked hypothesis (None -> #G-001 HARKing guard) and rejects any tail not in {greater,less}. confirm_unit_level (confirm.py:180-197) only shape-validates a non-None predicted_direction and otherwise lets the caller-supplied direction_supports_claim flag decide; a locked hypothesis with predicted_direction=None passes unit-level confirmation with the polarity effectively chosen post-hoc by the caller — exactly the HARKing case the whole-corpus path refuses. The asymmetry is documented (confirm.py:163-169) but is a cross-mechanism guard-strength divergence.
all.poc: Lock a UNIT_LEVEL hypothesis with predicted_direction=None. confirm_unit_level(..., direction_supports_claim=True) -> supported; flip the flag -> the SAME locked hypothesis -> contradicted. The whole-corpus twin refuses this outright via _resolve_direction.
all.suggested_fix: If unit-level studies are expected to bind the tail at lock time, add a symmetric refusal: when a unit-level hypothesis is locked, require predicted_direction to be non-None. If the asymmetry must stand, emit a runtime warning when direction_supports_claim is supplied for a directionless-locked hypothesis, mirroring _gate_preregistration's authority=None warning.

a.why: Verified against current code. confirm_unit_level (confirm.py:146-197) never routes through _resolve_direction; it only shape-validates a non-None predicted_direction (lines 180-186) and otherwise calls verdict_from_pvalue with the caller's direction_supports_claim flag (lines 189-196). verdict_from_pvalue (lines 137-143) maps p_value<=alpha + direction_supports_claim=True -> supported, and the same significant result with the flag flipped -> contradicted, with no reference to the locked tail. Hypothesis.predicted_direction defaults to None and __post_init__ (hypothesis.py:80-94) permits None, so a UNIT_LEVEL hypothesis can be locked directionless. _gate_preregistration (confirm.py:40-63) checks lock/pre-registration binding but does NOT require a non-None predicted_direction. Therefore the PoC reproduces exactly: a directionless-but-locked UNIT_LEVEL hypothesis passes unit-level confirmation with the polarity chosen post-hoc by the caller, while the whole-corpus twin refuses the identical situation via _resolve_direction's #G-001 HARKing guard (lines 270-277). This is a real cross-mechanism guard-strength divergence: the HARKing refusal that the whole-corpus path enforces has no equivalent (neither a symmetric refusal nor even a runtime warning like the authority=None warning) at unit level. Mitigating: the asymmetry is documented as by-design (confirm.py:163-169, #G-017) because the engine cannot itself infer the unit-level tail from an opaque held-out statistic, so direction_supports_claim is an intentional trusted caller obligation; the open HARKing path requires a caller to deliberately drive a directionless-locked hypothesis. It is genuine but a narrowly-scoped integrity-hardening gap rather than a critical/high defect, hence medium.

b.why:
Verified against current code at src/assay_engine/methodology/confirm.py.

The described asymmetry is real and the PoC reproduces:
- confirm_whole_corpus routes the locked tail through _resolve_direction (confirm.py:254-289), which at lines 271-277 REFUSES a directionless-but-locked hypothesis (predicted_direction is None) as HARKing (#G-001) and rejects any tail outside {greater,less}.
- confirm_unit_level (confirm.py:146-197) only shape-validates predicted_direction WHEN it is non-None (line 180: `if hypothesis.predicted_direction is not None and ...`). A None locked direction skips that guard entirely. It then calls _gate_preregistration (lock check only) and verdict_from_pvalue, where the polarity is decided SOLELY by the caller-supplied direction_supports_claim flag (confirm.py:140-142). So a directionless-locked unit-level hypothesis confirms as `supported` with direction_supports_claim=True and `contradicted` with the flag flipped — the exact post-hoc tail-selection (HARKing) the whole-corpus path refuses. PoC reproduces.

This is a genuine cross-mechanism guard-strength divergence in a methodology engine whose stated purpose is preventing post-hoc rule/direction selection. Not speculative.

Severity is medium rather than high because: (1) the asymmetry is explicitly documented as by-design at confirm.py:163-169 — the unit-level path receives an opaque held-out statistic/p_value and structurally cannot derive the tail itself, so the engine delegates the deciding flag to the caller with a documented obligation to derive it from the locked tail; (2) it is not a silent computational error but a delegated contract. The real gap is enforcement: the engine does not refuse a directionless lock at unit level, nor warn when direction_supports_claim is supplied for a None-direction locked hypothesis (the suggested fix mirrors _gate_preregistration's authority=None warning). A careless caller can therefore HARK. Confirmed as a real production-hardening concern, scoped to medium given the documented-by-design + caller-delegation mitigation.

all.title: Confirm-step double-dip leak check (assert_confirm_only) enforced by confirm_unit_level but not by the discovery/pipeline runners
all.description: DiscoverConfirmSplit.assert_confirm_only (firewalls.py:126-145) is the runtime guard that the confirm step touched ONLY held-out ids. confirm_unit_level calls it on the caller-supplied evaluated_ids (confirm.py:188). The two composing runners that also confirm — discover_and_confirm (discovery.py) and the pipeline's DISCOVERY/CONFIRM block — never call assert_confirm_only; they only verify the held-out partition is non-empty and subset the corpus, then trust the study-supplied confirmer to evaluate within it without checking which ids it actually scored. A confirmer that internally pulls in discovery-partition units is caught by the standalone primitive but NOT by either runner. Firewall-B's leak check exists on one parallel mechanism and is absent on the other two.
all.poc: Build a confirm_held_out that ignores its held_out arg and evaluates discovery-partition units. confirm_unit_level(hyp, split=split, evaluated_ids=<discovery ids>, ...) raises FirewallViolation 'double-dipping'. The same leak through discover_and_confirm(... confirm=that_callable ...) completes cleanly because the runner never asks the confirmer which ids it evaluated.
all.suggested_fix: Have the discovery runners obtain (or require) the set of ids the confirmer evaluated and pass it through split.assert_confirm_only before accepting the verdict (e.g. have HeldOutConfirmer return evaluated ids alongside the verdict). At minimum document in both runners that the confirm-time double-dip check is delegated to the study, making the asymmetry with confirm_unit_level explicit.

a.why:
The factual claims are accurate against current code. `DiscoverConfirmSplit.assert_confirm_only` (firewalls.py:126-145) is the per-id double-dip leak check, and a src-wide grep shows it has exactly ONE caller: confirm_unit_level at confirm.py:188, where evaluated_ids is an explicit parameter. The two composing runners do NOT call it: discover_and_confirm (discovery.py:69-165) subsets the corpus (subset_corpus), guards that both partitions are non-empty (lines 100-126), validates DISCOVERY origin, pre-registration, and verdict<->hypothesis attribution (lines 133-163), but never obtains or checks which ids the confirmer scored — the HeldOutConfirmer protocol (discovery.py:42-45) returns only a Verdict, no evaluated-ids. The pipeline CONFIRM block (pipeline.py:558-584) is identical: it calls plan.confirm_held_out(h, held_out), validates Verdict type (566-570) and hypothesis-id attribution (571-575), but never calls assert_confirm_only. So Firewall-B's per-id leak check exists on confirm_unit_level and is absent on both runners — the asymmetry is real and as described. The PoC reproduces: a confirmer that returns a verdict without honestly scoring only held-out ids is caught by confirm_unit_level (which is handed evaluated_ids) but not by either runner (which never asks the confirmer which ids it scored).

I downgrade severity, however. The runners enforce the separation STRUCTURALLY by handing the confirmer only the held-out sub-corpus (discovery.py:119, pipeline.py:562) — the confirmer is never given the discovery partition or the full corpus, so it cannot ACCIDENTALLY double-dip. The finding's specific exploit ("a confirmer that internally pulls in discovery-partition units") requires the confirmer to willfully reach for data outside its handed corpus, which the discovery.py module docstring (lines 22-24) already explicitly disclaims as out of scope: "this is a *signature-level* guarantee — it prevents accidental double-dipping, not a step that willfully reflects into the caller's frame (only process isolation could)." Thus the genuine, novel issue is the inconsistency between confirm_unit_level offering a stronger evaluated-ids check that the runners don't replicate (and don't even document the omission of) — a defense-in-depth and documentation gap, not an exploitable firewall bypass. The suggested fix's own minimum bar is "document the asymmetry," consistent with a low-severity hardening item.

b.why:
The factual core of the finding is verified true in current code. `DiscoverConfirmSplit.assert_confirm_only` (firewalls.py:126-145) is the runtime double-dip leak check. Grep across src/ shows it has exactly ONE call site: `confirm_unit_level` (confirm.py:188). Neither composing runner calls it: `discover_and_confirm` confirms via `confirm(hypothesis, held_out)` at discovery.py:150 and the pipeline's DISCOVERY/CONFIRM block confirms via `plan.confirm_held_out(h, held_out)` at pipeline.py:562 — both only guard non-empty partitions (discovery.py:100-126; pipeline.py:504-527) and never check which ids the confirmer scored. The `HeldOutConfirmer` Protocol returns only a `Verdict` (discovery.py:42-45), so the runners structurally cannot learn the evaluated id set. Tests confirm the asymmetry: assert_confirm_only/evaluated_ids are exercised only for the standalone primitive (test_firewalls.py:40-64, test_confirm.py:535-616), with no runner-level leak-check coverage. The PoC reproduces as described: confirm_unit_level raises on a lying confirmer's discovery ids, while the same callable through discover_and_confirm/pipeline completes cleanly.

Severity is LOW, not high, for two concrete reasons: (1) Both runners hand the confirmer a structurally-subset corpus — `held_out = subset_corpus(corpus, split.confirm_ids)` (discovery.py:119, pipeline.py:500) — which contains only held-out units (and relations with both endpoints held-out, discovery.py:63-66). So ACCIDENTAL double-dipping by a normally-written confirmer operating on its argument is already prevented structurally; the PoC requires a confirmer that willfully ignores its handed corpus and reaches discovery data from a captured closure. (2) Even the "protected" primitive's check is weak in the same way: confirm_unit_level's `evaluated_ids` is caller-DECLARED, not derived from the actual computation, so a willful confirmer can declare held-out ids while internally scoring discovery units and assert_confirm_only still passes. The module docstring explicitly scopes this (discovery.py:23-24): "signature-level guarantee — it prevents accidental double-dipping, not a step that willfully reflects into the caller's frame (only process isolation could)." So the finding identifies a genuine consistency/defense-in-depth asymmetry worth closing (the suggested fix of having HeldOutConfirmer return evaluated ids, or at minimum documenting the delegation, is legitimate), but it is not a critical/high production breach — it neither enables accidental leakage nor is the comparison primitive a real barrier against the same willful actor.

all.title: Persisted provenance records carry no key/algorithm marker — a keyed (HMAC) trail is indistinguishable from an unkeyed one on re-verify
all.description: entries_to_records()/to_records() serialize seq/kind/summary/payload/timestamp/prev_hash/entry_hash but record nothing about whether the chain was sealed with SHA-256 (unkeyed) or HMAC-SHA256 (keyed). from_records()/verify_records() require the caller to supply the correct secret out-of-band; omitting it on a keyed trail fails with the generic 'content was altered, or the wrong key was used' error, conflating a missing-key config with genuine tampering. Conversely there is no recorded signal that a trail SHOULD have been keyed, so a downstream consumer cannot detect that an audit-grade trail was persisted unkeyed. Weakens the provenance-completeness claim for persisted trails.
all.poc: trail = ProvenanceTrail(secret=b'x'*16); trail.record('a','b'); recs = trail.to_records(); from_records(recs) -> raises ProvenanceError as if tampered, because no secret was passed and nothing in recs indicates one is required.
all.suggested_fix: Emit a non-authenticated-but-informative envelope alongside the records (e.g. {'algo':'hmac-sha256'|'sha256','keyed':bool}) so a verifier can fail with a precise 'this trail is keyed; supply the secret' message and a consumer can detect an unkeyed audit trail. Do not include the secret. Optionally fold an algorithm tag into the hashed preimage so it is itself integrity-protected.

a.why:
Verified against current code at src/assay_engine/provenance.py. entries_to_records() (lines 219-233) serializes exactly seq/kind/summary/payload/timestamp/prev_hash/entry_hash and records NO key/algorithm marker. _digest() (lines 69-91) branches on secret being None (SHA-256) vs set (HMAC-SHA256) but the choice leaves no trace in the serialized record. verify_records() (lines 236-257) and from_records() (lines 260-288) require the caller to supply the secret out-of-band; on a keyed trail verified without the secret, the recomputed digest mismatches and raises the generic message at line 255 "content was altered, or the wrong key was used (hash mismatch)", conflating a missing-key config with genuine tampering. The PoC reproduces verbatim: ProvenanceTrail(secret=b'x'*16); record('a','b'); to_records() -> record keys are ['entry_hash','kind','payload','prev_hash','seq','summary','timestamp'] (no algo/keyed field); from_records(recs) raises ProvenanceError "provenance entry 0 content was altered, or the wrong key was used (hash mismatch)". So both claims hold: (1) no recorded signal that a trail is/should-be keyed, and (2) the diagnostic conflates two distinct failure modes.

However, this is a diagnostics/completeness gap, not an integrity or security defect. The module docstring (lines 16-28) explicitly documents that keyed verification requires supplying the same secret out-of-band as by-design (it is the source of tamper-resistance — an unauthenticated keyed marker would be attacker-flippable, which the finding itself acknowledges by suggesting it be folded into the hashed preimage). The integrity guarantees are intact; the genuine weaknesses are a conflated error message and a downstream consumer's inability to detect an unkeyed audit trail. That mildly weakens the provenance-completeness UX but is not high severity.

b.why:
Verified against current code at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/provenance.py. All factual claims hold and the PoC reproduces exactly.

1. entries_to_records() (lines 211-233) and to_records() (lines 206-208) serialize ONLY seq/kind/summary/payload/timestamp/prev_hash/entry_hash. Confirmed at runtime: record keys are exactly those seven; no algo/keyed/algorithm field is present.

2. verify_records() (line 252) and from_records() (lines 260-288) require the caller to supply the correct secret out-of-band; default secret=None.

3. PoC reproduced verbatim: ProvenanceTrail(secret=b'x'*16); trail.record('a','b'); from_records(trail.to_records()) raises ProvenanceError: "provenance entry 0 content was altered, or the wrong key was used (hash mismatch)" — line 255. This conflates a missing-key config error with genuine tampering.

4. Confirmed: nothing in the persisted records signals that a trail SHOULD have been keyed, so a downstream consumer cannot detect that an audit-grade trail was persisted unkeyed.

Genuineness: This is a real, reproducible diagnosability/completeness gap, not speculative. However it is NOT an integrity vulnerability — within each chosen tier the integrity guarantees hold (a keyed trail still verifies correctly when the secret is supplied; unkeyed tampering of the documented kind is still caught). The module docstring (lines 16-28) already documents the two tiers and that verify_records must be given the same secret, so the tier boundary is partly by-design. The genuine defect is (a) the error message conflating config-vs-tamper and (b) the inability of a consumer to detect an unkeyed audit trail — both diagnostic/completeness weaknesses against the 'audit-grade provenance' claim, with the suggested remedy being an informational (non-authenticating) envelope. No data loss, no integrity bypass.

Severity: low — confirmed and reproducible, but bounded to diagnosability/completeness rather than correctness or security; partially documented as a known tier boundary.

all.title: Operator-username PII not redacted from transcripts when the home-dir name is short (<4 chars) or appears in a dash-delimited project slug
all.description: _scrub redacts the operator username two ways: the path rule '(/(?:Users|home)/)[^/\s"']+' (requires a literal '/Users/[REDACTED]') and _USERNAME_RE derived from Path.home().name. The Claude project-dir slug is dash-delimited (e.g. '-Users-[REDACTED]-hc-grc'); the path rule does NOT match it (no slashes), so the only mask inside slugs/git-author strings/prose is _USERNAME_RE — which returns None for any home-dir name shorter than 4 chars (to avoid over-redacting common words). For operators whose login is <4 chars, the username then leaks verbatim into committed transcripts via the slug and prose, defeating the stated #F-024/#G-007 PII-scrubbing goal. The docstring claims operator PII is redacted, so this is an undisclosed completeness gap.
all.poc: Set HOME so Path.home().name='bob'; create $SRC=.../-Users-[REDACTED]-hc-grc/<id>.jsonl containing the slug path and 'bob'. Run capture_transcripts — the committed transcript still contains 'bob' and '-Users-[REDACTED]-...' because _USERNAME_RE is None (len<4) and the '/Users/' rule never matches the dash slug.
all.suggested_fix: Always derive the slug form of the home path (replace os.sep with '-') and redact it explicitly regardless of username length; lower/remove the len<4 skip in favor of word-boundary-anchored token redaction.
