At minimum document that usernames <4 chars are NOT auto-redacted so reviewers scrub before publishing.

a.why: Confirmed in current code and reproduced via PoC. scripts/capture_transcripts.py:67 (`if not name or len(name) < 4: return None`) disables _USERNAME_RE for home-dir names shorter than 4 chars. The only other username mask is the path rule at line 51, `re.compile(r"(/(?:Users|home)/)[^/\s\"']+")`, which requires slash-delimited `/Users/[REDACTED] and never matches the dash-delimited Claude project slug `-Users-[REDACTED]-...`, git-author strings, or prose. PoC: HOME set so Path.home().name='bob' (3 chars); a transcript containing `/Users/[REDACTED]/...`, slug `-Users-[REDACTED]-hc-grc`, author `bob`, and prose `the operator bob did this` was run through capture_transcripts.py. Output: only `/Users/[REDACTED]/` was masked; `-Users-[REDACTED]-hc-grc`, `bob` (author), and `the operator bob did this` all leaked verbatim into the committed transcript. This contradicts the explicit claims in the docstring/comments (lines 11, 34-35 "operator PII... username embedded in filesystem paths"; lines 56-61 and 83-84 claiming the bare username is "redact[ed]... everywhere" via #G-007 "wherever it appears... project-dir slug"). It is an undisclosed completeness gap defeating #F-024/#G-007 for operators with logins <4 chars. Severity is medium rather than high: it requires a specific precondition (operator login name <4 chars — the campaign operator is '[REDACTED]', 11 chars, so not currently exposed), transcripts are not shipped in the wheel, and the README already carries a general public-release review caveat. But the gap is real, the in-code claim of universal username redaction is false for short handles, and the skip is silent/undocumented at the failure point.

b.why: Verified against current code at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/scripts/capture_transcripts.py and reproduced the PoC. Confirmed mechanics: (1) line 51 path rule `(/(?:Users|home)/)[^/\s"']+` requires literal slashes and cannot match the dash-delimited Claude project slug `-Users-[REDACTED]-hc-grc`; (2) line 67 `if not name or len(name) < 4: return None` disables `_USERNAME_RE` for any home-dir name shorter than 4 chars; (3) lines 85-86 only mask the bare username when `_USERNAME_RE is not None`. PoC run with HOME making `Path.home().name='bob'` (len 3) and a transcript containing the slug, a git-author field, and prose: output was `{"cwd":"/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-grc","slug":"-Users-[REDACTED]-hc-grc","author":"bob","prose":"committed by bob today"}` — the `/Users/[REDACTED]/` path was masked but the slug, author, and prose leaked 'bob' verbatim (3 hits). This is a genuine completeness gap vs. the stated #G-007 goal: the `_username_pattern` docstring (lines 56-61) explicitly claims it redacts the username 'wherever it appears' including 'the Claude project-dir slug', yet silently skips short handles, and the module docstring/`_SECRET_PATTERNS` comment claim operator PII is redacted without disclosing the <4-char exception. Not speculative, not by-design (the by-design rationale only covers avoiding over-redaction of common words, not the slug-leak), and not fully handled. Severity is low rather than higher because it is compound-conditional: it requires an operator login <4 chars AND the transcripts being published, and the module docstring (lines 11-13) already warns operators to review/exclude transcripts/ before publishing and that PII may remain (an existing human-review backstop); additionally the wheel never ships transcripts (line 13) and the actual default/live operator is `[REDACTED]` (len 10), so the deployed instance is unaffected. The suggested fix (always redact the os.sep-to-dash slug form of the home path regardless of name length, and/or word-boundary token redaction; at minimum document the <4-char exception) is appropriate.

all.title: No concurrency group — superseded PR pushes keep running and audit/lockfile-compile can race
all.description: Verified: ci.yml has no concurrency block, so every push to an open PR spawns the full matrix without cancelling the prior in-flight run. Beyond wasted minutes, two overlapping audit runs on the same branch both run lockfile resolution / 'pip install -e .' and upload identically-named SBOM/license artifacts that can race. On a single dedicated compute node this compounds contention with the reasoning/checkpoint pools.
all.poc: grep -n concurrency .github/workflows/ci.yml -> no match. Push twice in quick succession to a PR; both runs execute to completion.
all.suggested_fix: Add a top-level concurrency: { group: ${{ github.workflow }}-${{ github.ref }}, cancel-in-progress: true } so a newer commit cancels the stale run and only one audit/lockfile-compile executes per ref.

a.why:
Verified against current code at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/.github/workflows/ci.yml (the only workflow file). The PoC reproduces exactly: `grep -ni concurrency .github/workflows/ci.yml` returns no match, and recursive grep over .github/ finds no concurrency block anywhere. The trigger is `on: pull_request` (line 6) with no type filter and `push` to main (lines 3-5), and there is no `cancel-in-progress`, so every new commit on an open PR spawns the full matrix (core x4 python versions, integration, audit, all-checks) while the prior in-flight run continues to completion. On the documented single dedicated compute node (per MEMORY context: one Mac-mini production node sharing reasoning/checkpoint pools), this is a genuine resource-contention / wasted-minutes problem. The suggested fix (top-level concurrency group keyed on github.workflow + github.ref with cancel-in-progress: true) is correct and standard.

Caveat lowering severity: the finding's secondary rationale about an artifact/lockfile RACE is technically inaccurate. The audit job uploads `sbom-cyclonedx` (ci.yml:154-160) and `license-report` (ci.yml:173-179); GitHub scopes uploaded artifacts per run ID, so concurrent runs on the same ref do not overwrite/corrupt each other's artifacts. Likewise `pip install -e .` (lines 29, 74, 169) runs in isolated ephemeral runner VMs, so there is no shared-state lockfile race between runs. The real, confirmed harm is duplicate concurrent execution wasting CI capacity on a single node — an efficiency/hardening concern, not a security or correctness bug.

b.why:
The literal grep claim is true: .github/workflows/ci.yml (the only workflow file, confirmed via ls and grep across .github/) has no `concurrency` block, so a newer push to an open PR does not cancel the prior in-flight run (ci.yml:1-7 trigger on push/pull_request with no concurrency key). So superseded runs do keep executing.

However, the finding's severity-justifying claims — the parts that elevate it above trivial CI hygiene — are false:

1. "two overlapping audit runs ... upload identically-named SBOM/license artifacts that can race": GitHub Actions artifacts are scoped per workflow RUN (distinct run IDs), not per ref. Two concurrent runs have separate artifact namespaces, so the fixed names (sbom-cyclonedx ci.yml:158, license-report ci.yml:177) cannot collide or corrupt each other across runs. No race.

2. "both run lockfile resolution / pip install -e . and can race": every job uses runs-on: ubuntu-latest (ci.yml:12,45,90,188) — ephemeral, fully isolated GitHub-hosted VMs per run. Concurrent runs share no filesystem or state, so the uv pip compile (ci.yml:108-116) and pip install -e . (ci.yml:29,74,169) cannot race.

3. "compounds contention with the reasoning/checkpoint pools on a single dedicated compute node": CI executes on GitHub-hosted runners, not the dedicated Mac mini production node. There is no resource contention with local pools.

Stripped of the false race/contention claims, the only genuine residual is wasted CI minutes on superseded pushes — a best-practice efficiency improvement, not a production-readiness defect or any kind of correctness/security problem. Adding cancel-in-progress is a reasonable nice-to-have, but the finding as DESCRIBED (race conditions, node contention) does not reproduce and is not a true hardening defect. Marking not confirmed / false_positive on the substantive (non-cosmetic) claims.

all.title: pip-audit / SBOM each re-fetch the advisory DB and re-resolve — a network blip fails the 'reproducible' audit gate non-reproducibly
all.description: The audit job invokes pip-audit multiple times (gate at line 131, SBOM generation at line 151) plus a license-gate install, each reaching the OSV vuln DB / PyPI at run time. The stated goal is a reproducible supply-chain gate, but the result still depends on live availability of those services: a transient OSV/PyPI outage fails a commit that passed yesterday — the same non-reproducibility class the version pins were meant to remove. There is no retry, no vuln-DB caching, and the SBOM step re-runs the whole audit instead of reusing the gate's result.
all.poc: The gate step (line 131) and SBOM step (line 151) both run pip-audit against requirements.lock, each performing its own vulnerability-service lookup; no --cache-dir reuse or retry wrapper. An OSV/PyPI 5xx during any fails the required audit job.
all.suggested_fix: Run pip-audit once with --cache-dir (or a pinned/cached vuln DB), emit both the pass/fail and the CycloneDX SBOM from that single invocation, and wrap network steps in a bounded retry — halving PyPI/OSV calls and removing the duplicate live resolution from the reproducible gate.

a.why: Verified against /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/.github/workflows/ci.yml. The cited mechanics are accurate: the audit job runs pip-audit twice against requirements.lock — the gate at line 131 (pip-audit --strict --require-hashes -r requirements.lock) and the SBOM step at line 151 (pip-audit -r requirements.lock --format cyclonedx-json -o sbom.cyclonedx.json) — each performing its own independent OSV/PyPI vulnerability lookup. The license gate at line 168 (pip install --require-hashes -r requirements.lock) adds a third live PyPI fetch. There is no --cache-dir reuse, no pinned/cached vuln DB, and no retry wrapper anywhere in the job. The job's own comments (lines 97-101, #G-022) state the explicit goal is reproducibility ("a green commit could fail tomorrow on a tooling bump"), yet runtime dependence on live OSV/PyPI availability reintroduces exactly that non-determinism for a different reason: a transient OSV/PyPI 5xx fails a commit that passed yesterday. The PoC is correct and reproduces by design — any network blip during these required steps fails the audit job. The duplicate audit invocation is also genuinely redundant (the SBOM step re-resolves the same set the gate already audited). Not speculative, not already-handled, not by-design.\n\nSeverity, however, is LOW. This is a CI-robustness/flakiness and minor-waste concern, not a security vulnerability or a production-runtime defect. It does not affect the shipped artifact or runtime behavior; worst case is occasional spurious red CI on transient upstream-service outages plus one redundant pip-audit call. The suggested fix (single invocation emitting both pass/fail and SBOM, --cache-dir reuse, bounded retry on network steps) is a reasonable hardening improvement but addresses reliability, not a critical/high production-readiness gap.

b.why:
The literal code facts are accurate: pip-audit runs twice against requirements.lock — the gate at .github/workflows/ci.yml:131 (`pip-audit --strict --require-hashes --progress-spinner off -r requirements.lock`) and the SBOM at :151 (`pip-audit ... --format cyclonedx-json -o sbom.cyclonedx.json`), each performing its own live OSV/PyPI lookup, with no `--cache-dir` and no retry wrapper (confirmed via grep — no cache/retry anywhere in the file). However, this is not a true production-readiness problem; it is a mischaracterized CI-quality/optimization suggestion.

Reasons it is a false positive:
1. Conflated reproducibility classes. The version pins (#F-031) make *dependency resolution* deterministic — the same lockfile installs the same set. That IS reproducible (and separately gated by the lockfile-sync step at :102-116). A vulnerability scan against the OSV advisory DB is, by design, time- and network-dependent: a newly disclosed CVE SHOULD fail a commit that passed yesterday. That is the entire purpose of vuln scanning, not a reproducibility regression. The achievable reproducibility (pinned tool version pip-audit==2.10.1 at :101, pinned audited set via requirements.lock) is already implemented. The finding's central premise — that this re-introduces 'the same non-reproducibility class the version pins were meant to remove' — is incorrect.
2. The transient-outage failure mode is generic CI flakiness common to every network-dependent step, remedied by a re-run; it is not broken, insecure, or incorrect behavior. No production behavior is affected — this job only runs in CI.
3. The duplicate invocation is intentional: the second run produces a different artifact (CycloneDX SBOM), and the comments at :135-153 document the deliberate coupling. Adding --cache-dir/retry or merging the two calls is a reasonable enhancement but its absence is not a defect — gate correctness and security are unaffected.

The suggested fix is a legitimate optimization, but the finding as framed (a reproducibility defect / production-readiness problem) does not hold.

all.title: QdrantVectorStore.query has no k validation and assumes every returned point carries a 'ref' payload (raw KeyError)
all.description: QdrantVectorStore.query (line 110) is the public VectorStore.query implementation. (1) It does not validate k — a k<=0 is forwarded straight to the backend, unlike upsert() which validates len-equality and batch_size>=1. (2) It reads p.payload['ref'] for every returned point; any point NOT written by this store's upsert() (inserted by another process/tool, or a future schema change) has no 'ref' key and query() raises a bare KeyError rather than a typed store error. The class docstring promises ids round-trip via the 'ref' payload but never defends the read path against foreign points. Both are edge-case gaps at an otherwise-hardened public boundary.
all.poc: A point upserted by an external writer lacks 'ref'; query() does [(p.payload['ref'], float(p.score)) for p in res.points] -> KeyError 'ref' escapes untyped. Separately store.query(vector, k=0) forwards k=0 with no guard.
all.suggested_fix: Validate k at the top of query ('if k < 1: raise ValueError("k must be >= 1")') to mirror upsert, and read defensively: ref = (p.payload or {}).get('ref'); if ref is None: continue (or raise a typed store error) so a foreign point cannot crash query with a raw KeyError.

a.why: Verified against current code at src/assay_engine/persistence/vectorstore.py. Both sub-claims are factually accurate. (1) query (line 110-114) does NOT validate k: it forwards k directly as limit=k to query_points (line 112) with no guard, in clear asymmetry with upsert which validates len-equality (line 96-97) and batch_size>=1 (line 98-99). (2) query reads p.payload["ref"] via bare subscript on line 114 ([(p.payload["ref"], float(p.score)) for p in res.points]); any returned point lacking the "ref" key raises a bare, untyped KeyError. The class docstring (lines 53-59) promises ids round-trip via the payload but the read path has no defense for foreign points. No typed store-error class exists in the persistence package and there are no internal callers, so query is a genuine public boundary. The PoC reproduces: an externally-written point without "ref" -> KeyError escapes; k=0 forwards unguarded. Severity is low rather than higher because: the k=0 case does not actually crash (Qdrant limit=0 returns empty results — it is an inconsistency, not a fault), and the KeyError requires a foreign writer into a loopback-only, study-owned collection (an edge case, partly defensible-by-design since the store documents it owns the payload). It is a real but minor boundary-hardening gap at an otherwise-hardened seam, not a speculative or already-handled issue.

b.why:
Both claims verified against actual code at src/assay_engine/persistence/vectorstore.py.

(1) k validation gap: query (line 110-113) forwards k directly to limit=k with no guard. This is a genuine asymmetry: the sibling upsert (lines 96-99) validates len-equality and batch_size>=1, but query does not validate k. PoC store.query(vector, k=0) forwards k=0 to the backend unchecked — reproduces as described.

(2) raw KeyError on foreign payload: line 114 reads p.payload["ref"] via raw subscript for every returned point. The class docstring (lines 53-59) states ids round-trip via the 'ref' payload, but the read path is undefended. Any point lacking 'ref' (inserted by an external writer, a different tool, or after a schema change) makes query() raise a bare KeyError instead of a typed store error. PoC reproduces exactly: [(p.payload["ref"], ...) for p in res.points] -> KeyError 'ref'.

Both are factually present, reproduce mechanically, and are not already-handled or by-design — query simply lacks the defensive handling its sibling upsert has. This is a real production-hardening gap at a public VectorStore boundary.

Severity is low rather than high: the collection is loopback-only and study-owned, so a foreign writer with a payload missing 'ref' is an edge case rather than a routine path; and k<=0 is input-hygiene asymmetry with no security/correctness/data-loss consequence (limit<=0 is rejected/empty at the backend). The fix is the suggested one: guard k<1 at the top of query, and read defensively via (p.payload or {}).get("ref") with continue-or-typed-error.

all.title: Import-time int() of ASSAY_VECTOR_HTTP_PORT (and tracing port) raises an opaque ValueError masquerading as an import failure
all.description: VECTOR_HTTP_PORT = int(os.environ.get('ASSAY_VECTOR_HTTP_PORT', '6333')) executes at module import (vectorstore.py:18); observability/tracing.py does the same for ASSAY_TRACING_PORT (line 30). A non-numeric value raises ValueError during `import`, surfacing as an import-time crash with no context tying it to the offending env var — and for any module importing these transitively it can present as a confusing ImportError-adjacent failure. The codebase elsewhere re-reads and bounds numeric env config at point-of-use with clamping (seam.py, checkpoint.py), so this unbounded import-time parse is a config-error-contract inconsistency. Low because it only triggers on misconfiguration, but the failure mode is opaque.
all.poc: ASSAY_VECTOR_HTTP_PORT=notaport python -c 'import assay_engine.persistence.vectorstore' -> ValueError: invalid literal for int() with base 10: 'notaport' at import, no env-var context.
all.suggested_fix: Parse these ports lazily inside vector_store_url()/get_qdrant_client() and tracing_endpoint(), or wrap int() in a helper raising a clear configuration error naming the env var. Keeps import side-effect-free and gives an actionable message.

a.why: Confirmed in current code. vectorstore.py:18 executes VECTOR_HTTP_PORT = int(os.environ.get("ASSAY_VECTOR_HTTP_PORT", "6333")) at module import; observability/tracing.py:30 does the same for ASSAY_TRACING_PORT. Both PoCs reproduce verbatim with the venv interpreter: `ASSAY_VECTOR_HTTP_PORT=notaport .venv/bin/python -c 'import assay_engine.persistence.vectorstore'` raises `ValueError: invalid literal for int() with base 10: 'notaport'` at vectorstore.py:18, and the tracing variant raises the identical error at tracing.py:30. The error message names only the offending literal value, not the env var, so it is opaque for an operator. The config-error-contract inconsistency claim is also accurate: checkpoint.py:61, 73, 263-265 parse numeric env config at point-of-use with max()/min() clamping (e.g. max(0, int(os.environ.get("ASSAY_MIGRATION_LOCK_TIMEOUT_MS","30000")))), whereas these two ports are parsed unbounded at import time. Caveats that keep this low: it triggers only under operator misconfiguration (non-numeric value), the traceback does cite the exact file/line, and the "masquerading as ImportError" framing is slightly overstated — it is a plain ValueError raised during import, not an actual ImportError. Net: a genuine, low-severity operability/config-contract inconsistency, not a correctness or security defect.

b.why:
Verified in current code. vectorstore.py:18 `VECTOR_HTTP_PORT = int(os.environ.get("ASSAY_VECTOR_HTTP_PORT", "6333"))` and tracing.py:30 `TRACING_PORT = int(os.environ.get("ASSAY_TRACING_PORT", "6006"))` both parse ports unconditionally at module-import scope. PoC reproduces exactly with the project's venv interpreter (.venv/bin/python3.14): `ASSAY_VECTOR_HTTP_PORT=notaport` -> traceback terminating at vectorstore.py:18 with `ValueError: invalid literal for int() with base 10: 'notaport'`, and the tracing equivalent fails identically at tracing.py:30. The error fires during `import` and the message contains no reference to the offending env var.

The "contract inconsistency" claim is substantiated: every other numeric env parse in the codebase bounds the value at parse — seam.py:99-110 (`max(1, int(_env(...)))`, etc.) and checkpoint.py:61,263-265 (`max(0, int(os.environ.get(...)))`) — while these two are unbounded. More tellingly, determinism.py:140-146 (audit #G-021) documents and implements the exact convention this finding recommends: catching an opaque parse error and re-raising with the offending key named. The two port parses violate that established config-error contract. tracing.py's own module docstring (lines 4-11) promises the module "loads ... with neither [extra] present" and degrades to a no-op, which an import-time crash on a malformed port contradicts — so this is not by-design.

Not speculative, not already-handled, reproduces as described, present after 4 hardening passes. Severity low is correct: only triggers on operator misconfiguration, no security or data-integrity impact, trivially recoverable by fixing the env var; the only harm is an opaque/mislocated failure message.

all.title: redact_creds docstring claims the primary connection string is "always scrubbed precisely"
all.description: Verified: redact_creds documents 'the primary connection string is always scrubbed precisely' (line 140) via layer 1, an exact substring match text.replace(conn_str, sanitized) (line 143). psycopg/libpq error messages do not always echo the DSN verbatim: a password can be URL-decoded, re-quoted, partially rendered, or the host/port reformatted, so the exact-match replace can miss the credential and only the generic best-effort regex backstops catch it. 'always' promises a precision the exact-match layer cannot deliver for every backend rendering.
all.poc: Construct a conn_str with a percent-encoded password (e.g. 'a%2Fb') so libpq decodes it before embedding the decoded form in an error string. text.replace(conn_str, ...) does not match the decoded substring; layer 1 misses it and only the regex backstops (best-effort) apply.
all.suggested_fix: Change 'the primary connection string is always scrubbed precisely' to '...is scrubbed precisely when the backend echoes it verbatim; the URI-userinfo and DSN-password regex layers backstop other renderings (best-effort).'

a.why: Verified against current code. checkpoint.py:128-145: redact_creds layer 1 is text.replace(conn_str, _sanitize_conn_str(conn_str)) (line 143) — an exact substring match — and the docstring at line 140 claims "the primary connection string is always scrubbed precisely." This is an accurate description of an overstated guarantee. PoC reproduces: with conn_str 'postgresql://[REDACTED]@localhost:5432/db', if libpq URL-decodes %2F to '/' before echoing it ('...postgresql://[REDACTED]@localhost:5432/db'), the exact-match layer 1 does NOT match the decoded substring (confirmed by execution: "after layer1" shows the password still present). Only the best-effort regex backstop _CREDS_RE (layer 2, line 99/144) then strips it. So the "precise" layer 1 demonstrably misses a legitimate backend rendering, contradicting the word "always". The claim that layers 2-3 are merely for "secondary endpoints" is also inaccurate — they are doing the actual redaction work for the primary conn_str in this case. This is genuine: it is a real documentation-accuracy defect, not speculative (the decode behavior is standard libpq/URI handling) and not by-design (the docstring asserts a precision the implementation cannot deliver). However, it is low severity: it is a docstring overstatement, not a credential leak in realistic conditions — the regex backstops still scrub the common decoded-URI and keyword/value DSN renderings (verified layer 2 catches the URI case). The suggested fix (soften "always...precisely" to "...when the backend echoes it verbatim; ...regex layers backstop other renderings (best-effort)") correctly aligns the docstring with behavior. A residual prose-rendering case (e.g. password "a/b" without '=') would escape all layers, but libpq does not emit plaintext passwords in such prose, so this is not a practical leak.

b.why: Confirmed as a genuine docstring-overclaim issue in current code. At /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/persistence/checkpoint.py:140 the docstring asserts "the primary connection string is always scrubbed precisely," but the layer-1 implementation at line 143 is a literal exact-substring replacement: text.replace(conn_str, _sanitize_conn_str(conn_str)). Exact substring matching only succeeds when the backend echoes conn_str byte-for-byte. The PoC is sound: a DSN with a percent-encoded password (e.g. a%2Fb) is URL-decoded by libpq to a/b before use; if the error text embeds the decoded form (a/b) rather than the original encoded substring (a%2Fb), text.replace finds no match and layer 1 silently misses the credential. The same gap applies to any backend reformatting (re-quoting, host/port normalization, partial rendering). In those cases only the regex layers (_CREDS_RE at line 144, _DSN_PW_RE at line 145) catch it — and the docstring itself (lines 135-139) explicitly labels those as "best-effort." So "always ... precisely" promises a guarantee the exact-match layer cannot deliver for every backend rendering; the suggested rewording is accurate. Severity is low: this is a documentation accuracy/overclaim defect, not a runtime vulnerability — the multi-layer redaction still runs unconditionally (lines 144-145 always execute), so credentials are still attacked by the regex backstops even when layer 1 misses. The concrete harm is a misleading invariant that could lead a maintainer to over-trust layer 1; no credential is guaranteed to leak because the backstops remain in force.

all.title: G-004 in-process unfreeze test is a probabilistic guard and its two assertions use the same set value (redundant coverage)
all.description: test_unfreeze_frozenset_of_unorderable_types_is_deterministic (lines 43-48) asserts at the AMBIENT (unpinned) PYTHONHASHSEED. As a regression guard against a revert to raw set iteration it is probabilistic — raw frozenset iteration of {1,'a',2,'b'} happens to equal the stable order ~30% of seeds, so the buggy code would still pass ~1/3 of runs (the test's own comment at lines 52-53 admits this). Verified secondary defect: line 47 {1,'a',2,'b'} and line 48 {'b',2,'a',1} are the SAME set value with identical per-process iteration order, so line 48 adds zero coverage and gives a false impression of testing two independent orderings. NOTE: the real discriminating guard already exists — test_unfreeze_frozenset_order_is_seed_independent_subprocess (lines 51-69) pins PYTHONHASHSEED=2 in a subprocess and fails the buggy code every run — so this is a test-quality/redundancy issue, not a true coverage gap (downgraded from the reported high).
all.poc: Across 200 PYTHONHASHSEEDs, list(frozenset({1,'a',2,'b'})) equals the stable [1,2,'a','b'] for ~60/200 seeds; on raw-iteration code the in-process assertion passes whenever the ambient seed lands there. And frozenset({1,'a',2,'b'}) == frozenset({'b',2,'a',1}), so both assertions exercise one ordering.
all.suggested_fix: Treat the in-process assertion as documentation only (the subprocess test is the guard), and replace the duplicate {'b',2,'a',1} on line 48 with a genuinely different input (e.g. frozenset({1,'a',2.5,'b'})) so the two assertions cover distinct cases. Optionally parametrize the in-process check over several pinned-and-differing PYTHONHASHSEEDs run in subprocesses.

a.why: Verified both sub-claims in current code at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/tests/test_immutability.py. (1) Lines 47-48: frozenset({1,'a',2,'b'}) == frozenset({'b',2,'a',1}) returns True and list() of each gives the identical iteration order (confirmed empirically: both yield [1,'a',2,'b']). Line 48 therefore adds zero coverage over line 47 — it is the same set value, not a second independent ordering, contradicting the appearance of testing two orderings. (2) The in-process assertion runs at the ambient PYTHONHASHSEED and is probabilistic: across 200 seeds, raw frozenset iteration of {1,'a',2,'b'} equals the stable [1,2,'a','b'] for 60/200 (~30%) of seeds, so a revert to raw set iteration would still pass the in-process assertion ~1/3 of runs. The test's own comment (lines 52-56) admits this. (3) The finding's downgrade is also accurate: the real discriminating guard exists at lines 51-69 (test_unfreeze_frozenset_order_is_seed_independent_subprocess) which pins PYTHONHASHSEED=2 in a subprocess and asserts the stable output, failing buggy code every run. So this is a genuine but low-severity test-quality/redundancy defect, not a coverage gap, exactly as described.

b.why:
Both factual claims reproduce against current code at tests/test_immutability.py:47-48.

Secondary defect (the load-bearing claim): frozenset({1,'a',2,'b'}) (line 47) and frozenset({'b',2,'a',1}) (line 48) are the SAME set value. Verified: a==b is True, and list(a)==list(b) in-process (both yield [1,2,'b','a']). Within one interpreter process the hash seed is fixed, so both assertions exercise an identical iteration order — line 48 contributes zero additional coverage and falsely implies two independent orderings are tested.

Probabilistic claim: Across 200 PYTHONHASHSEEDs, raw list(frozenset({1,'a',2,'b'})) equals the stable [1,2,'a','b'] for 60/200 seeds (~30%), matching the PoC. So the in-process assertions (lines 47-48), as a regression guard against a revert to raw set iteration, would pass ~1/3 of runs even on buggy code.

Correctly downgraded: the genuine deterministic guard exists — test_unfreeze_frozenset_order_is_seed_independent_subprocess (lines 51-69) pins PYTHONHASHSEED=2 in a subprocess; I confirmed raw iteration at seed 2 yields [1,2,'b','a'] (differs from stable), so a revert to raw iteration fails that subprocess test every run. The unfreeze implementation (src/assay_engine/_frozen.py:153-161) is correct, falling back to a stable (type name, repr) sort key. Therefore this is a real test-quality/redundancy issue, not a coverage gap or production-readiness defect.

Severity: low — cosmetic/redundant test coverage; the actual invariant is correctly implemented and is guarded deterministically by the subprocess test.
